query_text:
Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token needs a full
large-model forward pass; how can a small draft model propose
tokens in parallel that the large model verifies without quality
loss?

A.method:
We propose Parallel Draft-and-Verify Decoding (PDVD), a two-model decoding procedure that preserves the exact output (greedy) or distribution (sampling) of a large target model while substantially reducing wall-clock latency. A compact draft model generates multiple-step speculative continuations and multiple alternative branches in one pass; the large model verifies these candidates in a single, fused verification pass that shares the prefix key/value cache. The algorithm commits the longest verified prefix, updates the large-model cache without recomputation for committed tokens, and repeats. Because verification enforces exact agreement (greedy) or uses rejection-corrected sampling (stochastic decoding), output quality is identical to running the large model alone.

The draft model is trained offline to approximate the target model’s short-horizon conditional distributions. Starting from a distilled checkpoint with the same tokenizer, we add k-step prediction heads that jointly predict tokens at t+1..t+k from the same prefix using a lightweight causal sub-block with a triangular intra-head mask. Training minimizes a temperature-calibrated KL divergence to the frozen large-model logits for each horizon, plus an auxiliary top-1 agreement loss at low temperature to maximize greedy match. We further train an uncertainty head to estimate acceptance likelihood per position; at decode time this modulates both the rollout depth k and the number of branches B, allocating more speculation to low-entropy regions.

At runtime, given a prefix and the large model’s KV cache, the draft model proposes B speculative branches of up to k tokens each in a single forward pass. We construct a verification batch by packing sequences [prefix + branch_i] with an attention mask that shares the prefix keys/values across all branches while isolating each branch’s tokens. One fused large-model pass yields logits at every proposed position for all branches. For greedy decoding, we compute, per branch, the longest contiguous prefix for which the proposed token equals the large model’s argmax at that position; we then select the branch with the maximum verified length and commit that span. If no token matches at the first position, we directly emit the large model’s argmax (already computed), incurring no additional pass. Committed tokens’ KV states come from the verification pass, so we continue without recomputation.

For stochastic decoding, we achieve exactness via per-position acceptance correction. Let q be the draft distribution and p the large-model distribution for the same prefix. For each proposed token y, accept it with probability alpha = min(1, p(y)/q(y)). If rejected, sample a token from the residual distribution proportional to [p - alpha q], which we compute from the same large-model logits in the verification pass; proceed to the next step using the realized token. This yields samples exactly from p while still allowing multi-token acceptances. As in the greedy case, we commit the longest contiguous span of accepted tokens, leveraging already-computed KV states.

To maximize throughput and stability, we use: (1) adaptive k and B driven by draft entropy and recent acceptance rates; (2) prefix-sharing KV caches and batched verification to amortize GPU kernel launches; (3) low-precision execution for the draft and logits-only low-precision paths for the verifier; and (4) early-abort of unpromising branches during verification via lightweight top-1 checks. These engineering choices preserve correctness while converting sequential token-by-token launches into a small number of high-utilization fused passes.
A.rationale:
Quality is preserved because verification enforces equivalence to the large model’s decisions. In greedy mode, committing only tokens whose identities match the large model’s argmax ensures the emitted string is exactly what the large model would have produced. In sampling mode, rejection-corrected acceptance uses the draft merely as a proposal distribution and corrects any mismatch to sample from the target p exactly, so the output distribution is unchanged.

Speed comes from two sources: the draft model proposes multiple future tokens and multiple branches in parallel, and the verifier evaluates all proposals in one fused pass with shared prefix caches. This turns many small, latency-bound kernel launches into a small number of large, throughput-optimized operations. Adaptive control of rollout depth and branching concentrates speculation where acceptance is likely, further improving effective tokens-per-second without sacrificing correctness.

novelty.reasoning: The literature search reveals that PDVD combines several well-known mechanisms: (1) multi-draft/multi-branch speculative decoding (SpecHub, MDSD papers), (2) adaptive draft length based on acceptance rates and entropy (SpecDec++, SVIP, EAGLE-2, TALON), (3) KL distillation training for draft models (EAGLE series), (4) k-step prediction heads (PARD, Medusa), and (5) rejection-corrected sampling for exact distribution preservation (standard speculative decoding). EAGLE-2 already uses context-aware dynamic draft trees with confidence scores approximating acceptance rates. TALON uses adaptive tree depth/width based on confidence. SpecDec++ uses a trained acceptance prediction head. The specific combination in PDVD — a draft model with k-step prediction heads trained via temperature-calibrated KL divergence plus a dedicated uncertainty head that jointly controls both rollout depth k AND number of branches B — is not found as an exact end-to-end match. However, the individual components are all well-established and closely related papers (EAGLE-2, SpecDec++, TALON, PARD) combine very similar mechanisms for the same problem. The uncertainty head controlling both k and B simultaneously is a modest extension of SpecDec++'s acceptance prediction head and EAGLE-2's confidence-based dynamic tree. This matches the Score 3 anchor: combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint application to this problem family, where searches surface papers pairing the same mechanisms for closely related problems.

validity.reasoning: (a) The failure mode is sequential large-model forward passes per token, causing high latency. (b) The method proposes a draft model that generates multiple tokens and multiple branches in a single pass, then verifies all candidates in one fused large-model pass with shared KV caches, committing the longest verified prefix. (c) The mechanism directly engages the failure mode: sequential per-token large-model passes are replaced by batched draft proposals verified in a single fused pass, with the causal chain from problem (sequential passes) to solution (parallel speculation + batched verification + KV cache reuse) fully traceable. Quality preservation is addressed through exact greedy matching and rejection-corrected sampling, closing the quality-loss concern in the query. No significant gaps exist in the causal chain.

B.method:
Build a parallel draft-and-verify pipeline around a strong target model T (the verifier) and a much smaller draft model D. Offline, distill D from T so that D approximates T’s local next-token distribution and short multi-token continuations. Use a KL loss on logits plus a short-rollout loss: unroll D for L steps under teacher-forced prefixes and match T’s logits at each step to train D to propose L-length blocks. In parallel, construct a dense next-step memory for T: run T over a large text corpus, record its final pre-LM-head state h_t and the top probable next-token sequences of length 1..L, then apply dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA to 64 dims) and normalization to the stored keys. Train a small linear projection P so D’s hidden state h^D_t can be mapped into this reduced teacher space to query the memory without running T.

At inference, given a context c, run D once to obtain its hidden state h^D_t and parallel proposals: either via a multi-step head that predicts tokens t+1..t+L simultaneously or by sampling short continuations with shared KV cache. Query the dense memory with P h^D_t to retrieve M semantically close next-token sequences. Filter candidates to those whose first token aligns with D’s own top-1 next token (to increase precision) and combine with D’s internally drafted sequences; rank by a weighted score that blends memory similarity and D’s log-prob, then select the top B distinct L-length candidates.

Verify exactly with T using speculative decoding so output quality is identical to running T alone. First, prefill T on c to build its KV cache. Organize the B candidates into a prefix tree. Proceed depth-by-depth: for the current verified prefix, collect the set of unique next tokens suggested by surviving candidates, run T to produce the next token under the chosen decoding policy (greedy or matched sampling), and accept that token if it equals T’s outcome; reject all branches proposing a different token. Continue until a mismatch occurs; on mismatch, emit T’s actual token, discard the remaining draft suffixes, update the cache, and restart the propose–verify cycle. Because accepted runs can span multiple tokens, T performs far fewer forward steps than naive autoregressive decoding while producing the exact same sequence.

Adapt online for throughput. Maintain an exponential moving average of accepted-run length and adjust L and B accordingly: increase L/B when acceptance is high to maximize amortization; decrease when acceptance drops to avoid wasted proposals. If acceptance falls consistently, fall back to shorter drafts or disable the external memory (retrieval-only failures) and rely on D’s proposals. Optionally, continue lightweight on-the-fly distillation by adding a small KL term between D and T on recently seen contexts (if access to T’s logits is allowed) to keep D calibrated to T and sustain high acceptance rates.
B.rationale:
The bottleneck is that T must execute a full forward step per token. By front-loading cheap, parallel proposals from D and a dense memory of T’s typical continuations, then verifying them with exact speculative decoding, we reduce how often T needs to compute the next token. Verification accepts only tokens that match T’s own outcome under the same decoding policy, so the final output is provably identical to running T alone (no quality loss). Dense, semantic retrieval supplies multi-token candidates that better match T than naive n-gram caches, increasing acceptance-run lengths and thus speedup; filtering candidates to align with D’s top-1 reduces low-precision drafts that would be rejected.

Distilling D on short rollouts aligns it closely to T’s local behavior, which raises both the probability of correct first tokens and the stability of longer drafted blocks. Mapping D’s state into T’s reduced latent space lets us exploit a teacher-indexed memory without invoking T, preserving the latency advantages. Prefix-tree, batched verification respects autoregressive causality while amortizing T’s work across multiple candidates at each depth, and the adaptive controller keeps the system near its optimal acceptance/overhead trade-off across diverse prompts.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: dense retrieval of next-token sequences from hidden-state ANN with PCA and normalization plus first-token filtering
exact speculative decoding verification (FSD-style) that preserves the target model’s output while running near greedy speed
restricting action space to top-k plausible tokens to curb unlikely exploration

novelty.reasoning: The proposed method combines: (1) a distilled draft model D trained with KL + multi-step rollout loss, (2) a dense memory of T's continuations queried via a learned linear projection of D's hidden state into T's reduced PCA space (rather than T's own hidden state as in DReSD), (3) prefix-tree batched verification, (4) adaptive L/B control, and (5) optional online distillation. DReSD (2502.15572) covers dense retrieval using the target model's own hidden state with PCA+normalization and candidate filtering. RASD combines a draft model with retrieval via tree fusion/pruning. Neither paper uses the draft model's projected hidden state to query a target-indexed memory — this cross-model projection for retrieval is the key novel mechanism not found in any search result. The multi-step rollout distillation loss also does not appear in the surfaced literature. However, the overall framework assembles known components (speculative decoding, dense retrieval, distillation, adaptive control, prefix-tree verification) with one genuinely new design choice: the learned projection P mapping D's hidden state into T's latent space for retrieval. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application, introducing one named design choice (cross-model hidden-state projection for teacher-memory retrieval) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is sequential large-model decoding requiring one full forward pass per token. The method directly addresses this by (a) training a small draft model D via KL distillation to approximate T's token distributions and propose L-token blocks in parallel, (b) augmenting proposals with a dense memory of T's precomputed continuations queried via a projected hidden state, and (c) verifying candidates with exact speculative decoding using a prefix tree so T's forward passes are amortized across multiple candidate tokens while producing output provably identical to running T alone. The causal chain is fully traceable: cheap parallel proposals from D reduce how often T must run, verification preserves quality, and the adaptive controller maintains efficiency across varying acceptance rates. The only minor gap is that the dense memory construction and projection training add complexity whose interaction with acceptance rates is asserted rather than derived, but this does not break the core mechanism.

C.method:
We propose a speculative decoding pipeline in which a small draft model generates multi-token continuations in parallel and a large model verifies them exactly. At each step t, the draft model produces a block of K future tokens in a single forward pass using a multi-position prediction head. In parallel, we mine a small set of high-probability multi-token continuations from a dense retrieval index built over hidden states of previously seen contexts. We merge the draft and retrieved candidates into a compact candidate set and organize them as a prefix trie. The large model then performs batched, exact verification over this trie: it accepts tokens until the first mismatch with its own greedy trajectory, at which point it emits its preferred token and resumes. Because only verified tokens are committed, the output exactly matches the large model’s sequential decode.

Drafter design for one-pass, multi-token proposals: augment the small model with two-stream, position-aware masking so it can predict the next K positions {t+1, …, t+K} concurrently given the prefix. During distillation, supervise all K heads against the large model’s continuations using KL on each position and a sequence-level objective (e.g., teacher-forced unrolls of length K). This yields a single forward pass that proposes a run of tokens with per-position confidences, avoiding sequential unroll in the draft. The draft can further emit a small beam per head (e.g., top-2) to cover minor uncertainties without extra passes.

Retrieval-augmented proposals to raise acceptance: in parallel with the draft pass, compute a contextual embedding for the current state (e.g., the last hidden state before the LM head) and query a dense kNN datastore to fetch short next-token runs observed under similar contexts. Apply lightweight normalization and dimensionality reduction for fast ANN search, and fuse retrieved runs with the draft’s logits by re-ranking on estimated per-run likelihood. This provides high-precision, longer candidate runs that the verifier is more likely to accept, while remaining cheap to compute.

Batched exact verification and pipelining: place all candidate runs into a prefix trie and verify them with the large model using its KV cache. Run a single batched forward that advances the verifier token-by-token across the frontier until the first mismatch on each sequence; accept the longest common verified prefix across candidates. If no token matches, fall back to the large model’s top-1 for the next step. Pipeline the system so that while the large model verifies batch i, the draft model and retriever prepare batch i+1; and use a sliding window over the horizon to keep GPUs saturated. A Picard-style windowing schedule (partitioning steps within a window across workers and iterating with cached drafts) keeps proposal generation parallel without changing the verifier’s exactness criterion.

Adaptive control closes the loop: monitor acceptance length and adjust K, beam width, and retrieval fan-out online to trade draft cost for verifier savings. Because verification is exact and the draft/retrieval only affect how many tokens are skipped, the final text is provably identical to the large model’s greedy decode while significantly reducing the number of large-model forward passes.
C.rationale:
The key to speeding up decoding without quality loss is to reduce how often the large model must advance one token at a time, while preserving its outputs. Speculative decoding achieves this by letting a cheaper process propose multiple tokens and committing only those that the large model would have produced. Our method raises acceptance length and GPU efficiency on two fronts: (1) a two-stream, position-masked head allows the draft to propose K future tokens in one pass, turning sequential drafting into parallel prediction; and (2) dense retrieval supplies semantically consistent multi-token runs that empirically yield higher verified lengths. Both sources are verified exactly, so the committed text matches the large model’s sequential result.

Batched trie-based verification amortizes large-model compute over many candidates and contexts, while pipelined, windowed scheduling keeps drafting and verifying overlapped on modern accelerators. When acceptance is high, the number of large-model steps per output token drops substantially; when acceptance is low, the system safely falls back to normal decoding with only a small overhead from the cheap draft/retrieval passes.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: dense retrieval of multi-token continuations for speculative decoding
two-stream attention with position-specific masking for parallel multi-position prediction
Picard-style horizon partitioning and iterative updates to parallelize inherently sequential processes

C.paraphrase_essence: Sequential single-step inference incurs O(N) expensive forward passes when N items must be decided in order, even when many decisions could be made in parallel.

novelty.reasoning: The literature search reveals that the individual components of the proposed method are all well-covered: DReSD covers dense retrieval with hidden-state embeddings for speculative decoding; RASD covers retrieval-augmented speculative decoding with tree fusion and prefix matching; DART, P-EAGLE, and PARD cover parallel multi-token prediction in a single forward pass for draft models; and "Double" combines retrieval with parallel speculative decoding. The proposed method's combination of (1) a two-stream position-masked parallel draft head, (2) dense kNN retrieval fused with draft candidates, (3) prefix trie batched verification, and (4) Picard-style pipelining is not found as a single published system. However, RASD already pairs retrieval with trie/tree-based verification and draft model fusion, and DReSD covers the dense retrieval angle, and DART/P-EAGLE cover the parallel multi-token draft head. The proposed method is essentially a combination of these known mechanisms (dense retrieval SD + parallel draft head + trie verification + pipelining), with no single paper combining all four, but with at least one paper (RASD) pairing retrieval+trie verification and another (Double) pairing retrieval+parallel SD. This matches the Score 3-4 boundary; since RASD+Double together cover retrieval+trie+parallel drafting for the same problem, but no single paper unifies all four mechanisms as proposed, this sits at the Score 3 anchor (combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint application to this problem family).

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that sequential LLM decoding requires a full large-model forward pass per token, making it slow. The method directly addresses this by (a) using a small draft model with a multi-position prediction head to propose K tokens in a single forward pass, (b) augmenting with retrieval-based candidates to increase acceptance rates, (c) organizing candidates in a prefix trie for batched exact verification by the large model, and (d) guaranteeing output identity with the large model's greedy decode through exact verification with fallback. The causal chain is fully traceable: fewer large-model forward passes per output token are achieved because multiple tokens are proposed cheaply and verified in batch, with quality preserved by the exactness criterion. There are no significant gaps in the mechanism connecting the failure mode to the proposed solution.

D.method:
We propose a speculative, parallel verification decoder with two models: a small draft model S and the target large model L. At a high level, S rolls out a short lookahead in parallel—forming a small prefix-trie of K candidate branches up to depth B from the current prefix—while L verifies many proposed tokens in a single or few batched passes. Acceptance is performed with an exact, distribution-preserving correction, so the emitted tokens are statistically identical to those produced by L under the chosen decoding policy (e.g., greedy or temperature sampling).

Before deployment, we distill S from L on in-domain text to reduce divergence and calibrate S’s uncertainty. We minimize KL(p_L(·|x) || p_S(·|x)) on next-token prediction and fit a lightweight acceptance-length predictor using features like entropy, top-2 margin, and position to estimate how many draft tokens will likely be accepted by L. This calibration drives an adaptive controller that chooses B (lookahead depth) and K (branching factor) per step, allocating more speculation when S is confident and pulling back in high-entropy regions.

At decode time, from prefix x≤t: (1) Parallel draft: run S autoregressively but cheaply for up to B steps to produce a prefix-trie of K branches (e.g., top-k or diverse nucleus samples), caching S’s token probabilities and uncertainty per node. (2) Batched verification: convert the trie into a packed batch for L using shared-prefix batching (common in paged attention/Flash-decoding): each branch becomes a short sequence whose initial tokens share the same KV cache for x≤t. One or a few forward passes of L then produce logits at all proposed positions across branches, amortizing the cost of many token checks. (3) Exact acceptance with correction: for greedy decoding, accept the longest chain along the best-scoring branch where S’s tokens match L’s argmax at every position; on first mismatch, commit only up to the last match and resume from there with L. For stochastic decoding, use speculative acceptance-rejection to preserve L’s distribution: at each proposed position i with draft token y_i, accept with probability min(1, p_L(y_i|·)/q_S(y_i|·)); on rejection (or if the draft token isn’t drawn), sample from the residual of p_L and discard the remaining draft in that branch. This guarantees the marginal output exactly matches decoding with L alone.

To keep throughput high when L is invoked, we fuse verification across branches and depths: teacher-forcing the proposed tokens yields logits for all verified positions in the same kernels, and we stop early for a branch once a mismatch is found (so we don’t compute unused positions). The adaptive controller sets (B, K) each step from the calibrated acceptance predictor: in low-entropy spans we expand B and sometimes K>1 to harvest longer accepted runs; in ambiguous spans we shrink B (and K) to avoid wasted proposals. Optionally, we prioritize which branches to verify first using a graded score that blends S’s branch probability with its confidence, ensuring scarce verifier compute is spent on high-yield candidates.

The result is a drop-in decoding procedure: it preserves L’s quality exactly for both greedy and sampled decoding, but replaces many single-token large-model steps with fewer, larger, batched verification calls amortized over several accepted tokens. Empirically, this yields substantial wall-clock speedups when S is a good proposal and the controller successfully targets longer, high-confidence drafts.
D.rationale:
Sequential decoding is slow because L must advance one token per expensive forward pass. By letting a much cheaper S explore several steps and branches, then verifying many proposed tokens in one or a few batched passes of L, we amortize L’s compute over multiple accepted tokens. The adaptive controller maximizes effective accept length by increasing speculation where S is calibrated to be trustworthy and shrinking it where it is not, reducing wasted proposals.

Crucially, quality is not compromised: for greedy decoding, we only commit tokens that L itself would have emitted (longest matching prefix). For stochastic decoding, the acceptance-rejection correction ensures that, marginally, the sampled outputs follow exactly the same distribution as if we had decoded with L alone. Thus we obtain near-oracle quality with substantially less wall-clock latency per generated token.

novelty.reasoning: The literature search reveals that tree/trie-based speculative decoding (OPT-Tree, ASDT, EAGLE-2, AdaEAGLE) and adaptive draft length control (AdaEDL, AdaSD, PACER) are well-established. Draft model distillation from the target LLM is also covered (FSPAD, EAGLE). However, the specific combination proposed here — a jointly adaptive controller that tunes BOTH branching factor K AND depth B per step using a trained acceptance-length predictor with entropy+top-2 margin+position features, combined with KL distillation of the draft model — does not appear as an end-to-end published system. Individual mechanisms (tree speculation, adaptive depth, distillation) are all known, and some papers (AdaEAGLE, OPT-Tree) combine adaptive tree structure with draft models, but the specific joint adaptive controller over both K and B driven by a calibrated predictor with those features is not surfaced. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines known mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application in exactly this form, introducing a named design choice (the calibrated acceptance-length predictor controlling both branching factor and depth jointly) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is sequential large-model decoding requiring one full forward pass per token. The method directly addresses this by (a) using a small draft model S to propose multiple tokens in parallel via a prefix-trie, (b) verifying many proposed tokens in a single batched forward pass of L using shared-prefix batching, and (c) applying exact acceptance-rejection correction to guarantee the output distribution matches L exactly, preserving quality. The causal chain is fully traceable: draft parallelism reduces the number of L forward passes, batched verification amortizes L's cost over multiple tokens, and the distribution-preserving correction closes the quality-loss concern. The adaptive controller further tightens the mechanism by dynamically tuning speculation depth to maximize accepted tokens per L call.

query_text:
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?

A.method:
We propose a training-free, query-conditioned token thinning pipeline that builds a compact visual memory for the language model by (1) scoring visual tokens for information content with respect to the current textual prompt, (2) selecting a small anchor set, and (3) merging the remainder into a few surrogate tokens that preserve their aggregate attention effect. The vision encoder remains frozen; we only repack its outputs into fewer keys/values consumed by the VLM’s cross-attention. The pipeline runs once before decoding and exposes a knob for target token budget or an automatic tolerance test that guarantees minimal change in the model’s next-token distribution.

Stage 1: Preview-based importance scoring. After extracting visual tokens from the frozen encoder, we run a lightweight preview pass that computes the text-side queries for the prompt (and BOS) and the visual-side keys/values using the model’s existing cross-attention projections. We aggregate, across the first few cross-attention layers and heads, each visual token’s attention evidence given the prompt: for token i, score s_i is the smoothed sum of softmax(q·k_i) times the value norm, averaged across heads/layers and (optionally) across the prompt positions most predictive for the first decoding step. To stabilize against noisy heads, we compute moving averages across layers and apply head-wise rank aggregation (e.g., Borda count) to obtain a robust ordering. In parallel, we compute redundancy metrics in key space: cosine similarity to nearest neighbors and local spatial coherence (e.g., 2D neighborhood correlation on the ViT grid).

Stage 2: Selection and merge planning. We form an anchor set A by taking the smallest prefix of tokens whose cumulative normalized importance covers a target mass (e.g., 90–95%), with a diversity correction using a fast determinantal point process or greedy farthest-point sampling in key space to avoid picking many near-duplicates. The non-anchor tokens R are then partitioned into M groups that are both spatially local and feature-similar (balanced clustering on the 2D grid with cosine distance in key space). For each group g, we create a surrogate token by weighted pooling: k_g and v_g are importance-weighted sums of member keys and values, and the 2D position embedding is averaged with the same weights. This yields a compressed set K', V' composed of anchors plus surrogates. Because attention is linear in V and softmax-normalized over K, this weighted pooling preserves the aggregate contribution of merged tokens to first order.

Stage 3: Safety check and dynamic control. Before generation, we run a single accept/reject check: compute the next-token distribution for the prompt using the full visual tokens and using the compressed K', V', and measure the change (e.g., KL divergence or drop in top-1 probability). If the change exceeds a tolerance, we automatically relax compression by increasing anchor coverage or the number of surrogates and repeat the check. During decoding, we monitor a cheap proxy—cross-attention entropy on visual memory or sudden spikes in perplexity—and, on trigger, unmerge the top suspect clusters from a cached pool (we keep original K,V in memory to allow reversible unmerging without recomputation). The entire procedure incurs one preview pass and one comparison step, after which decoding proceeds with a much smaller visual token set for all subsequent steps.

Engineering notes: The method is model-agnostic (works with CLIP/ViT patch tokens or region tokens) and introduces no new learned parameters. It shares the same projection layers as the base model, runs in mixed precision, and uses approximate nearest neighbors for redundancy estimation to keep O(N log N) overhead. For very high-resolution inputs, optional early local merges (e.g., 2×2 grid ToMe-style) can be applied before query-conditioned selection to cap the preview cost, and OCR/text-sensitive regions can be protected by a lightweight heuristic (edge density or a tiny text-detector head run once) to avoid over-pruning fine text details.
A.rationale:
Inference latency in VLMs scales with the number of visual tokens primarily through cross-attention KV memory and per-step attention. The proposed method targets this bottleneck by using signals already present at inference—query-key affinities and attention mass conditioned on the current prompt—to estimate token utility without any retraining. By selecting a small, diverse anchor set and merging the rest via weighted pooling in key/value space, it removes redundancy while approximately preserving the aggregate attention effect of pruned tokens.

The preview step and accept/reject test bound the impact on the model’s next-token distribution, letting the system adaptively choose how aggressively to compress for each prompt-image pair. Because the compression happens once and benefits every decoding step, the small upfront cost is amortized over the whole generation. Reversible unmerging provides a safety valve when the model’s uncertainty spikes, maintaining output quality while still delivering substantial latency reductions.

novelty.reasoning: The literature search reveals several closely related methods: FSR (Focus-Scan-Refine) combines visual importance + instruction relevance scoring, anchor selection, and score-weighted merging of remainder tokens into anchors — closely matching stages 1 and 2 of the proposed method. CDPruner uses DPP for diversity-maximizing query-conditioned token selection. ConsensusDrop fuses encoder saliency with cross-attention and merges remainder tokens. DyMU introduces dynamic merging with virtual unmerging. However, no single paper combines all of: (1) a preview pass with value-norm weighted attention scoring + head-wise Borda count rank aggregation, (2) DPP/farthest-point diversity in anchor selection with importance-weighted KV pooling for surrogates, AND (3) a KL-divergence accept/reject safety check with adaptive relaxation + reversible unmerging from a cached pool during decoding. The individual mechanisms (DPP selection, score-weighted merging, query-conditioned scoring) are all published separately or in pairs, but the full pipeline with the safety-check/adaptive-relaxation loop and reversible unmerging from cache is not surfaced as a combined system. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing specific named design choices (KL-divergence safety check with adaptive relaxation, reversible unmerging from cached pool) not present in the surfaced literature as a combined system.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that VLM inference latency scales with visual token count due to redundancy, and the constraint is no retraining of the encoder. The method directly addresses this by (1) scoring visual tokens using existing cross-attention projections (no new parameters, frozen encoder), (2) selecting a compact anchor set and merging redundant tokens via weighted pooling in key/value space, and (3) verifying the compression preserves the next-token distribution. The causal chain is fully traceable: redundant tokens inflate KV memory → importance scoring identifies low-utility tokens → merging reduces KV count → fewer tokens in cross-attention reduces per-step latency. The safety check and reversible unmerging close the gap on quality preservation. There are no significant disconnects between the failure mode and the proposed mechanism.

B.method:
We propose a training-free, query-aware token thinning pipeline that replaces most visual tokens with a compact, information-preserving set before decoding. First, obtain the frozen vision encoder’s patch/region embeddings and map them through the existing projection into the language space (as in your current VLM). Using just the text prompt and the projected visual keys/values, run a lightweight dry pass over the first 1–2 cross-attention layers to collect cross-modal attention. Identify “semantic” heads on-the-fly (those that allocate a majority of their attention to visual tokens for this input), and aggregate their attention onto visual tokens across text positions and layers (e.g., average across selected heads; optional attention rollout). This yields a query-conditional importance score per visual token without extra training or multiple model passes.

Second, reduce redundancy with structure-aware merging instead of naive dropping. Sort tokens by importance; for low-importance tokens, greedily merge each into its nearest higher-importance neighbor in joint feature+position space (e.g., cosine in embedding space with a tie-break by spatial distance). The merged token vector is a similarity- and importance-weighted average; its 2D position is the weighted barycenter so existing positional encodings remain coherent. Continue until a target budget B is reached (typically 20–40% of the original tokens). To avoid losing long-tail cues, compute a tiny set (r=2–6) of summary tokens from the pruned set via SVD/PCA on the discarded-token matrix and append these summaries as visual-prefix tokens before the text. This preserves most variance of the dropped evidence in a handful of vectors while keeping the cross-attention interface unchanged. If your VLM is sensitive to changing the visual sequence length, optionally keep the original length but zero-mask removed tokens’ key/values (or pad with zeros) so positions are preserved while computation attends only to the kept/merged tokens.

Third, add a progressive, fail-safe controller that can cheaply validate the budget during generation and restore tokens if needed. Start decoding with the sparse set (B + r). At select steps (e.g., after the first 2–3 generated tokens and when entropy spikes), compute a distributional dependency check: compare the current next-token distribution with image tokens enabled vs. with vision cross-attention masked (one extra forward pass with the same caches) and measure divergence (e.g., Hellinger/JS). If the dependency is high and uncertainty remains high, expand the visual context by re-introducing the next-best tranche of tokens (by importance) and continue; if dependency is low and hidden states stabilize (cosine similarity across successive layers exceeds a threshold), keep the current budget. As an even stricter guard on critical steps, you can contrast logits under the current sparse set vs. a denser set once; if the contrast is large on content words, admit more tokens for a few steps, then revert to the sparse budget.

Finally, cache and reuse the selected tokens across the whole reply. All steps are inference-only: no weight updates or retraining of the vision encoder or projector. The extra overhead consists of a shallow dry pass for attention collection, a single SVD over discarded tokens (small matrices), and occasional divergence checks conditioned on uncertainty; in return, cross-attention over visual tokens—typically the dominant per-step cost—operates on a fraction of the original tokens. In practice, retaining 20–40% tokens plus 2–6 summaries yields sizable speedups on long generations with minimal quality loss.
B.rationale:
Most visual tokens are redundant given the question; the most reliable training-free signal of their utility is how text queries attend to them. Pooling attention from heads that already emphasize semantic evidence provides a robust, per-input importance score without finetuning. Merging low-importance tokens into nearby, higher-importance neighbors preserves local evidence while directly shrinking the token set; adding a few summary (prefix) tokens conserves global variance of pruned regions at almost no per-step cost. Keeping positional structure via masking/padding mitigates distribution shift in models sensitive to sequence length changes.

Because some questions momentarily need finer details, the progressive controller guards against over-thinning: a simple divergence between image-conditioned and language-only predictions, together with stabilization checks on hidden states, decides when to keep, prune further, or temporarily re-introduce tokens. This staged sparse-to-dense fallback preserves accuracy while still letting most steps run with a small, query-relevant visual context—achieving lower latency without retraining the encoder.

C.method:
We propose an inference-time token thinning pipeline that keeps the vision encoder frozen and reduces cross-modal compute by (1) scoring visual tokens for question-conditioned importance with a single light probe, (2) structure-preserving compression of the visual keys/values into a much smaller set of landmark tokens via low-rank approximation, and (3) adaptive shrinking of the visual token set as decoding proceeds. The model’s architecture and weights remain unchanged; only the set of visual key/value vectors supplied to cross-attention (or the visual prefix in prefix-based VLMs) is replaced by a compressed set that is recomputed per image–prompt.

First, we compute a cheap, question-conditioned importance score for each visual token. Using the frozen vision encoder already present in the VLM (e.g., CLIP ViT), we obtain patch tokens and a text embedding of the prompt. We derive per-token semantic relevance from cosine similarity between each patch token and the text embedding, optionally aggregated over object proposals produced by a class-agnostic segmenter to group spatially coherent tokens. We then run a one-step calibration pass that predicts just the next token with and without enabling vision-to-text attention; the difference in the output distribution gives a prompt dependency signal. We apportion that difference to individual visual tokens using a first-order influence proxy: the gradient of the next-token log-prob w.r.t. each token’s key/value contribution, weighted by the token’s attention mass in the probe. This yields a stable importance score that fuses semantics and model-internal dependency.

Second, we replace many low-importance tokens by a small set of landmark tokens that provably preserve most of the cross-attention effect. For each decoder layer and head, form the key matrix K and value matrix V over visual tokens once per prompt. Select r landmarks by leverage-score sampling or pivoted QR on K; these identify the most informative columns. For discarded tokens with keys K_rest and values V_rest, compute coefficients C = K_rest K_keep^+ (pseudoinverse), and fold their contribution into the kept values via V_keep ← V_keep + C^T V_rest. Thereafter, the decoder attends only to the r kept keys with the merged values at every time step, reducing cross-attention from O(N) tokens to O(r) without altering the model. When visual tokens are provided as a prefix to a causal LM, we analogously Nyström-compress the prefix self-attention cache for those tokens, or, where self-attention compression is unavailable, cluster low-importance tokens and average their embeddings and positional encodings to emit r synthetic prefix tokens.

Third, we adapt the token budget during generation. Because reliance on the image typically fades as more text is generated, we compute a cheap prompt-dependency score every k steps by masking visual attention in the current layer and measuring the change in the next-token logits. If this score falls below a threshold, we shrink r by reusing the already computed leverage ordering (or by merging the weakest landmarks). A safety check guards quality: before committing to a smaller set, compare the next-token distribution under the current and proposed compressed caches; if the KL divergence exceeds a small budget, revert or raise r. Optionally, a tiny auxiliary gate trained offline on held-out data maps the cheap features (token norm, segment-text similarity, early attention mass) to an initial r to avoid the gradient probe altogether on latency-critical paths.

This pipeline is drop-in: it requires no retraining of the vision encoder or the VLM, runs a single light probe per prompt, and then reuses the compressed visual K/V across all decoding steps. In practice it cuts the visual cross-attention cost from proportional to the raw token count to proportional to a small, adaptive budget while maintaining answer quality, and it also lowers memory bandwidth by caching only r key/value pairs per head and layer.
C.rationale:
Most images contain large regions that are either background or redundant at the task granularity. In transformer cross-attention, the set of visual keys is typically highly correlated and low-rank relative to the text queries, so a small set of landmark columns plus a linear combination can approximate the attention output with little error. By selecting landmarks using leverage scores of the actual keys and folding the discarded tokens’ effect into the kept values, we preserve the attention result without changing any model parameters.

Determining which tokens to keep must be conditioned on the prompt and model behavior. A quick probe that contrasts next-token predictions with and without vision yields a dependency signal; attributing this difference to individual tokens with gradient-weighted attention produces reliable importances at negligible cost compared to full decoding. Grouping tokens by object-like regions and semantic similarity further concentrates budget on visually and linguistically relevant areas. Finally, dynamically shrinking the token set as dependency wanes ensures we do not overpay for visual context late in generation while a small KL-based safety check prevents harmful pruning—all without retraining the encoder.

D.method:
We propose an inference-time, language-guided token condensation pipeline that scores, selects, and merges visual tokens without retraining the vision encoder. Given an image and a text prompt, we first run a short "scout" forward pass that exposes how the language model would attend to visual tokens, then use these scores—augmented with object priors—to pick a small, coverage-aware subset of tokens to keep. The remaining low-information tokens are not simply dropped; they are merged into a small number of spatially and semantically coherent clusters whose keys/values are pooled with attention-preserving corrections. The condensed token set is then reused for the next several decoding steps and refreshed only when needed.

Stage A: language-guided saliency. We compute visual patch embeddings and their projected cross-attention keys/values once. We then run a cheap scout pass consisting of the instruction and one or two initial decoding steps to gather cross-attention maps from the early cross-attention layers/heads of the language model. Per visual token importance is computed by head- and layer-averaged attention mass, optionally tempered by attention entropy to downweight diffuse heads. We fuse in an object prior by running a lightweight open-vocabulary detector on the image using nouns parsed from the prompt; tokens inside detected boxes get a whitelist boost so object regions are preferentially retained. The final saliency score is a convex combination of cross-attention saliency and the object prior.

Stage B: budgeted selection and attention-preserving merging. With a target budget T_budget (e.g., 64–128), we select the top tokens by saliency using stratified sampling over a spatial grid to avoid dropping entire regions. Remaining tokens are clustered by spatial adjacency and key-similarity into C clusters. For each cluster, we compute merged representations: K_bar as the mean key, V_bar as a similarity-weighted mean value, and store a log-count bias b = log(|cluster|). At cross-attention time, we replace the cluster’s members by a single supertoken whose attention logit is q·K_bar/√d + b, approximating the aggregate softmax mass of its constituents; V_bar is used for the value readout. This keeps attention approximately conservative while reducing the number of visual tokens that the language model attends to. We cache the condensed keys/values so subsequent decoding steps incur no extra vision cost.

Stage C: asynchronous reuse and two-stage refresh. We reuse the condensed token set across N generated tokens (e.g., 8–16) and monitor low-cost triggers to refresh: rising cross-attention entropy, shifts in the text (e.g., newly mentioned objects), or a spike in next-token loss. When triggered, we run a fast re-ranking: a cheap proxy (embedding norm or prior saliency) narrows candidates, then a one-step ablation test rescoring the top candidates (temporarily masking a cluster and measuring the delta in next-token loss) finalizes which tokens to keep or expand. If guardrails detect degradation (e.g., perplexity spike or user "zoom" cues), we temporarily increase T_budget or roll back the last condensation.

Implementation notes: All steps operate with a frozen encoder. Visual keys/values are computed once and pooled on-GPU with simple reductions. The open-vocabulary detector can be cached per image and skipped when prompts are generic. The condensation state (selected tokens, merged clusters, biases) is a small structure passed to the language model’s cross-attention, requiring only minor changes to accept per-token logit biases and reduced K/V lists.
D.rationale:
Cross-attention cost in VLMs scales linearly with the number of visual tokens; most patches are background or redundant. By measuring what the language model actually attends to for the current prompt, we can identify low-information tokens specific to the query without touching the vision encoder. Adding an object prior prevents accidental removal of semantically crucial regions even when early cross-attention is diffuse. Merging, rather than dropping, preserves aggregate attention mass and value content from many low-importance tokens in a few supertokens, retaining global context at far lower cost.

A short scout pass amortizes its overhead by enabling large downstream savings, and asynchronous reuse keeps latency low during generation. The two-stage refresh (cheap prefilter, targeted rescoring) lets us adapt when the textual focus shifts, while simple guardrails maintain output quality. The entire procedure is inference-only, encoder-agnostic, and compatible with existing VLMs by modifying only the cross-attention inputs.

query_text:
Inputs to generative models vary in complexity but standard
decoding allocates fixed compute per token; how can a model
adaptively allocate more or fewer computation steps without
degrading quality?

A.method:
We propose Confidence-Guided Adaptive Decoding (CGAD), a per-token dynamic-compute scheme for Transformer generators that preserves baseline quality while reducing average inference cost. CGAD adds a lightweight halting head and a routing controller that, at each decoding step, estimate uncertainty and decide whether to (a) accept the current prediction using a shallow pass, (b) allocate additional layers to deepen the computation for that token, or (c) trigger a brief local refinement pass that re-evaluates a small set of candidate tokens with deeper computation. The default path is the shallow pass, so easy tokens complete quickly; only uncertain tokens consume extra compute. The design keeps the core model and decoding algorithm unchanged (greedy or beam), adding only adaptive depth and optional micro-reranking.

Architecturally, the base Transformer is augmented with early-exit checkpoints after blocks of layers (e.g., every 4 layers). Each checkpoint exposes: (1) provisional next-token logits; (2) a halting head that consumes internal features (hidden state norms, layerwise variance, token-type features, top-1 probability, top-1–top-2 margin, entropy, and agreement across a tiny cheap stochastic ensemble such as two dropout passes) to predict the remaining compute needed; and (3) routing masks that enable selective continuation to deeper blocks only for tokens flagged as uncertain. To maintain throughput, tokens sharing the same required depth are batched together within a step, and KV caches are shared so deeper passes only extend attention without recomputation. An optional refinement module performs micro-deliberation: it samples or selects k candidates from the shallow logits, scores them using one deeper checkpoint, and commits the best—this is invoked only when uncertainty remains high after deepening, and it reuses the same caches to avoid latency spikes.

Training proceeds in three stages. Stage A: standard pretraining or supervised finetuning of the base model to establish a strong full-depth baseline. Stage B: compute teacher trajectories by running the full-depth model (optionally with a strong decoding setting) to collect, for each step, gold tokens, full-depth logits, and difficulty labels derived from shallow-vs-deep discrepancies and uncertainty targets (e.g., whether the shallow early-exit token would have been wrong, or whether entropy exceeds a percentile). Train the halting head to predict these labels and distill full-depth logits into earlier exits so that early stops reproduce full-depth behavior when safe. Stage C: joint finetuning with a compute-regularized objective: L = cross-entropy to the gold token (or distilled teacher) + lambda * expected compute per token, with a differentiable halting relaxation (e.g., soft halting probabilities across exits) and calibration losses for uncertainty (e.g., expected calibration error minimization via temperature scaling). A constraint-based scheduler adjusts lambda so that validation quality never drops below the full-depth baseline; when a drop is detected, the threshold is relaxed to allocate more compute.

Inference uses a budget-aware controller. For each new token: (1) run the shallow block; (2) compute uncertainty features and a halting score; (3) if the stop condition is met, emit the shallow prediction; otherwise (4) continue to the next checkpoint, re-evaluate uncertainty, and either stop or proceed. If after the final checkpoint uncertainty remains high, trigger micro-deliberation: evaluate k candidates with the deepest checkpoint and pick the top-scoring candidate. Safety rules override the controller for known hard cases (numbers, rare entities, code punctuation), always allocating deeper passes. The system exposes a single knob—target quality relative to baseline—which the controller uses to set halting thresholds adaptively per prompt. This yields an anytime decoder: when tokens are easy, they exit early; when they are hard, they receive more layers or a brief refinement step, matching full-compute quality while reducing average latency and FLOPs.
A.rationale:
Difficulty varies across tokens; uncertainty and shallow–deep disagreement are reliable signals of when extra computation changes the answer. By learning a calibrated halting policy and distilling full-depth behavior into early exits, the model can safely stop early on easy tokens without sacrificing accuracy, while reserving deeper layers and refinement for the few tokens where it matters. Routing masks and shared KV caches keep the selective deepening efficient and batching-friendly, so throughput remains high.

The compute-regularized training with a quality constraint prevents regressions: the controller only reduces compute where the distilled early exit already matches the full-depth output, and it escalates to deeper passes or micro-deliberation otherwise. This creates a controllable compute–quality curve and a practical default that matches baseline quality at lower average cost, fulfilling the goal of adaptive per-token computation without degradation.

CGAD combines (1) early-exit with uncertainty-based halting head, (2) a stochastic dropout mini-ensemble for uncertainty, (3) a micro-deliberation/candidate reranking module at deeper layers, (4) compute-regularized training with a quality-constraint scheduler, and (5) safety overrides for known hard token types. The micro-deliberation component (sampling k candidates and scoring with a deeper checkpoint) is not found in any surfaced paper. However, the overall architecture is a combination of known mechanisms (early exit, distillation into exits, uncertainty estimation, compute regularization) with one distinguishing addition (micro-deliberation reranking). This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application, introducing one named design choice (micro-deliberation with candidate reranking at deeper checkpoints) not present in the surfaced literature, while the individual components are all known.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that standard decoding allocates fixed compute per token regardless of token complexity, wasting resources on easy tokens and potentially under-computing on hard ones. The method directly addresses this by introducing per-token early-exit checkpoints with a learned halting head that estimates uncertainty and routes tokens to shallow or deep computation paths. The causal chain is traceable: uncertainty signals (entropy, margin, shallow-deep disagreement) trigger routing decisions, distillation ensures early exits match full-depth quality, and the compute-regularized training with quality constraints prevents degradation. The mechanism engages every aspect of the failure mode — adaptive allocation, quality preservation, and efficiency — with specific architectural and training components for each.

B.method:
We propose Adaptive Compute Decoding (ACD), a controller-driven decoding scheme that adjusts the amount of computation per token based on on-the-fly difficulty signals while preserving output quality. At each step, ACD estimates ease vs. hardness using calibrated indicators: next-token entropy and top-2 logit margin, agreement with a lightweight draft generator, and the recent speculative acceptance rate. When tokens look easy, ACD runs a dense-retrieval speculative draft-and-verify pass to accept multi-token runs in a single verification, amortizing compute across several tokens; the moving acceptance window also serves as a real-time signal of difficulty for escalation or de-escalation.

When difficulty rises, ACD switches decoding policy instead of immediately paying large compute. For closed-ended segments (e.g., code completion, math final-answer tokens), it elevates to a low-variance deterministic policy (e.g., greedy/contrastive/beam-2) with a small candidate count; for open-ended segments it uses low-temperature sampling with modest top-p, increasing candidate expansions only if the controller’s risk estimate (entropy+disagreement) exceeds a threshold. The controller tunes these knobs to meet a target confidence (e.g., expected log-prob margin) with the fewest extra evaluations, and it backs off to the cheaper speculative path as soon as the indicators recover.

For genuinely hard positions or when global constraints/verifiers matter, ACD activates a twist-guided particle stage. It runs a small number of Sequential Monte Carlo particles with learned twist functions that anticipate downstream rewards from verifiers (format checks, constraint classifiers, or lightweight reward models), focusing compute on promising prefixes. The number of particles and resampling frequency are adapted online to an effective sample size target; if ESS collapses, ACD temporarily increases particles or branch factor only around the shared prefix, and uses a step-level reward model to decide whether to extend chain-of-thought or to commit, ensuring that extra computation buys measurable verification gain.

To keep wall-clock bounded even when compute escalates, ACD uses predictor-guided inner-layer sparsity during the expensive stages: a small predictor gates FFN activations so only likely-active neurons are evaluated, reducing per-forward cost without changing the model’s outputs. ACD enforces budget caps (per-token and per-sequence) and a quality-first fallback: if the target confidence is unmet at the cap, it returns the best verified hypothesis from the particle or deterministic pool rather than overspending. Twist functions and step-level reward models are trained offline on task traces; the rest is plug-and-play at inference, requiring no model retraining.
B.rationale:
ACD adapts computation to input and position difficulty: easy spans are handled by long speculative accept runs, collapsing many tokens into one verification; ambiguous or risky spans receive more careful search, but only to the extent needed to hit a confidence target. This avoids degrading quality by (i) preserving the base model’s distribution via verify-and-accept, (ii) using deterministic decoding where it empirically reduces hallucinations, and (iii) invoking a principled particle sampler that concentrates effort on high-reward prefixes under sequence-level constraints.

The controller’s signals and staged escalation prevent overcomputing on simple tokens and undercomputing on hard ones. Predictor-guided inner-layer sparsity recovers much of the added cost during hard-mode search, keeping end-to-end latency near greedy decoding on easy inputs while reserving extra compute where it most affects correctness.

C.method:
We propose ADaPT-Gen, a multi-exit autoregressive generator with a token-wise halting policy that allocates depth (and optional micro-steps) adaptively at each decoding step. The base model exposes lightweight exit heads at several decoder depths. During generation, the model advances one block at a time and, after each block, evaluates a continue/stop gate built from: (a) representation saturation, measured as cosine similarity between consecutive layer states; (b) predictive uncertainty, e.g., entropy of the current next-token distribution; (c) stability of predictions, e.g., KL between successive exit logits; and (d) a learned value head that estimates the expected loss reduction if one more block is executed. If the combined signal falls below a dynamic threshold, the token halts early; otherwise, it allocates more compute up to a maximum depth. A mild per-time-step threshold decay biases later positions to exit slightly earlier to preserve throughput in long sequences.

To meet a target compute budget without degrading quality, ADaPT-Gen uses a primal–dual controller that tunes the halting threshold online: it minimizes task loss plus a compute penalty λ·E[depth], adjusting λ to hit a desired average layers-per-token. This turns compute into a controllable knob while letting the policy remain state-dependent. For very uncertain tokens, the gate can optionally trigger a few inexpensive refinement micro-steps inside the last block (parameter-shared recurrent sub-steps) rather than invoking additional full blocks—concentrating effort where marginal value is highest.

Training pairs the standard next-token objective at full depth with multi-exit distillation: every exit head is trained via KL to match the full-depth logits on the same examples, so that early exits produce high-quality distributions. To learn the value head, we log the realized improvement in token-level NLL from executing one more block (or to final depth) and regress to this improvement with a smooth L1 loss. We also apply depth dropout (randomly truncating depth during training) to expose exits to diverse contexts, and calibrate the uncertainty signals so that entropy and logit-KL are predictive of true error reductions. The saturation metric is used as-is and not directly optimized, preventing degenerate shortcuts while providing a stable, scale-free early-exit cue.

At deployment, ADaPT-Gen operates per token and can be applied to encoder–decoder models as well: the encoder uses the same saturation gate to stop early on easy inputs, while the decoder allocates depth adaptively per generated token. For batched serving, tokens that have halted skip subsequent blocks, while the remaining tokens continue; lightweight masking preserves high GPU utilization. A conservative fail-safe routes any token whose gate remains above threshold at max depth to a single re-evaluation pass (e.g., one micro-step sweep), ensuring that rare hard cases receive extra attention without impacting average latency.
C.rationale:
Token difficulty varies dramatically across prompts and positions, so fixed-depth decoding wastes layers on easy steps and starves hard ones. ADaPT-Gen makes compute state-dependent by combining a representation-saturation signal with uncertainty- and prediction-change metrics, then deciding whether an additional block is worth its marginal value. A learned value head explicitly estimates expected loss reduction from more compute; the halting rule continues only when that margin is positive enough, and the primal–dual controller keeps average depth on budget.

Quality is preserved by multi-exit distillation from the full-depth model so that early exits produce nearly the same logits as the final head. Using cosine saturation as the primary exit cue avoids brittle confidence heuristics, while threshold decay across time steps and optional micro-steps provide fine-grained control. Together, these choices deliver substantial speedups on easy tokens and targeted extra reasoning on hard ones, without a systematic loss in generation quality.

C.paraphrase_essence: Uniform per-step compute budgets ignore variation in instance and step difficulty, wasting computation on easy parts and starving hard parts, which forces a latency–quality trade-off under fixed resources.

The PPO-based token-adaptive early exit for MT is also close. However, no single paper combines all of: (1) a learned value head estimating expected NLL reduction from one more block, (2) a primal-dual online controller tuning the halting threshold to hit a compute budget, (3) optional parameter-shared recurrent micro-steps for very uncertain tokens, and (4) per-time-step threshold decay — all together in one system. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing named design choices (value head for marginal compute estimation, primal-dual threshold controller) not present as a combined system in the surfaced literature.

The method's mechanism directly addresses this by implementing a token-wise halting policy with multiple exit points at different decoder depths, where a combination of representation saturation, predictive uncertainty, prediction stability, and a learned value head determines whether to continue or halt computation for each token. D.method:
We propose Adaptive Ponder-Then-Halt Decoding (APHD), a token-wise controller that decides, at inference time, how many refinement steps to spend on each next-token prediction. The decoder performs an initial standard pass to produce logits and a lightweight set of signals: (i) classical confidence features (entropy, top-k margin); (ii) stability probes obtained by injecting small, structured perturbations into the hidden state to measure output variance at near-zero extra cost; and (iii) a twist score that estimates the long-horizon utility of preferring some local directions (e.g., whether additional thinking is likely to change the token in a way that improves downstream reward or future log-probability). From these signals, APHD predicts the marginal utility of one more refinement step at the current position.

A halting policy governs per-token compute: continue pondering while the estimated marginal utility exceeds a global price λ and a small step cap S_max is not reached; otherwise halt and emit the token. The global price λ is adapted online (dual control) to match a user-specified average compute budget, so the system automatically shifts effort toward “hard” tokens and away from “easy” ones as content varies. This creates a smooth trade-off between throughput and quality without manual thresholds. By default, with one step the method reduces exactly to standard greedy/sampling decoding.

Refinement uses a parameter-shared iterative block (a lightweight attention-MLP module) applied recurrently to the current token’s hidden state. Each step proposes a residual update to the logits under a KL trust-region constraint and maintains a safe fallback: if a step fails to increase a local proxy objective (e.g., calibrated log-likelihood or an external verifier score), the update is rejected and the previous logits are kept. For additional robustness on difficult tokens, APHD can maintain a small set of K internal particles (candidate hidden states) perturbed in diverse, low-cost directions; after each step, particles are reweighted by their twist scores and resampled to focus further refinement on the most promising directions before aggregating them into the final logits. Easy tokens exit after the first pass; hard tokens receive extra steps and, if helpful, short targeted exploration.

Training is multi-objective: standard next-token cross-entropy for language quality, a halting-head calibration loss to predict when extra compute helps, and a self-supervised twist predictor trained from teacher signals that measure the realized gain from additional steps (e.g., improvement in log-probability or an auxiliary verifier/reward). A small ponder cost encourages efficiency but is annealed to avoid under-thinking early in training. Knowledge distillation from a high-ponder teacher to a low-ponder student maintains base quality, and the KL trust region at inference guarantees safety: additional computation can improve predictions but cannot degrade them relative to the one-step baseline.
D.rationale:
APHD makes compute proportional to local difficulty: tokens with sharp, stable distributions and high agreement across perturbation probes halt immediately, while uncertain or unstable tokens trigger additional refinement. The twist score anticipates long-horizon effects, so extra steps are spent where they are most likely to improve downstream quality rather than merely sharpening short-horizon likelihoods. A global price λ implements budget-aware allocation, ensuring the model stays within a target average cost while automatically prioritizing the highest-utility opportunities.

Quality is preserved by design. First, refinement is residual and constrained by a KL trust region, preventing overcorrections. Second, a safe fallback rejects any refinement step that fails to improve a calibrated proxy objective, so the one-step baseline is a lower bound on quality. Third, particle reweighting steers limited exploration toward directions that predict better sequence-level outcomes, reducing variance and avoiding arbitrary deviations. Together, these mechanisms adaptively scale decoding compute without sacrificing the reliability of standard decoding.

Operationally, APHD slots into existing decoders: with S_max=1 it is identical to current decoding; increasing S_max activates pondering only where warranted, and microbatch masking lets easy positions exit early without stalling harder ones. This yields throughput close to fixed-compute decoding on easy inputs and higher quality on complex inputs for the same average budget.
