validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is the prohibitive memory and compute cost of updating billions of parameters on consumer hardware, requiring a small-parameter adaptation that matches full fine-tuning. The method addresses this through two mechanisms: (a) zeroth-order/forward-only optimization that avoids backpropagation entirely, keeping memory at inference levels, and (b) low-rank adapters (LoRA-style) that restrict trainable parameters to under 1% of the model. The causal chain is traceable: freezing the backbone eliminates gradient storage for base parameters, zeroth-order updates eliminate the backward graph for adapter parameters, and low-rank parameterization limits the parameter count. Minor gaps exist in whether the two-stage approach (soft prompt then LoRA) is strictly necessary versus just LoRA alone, and whether zeroth-order convergence reliably closes the gap to full fine-tuning performance — but these are effectiveness concerns, not validity concerns. The mechanism directly engages both named failure modes (memory/compute and parameter budget).

C.method:
HyperSpectral-ZO Adapters is a two-part adaptation pipeline that keeps the pretrained LLM frozen and learns a very small set of task-specific parameters generated by a shared hypernetwork. A single lightweight hypernetwork H takes a concatenation of three learnable embeddings (task identity, layer index, and block type) and emits all the per-layer tuning weights: (a) compact attention key/value prefixes and (b) low-rank residual adapters for the projection and MLP matrices. A tiny residual on the LM head is also learned and added to the frozen output layer. Only H, the three hyper-embeddings, layer norms, and the LM-head residual are trained; the base model remains untouched. For each task, the only additional state is its task embedding (plus the small LM-head residual), so storage scales with tasks rather than layers.

To capture both sequence-level steering and weight-level adaptation without bloating parameters, we instantiate two generated modules per block. First, HyperPrefix: H directly produces per-layer K/V prefix vectors of length p without an additional per-block MLP, injecting task signals into attention. Second, HyperLoRA: H produces low-rank increments ΔW = A B for each target linear map, inserted as residuals in parallel to the frozen weights with a learned scalar α per site. In the MLP, adapters are placed in parallel to the FFN path rather than in-series to preserve the base computation while enabling corrective capacity. A simple gating schedule warms α from 0 to its learned value over the first few thousand steps to avoid destabilizing early training.

We spectrally align and rescale the low-rank updates to preserve the pretrained model’s inductive biases. Before training, for each frozen weight W we compute or approximate its top singular values/vectors (once, offline). We constrain A and B to operate in these local spectral subspaces and regularize the update so that the singular value distribution of W + ΔW stays close to W (spectral drift penalty and orthogonality-preserving re-projection every few steps). A global rank budget R is allocated across layers by a short probing phase that estimates per-layer sensitivity (loss decrease under tiny random low-rank perturbations); layers with higher sensitivity receive higher rank r_i, keeping total parameters fixed while placing capacity where it matters.

Optimization is backprop-free and memory-light: we train H, the embeddings, layer norms, and the LM-head residual with a two-point zeroth-order (ZO) estimator that uses only forward passes. At each step we sample a shared perturbation u, evaluate the loss at θ ± σu, and form a finite-difference gradient estimate; perturbation seeds are reused in-place so memory remains comparable to inference. Because the trainable state is tiny and gradients are estimated without per-sample backprop, the method runs comfortably on a single consumer GPU. Optional scalar noise can be added to the ZO estimate if differential privacy is required, without changing memory costs.

At inference, we discard all optimizer state and retain: the frozen LLM, the shared hypernetwork H, and for each task its small task embedding and LM-head residual. H materializes all needed prefixes and low-rank residuals on-the-fly given the layer/block embeddings, enabling multi-task deployment with negligible per-task storage. The combination of prefix signals, spectrally aligned low-rank residuals, and a tiny LM-head residual consistently closes the gap to full fine-tuning within a fixed, very small parameter budget.
C.rationale:
The approach minimizes trainable parameters by amortizing all per-layer tuning weights through a single shared hypernetwork and by learning only a task embedding and tiny LM-head residual per task. Prefixes excel at steering attention while low-rank residuals adjust token mixing and MLP transformations; learning both via a single generator recovers much of full fine-tuning’s flexibility without duplicating parameters across layers. Adding a small residual on the output head further captures target-domain vocabulary/style shifts at negligible cost.

Training with a forward-pass-only zeroth-order estimator eliminates backpropagation’s activation memory, making adaptation feasible on consumer GPUs even with large base models. Spectral alignment and rescaling keep the update well-conditioned and prevent the low-rank increments from distorting the pretrained spectrum, which empirical and analytic studies suggest is key to matching full fine-tuning performance. The rank-allocation probe concentrates capacity on the most sensitive layers, improving accuracy under a fixed parameter budget.

C.paraphrase_essence: Adapting a massive pretrained model by updating all its weights is compute-, memory-, and storage-prohibitive on limited hardware, yet downstream performance close to full fine-tuning is still required.

HypeLoRA (2026) uses a hypernetwork to generate LoRA factors but without spectral alignment or ZO. No surfaced paper combines hypernetwork-generated PEFT (both prefix + LoRA) with spectral alignment/regularization AND zeroth-order optimization together. The hypernetwork+prefix+LoRA combination is very close to HyperPELT (the inspired seed), but adding spectral alignment constraints and ZO optimization together with this hypernetwork framework has no direct published match. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing the spectral alignment + ZO + hypernetwork combination that searches return only as individual mechanisms.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is the prohibitive cost of updating billions of parameters on consumer hardware, requiring a small-parameter adaptation approach that matches full fine-tuning. The method addresses this through multiple mechanisms: (a) a shared hypernetwork that generates all per-layer tuning weights from a tiny set of learnable embeddings, keeping the base model frozen; (b) zeroth-order optimization that eliminates backpropagation's activation memory overhead, directly targeting the consumer hardware constraint; and (c) spectral alignment and rank allocation to maintain adaptation quality within a fixed small parameter budget. The causal chain from problem to solution is traceable: frozen base model + hypernetwork amortization reduces parameter count, ZO training reduces memory to inference-level, and spectral regularization + rank allocation aim to close the performance gap to full fine-tuning. Minor gaps exist in whether the ZO estimator's gradient quality is sufficient to match full fine-tuning performance at scale, and whether the hypernetwork itself introduces meaningful overhead, but these are effectiveness concerns rather than validity gaps.

D.method:
We propose a three-part, parameter-efficient adapter stack that can be trained on consumer hardware while closely matching full fine-tuning: (1) a shared low-rank adapter basis (SLRA) across the model, (2) a tiny library of global attention-bias patterns mixed into attention, and (3) a few learned task-state tokens with sparse, top-K routing to focus compute where it matters. The base LLM is frozen and loaded in 4-bit quantization; only the small adapter stack is trained in 8–16 bit, keeping memory and optimizer state small.

SLRA: Replace standard per-layer LoRA with a factorized variant that shares a global low-rank basis across all layers and projections we choose to adapt (e.g., Q, K, V, and the up/down FFN). Concretely, for each adapted weight W we learn W + A·S_ℓ·B, where A and B are rank-r matrices shared across layers (one pair per projection type), and S_ℓ is a tiny per-layer scaling (e.g., diagonal or low-rank gate). This yields capacity similar to LoRA while cutting parameters from O(L·r·(d_in + d_out)) to O(r·(d_in + d_out)) + O(L·r). A residual mixing knob ε at each site retains a small fraction of the frozen path (W) to stabilize learning on small devices. 

Global attention-bias library: Insert a small set C (e.g., 6–12) of global, reusable attention-bias patterns that are shared across all layers and heads. Each pattern parameterizes a low-rank, token-to-token bias (e.g., via two learned vectors whose outer-product forms a bias map, optionally conditioned on positions or separators) that is added to the attention logits before softmax. Per layer, a learned mixer (few scalars) blends these biases with the native attention. This module adds only O(C·d) parameters total and helps the frozen attention discover reusable long-/short-range structures without touching the backbone weights.

Task-state tokens with sparse routing: Prepend a small number m (e.g., 4–8) of learned task-state tokens to the input (akin to deep prompts). At selected layers, we compute attention from a state token to all sequence tokens and select top-K salient tokens. Only those K tokens are passed through the SLRA-enhanced path and receive attention-bias mixing for that layer; the rest use the frozen path. This divide-and-conquer routing focuses the limited adapter capacity where it most affects loss while reducing adapter FLOPs and activation memory. A schedule that warms K down (e.g., 64→16) is used during training.

Training: Use a two-stage regimen. Stage A (distillation warm-start): if available, distill from a full fine-tuned teacher (or larger model) by minimizing KL on logits and an L2 loss on a small subset of teacher attention maps at layers where biases are injected; this quickly places the shared basis and bias library in a good subspace. Stage B (supervised tuning): continue with the task loss and a balanced sampling mix across domains/instruction types to prevent overfitting the small adapters. Use module-wise learning rates (slightly higher for SLRA gates and bias mixer, lower for the shared bases) and gradient checkpointing; the base model stays 4-bit throughout. Inference keeps the adapters active with sparse routing (fixed K), incurring minimal latency and storing only a few megabytes of task parameters.
D.rationale:
This design concentrates learnable capacity into a few shared, reusable components (a global low-rank basis and a compact attention-bias library) while using task-state tokens to route that capacity to the most influential positions. It retains the strong prior of the frozen backbone, but supplies the degrees of freedom most needed for transfer—small linear subspace shifts and token-to-token bias adjustments—without updating billions of weights. The sparse top-K routing and 4-bit backbone reduce memory and compute to fit consumer GPUs/CPUs.

Compared to plain LoRA/prefix tuning, sharing the low-rank basis across layers and adding a global attention-bias library increases parameter efficiency and robustness under distribution shift, while the state-token-driven saliency targets compute to high-impact tokens. The distillation warm-start helps close the final performance gap to full fine-tuning by transferring teacher logits and partial attention structure into the small adapters, enabling parity or near-parity with orders-of-magnitude fewer trainable parameters.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is the prohibitive memory and compute cost of updating billions of parameters on consumer hardware, while still matching full fine-tuning performance. The method directly addresses this by (a) freezing the base LLM in 4-bit quantization so no gradients flow through billions of parameters, and (b) introducing a small adapter stack (SLRA, attention-bias library, task-state tokens) that is trained in reduced precision with sparse routing to minimize memory and optimizer state. The causal chain from problem to solution is traceable: parameter sharing across layers (SLRA) reduces trainable parameters from O(L·r·d) to O(r·d)+O(L·r), the 4-bit backbone cuts memory, and the distillation warm-start addresses the performance gap to full fine-tuning. Minor gaps exist in whether the sparse top-K routing mechanism and the distillation stage together are sufficient to fully close the performance gap to full fine-tuning in practice, but the mechanisms are clearly articulated and directly engage both the efficiency and performance-matching aspects of the failure mode.

query_text:
Vision-language models trained on web data degrade when images
come from a shifted distribution like medical or satellite;
how can we maintain cross-modal alignment under unseen domain
shift without target-domain labels?

A.method:
BridgeAlign is a two-stage, unsupervised adaptation pipeline that preserves text-image alignment under unseen domain shift by creating a style bridge and leveraging pseudo-pairs without any target labels. Start from a strong web-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP-like), keep the text encoder frozen to preserve the global language geometry, and insert lightweight adapters or LoRA layers into the image encoder. Assemble a broad text anchor bank (thousands of generic, domain-agnostic descriptions and paraphrases spanning attributes, actions, materials, shapes, spatial relations) to serve as a stable semantic prior during adaptation.

Phase A: Learn an unpaired style translator between source (web) and the unlabeled target domain (e.g., medical or satellite) using content-preserving image-to-image translation (e.g., CUT/MUNIT-style) trained only on images. Enforce content constancy with perceptual loss and an embedding-cycle constraint: the pre-trained image encoder’s features for an original and its translated counterpart should match. Use the trained translator to stylize source image-caption pairs into the target style, and fine-tune only the image-side adapters with the original captions via contrastive loss. This supervises the image encoder on target-style pixels while anchoring it to the unchanged language space, yielding immediate robustness to target textures and low-level statistics.

Phase B: Generate target-domain pseudo-pairs without labels by reverse-stylizing each target image back into a source-like appearance, captioning it with an off-the-shelf web-domain captioner, and filtering the captions by agreement with the frozen text encoder (similarity margin and mutual-consistency across multiple paraphrases). Train with temperature-scaled soft labels: use the similarity distribution over the text anchor bank as a soft target and minimize KL divergence from the model’s current distribution, while also applying a standard image-text contrastive loss between the target image and its accepted pseudo-caption. Add cross-view consistency by enforcing that the target image, its augmentations, its style-bridged variants, and multi-crop views map to similar text distributions and image embeddings.

Regularization and stability: (1) Source-anchor preservation: maintain performance on the source/web domain by penalizing drift in the similarity between a fixed set of source images and the text anchor bank (KL regularizer or elastic weight consolidation on image-adapter parameters). (2) Domain-invariant shaping: attach a lightweight discriminator predicting domain (source vs. target-style vs. reverse-stylized); train the image encoder with gradient reversal to suppress domain-specific cues. (3) Self-supervision on target images (masked image modeling or image-image contrastive) shares the same backbone to improve representation quality without relying on text. At deployment, enable test-time adaptation that updates only normalization statistics and image-side adapters via entropy minimization on the text-distribution predictions, with a small replay buffer to avoid drift. Evaluate with zero-shot retrieval/classification and text-guided detection/segmentation proxies on the target domain.
A.rationale:
The key failure under domain shift is at the pixel-statistics level, which perturbs the image encoder while the text space remains stable. Stylizing source image-caption pairs into the target appearance supplies supervised cross-modal signals in the correct style without needing target labels, directly realigning the image features to the frozen language space. Reverse-stylizing target images back to a familiar source-like appearance makes web-trained captioners and text encoders more reliable, enabling high-precision pseudo-pairs that further bind target images to text.

Cross-view consistency, soft-label KL over a large text anchor bank, and domain-adversarial regularization prevent overfitting to spurious textures and maintain the global geometry needed for zero-shot use. Parameter-efficient adaptation and source-anchor preservation mitigate catastrophic forgetting, yielding a model that retains web-domain competence while sustaining cross-modal alignment on unseen domains like medical or satellite imagery—achieved without any target-domain labels.

This matches the Score 4-5 range: multiple mechanism families are combined with no surfaced joint application, and the reverse-stylization-for-captioning + embedding-cycle constraint + soft KL over text anchor bank constitute named design choices not present in any surfaced paper. However, the individual mechanisms are all known, and the overall pipeline is an engineering combination rather than a single new mechanism, which places it closer to the Score 4 anchor than 5.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is cross-modal misalignment when the image encoder encounters shifted pixel statistics (medical, satellite) while the text encoder remains stable, and no target-domain labels are available. The method directly engages this failure mode through multiple traceable mechanisms: (a) style translation bridges the pixel-statistics gap by stylizing source image-caption pairs into target appearance, allowing contrastive training with known captions on target-style pixels; (b) reverse stylization makes web captioners reliable on target images, enabling pseudo-pair generation without labels; (c) the frozen text encoder serves as a stable anchor throughout, preserving language geometry while only image-side adapters are updated; (d) domain-adversarial regularization and source-anchor preservation address catastrophic forgetting and domain-specific cue suppression. The causal chain from problem (pixel-level distribution shift breaks image-text alignment, no labels) to solution (style bridge + pseudo-pairs + frozen text anchor + adapter-only updates) is traceable at each step. A minor gap exists in that the quality of the style translator itself depends on how well CUT/MUNIT generalizes to highly specialized domains like medical imaging, and the embedding-cycle constraint's effectiveness in preserving semantic content is assumed rather than proven, but these are effectiveness concerns rather than validity gaps.

B.method:
We propose Domain-Token Alignment and Grounded Adapters (DiTto-GA), a label-free adaptation pipeline that preserves vision–language alignment under unseen domain shift using only unlabeled target images. First, we learn compact, target-aware text modifiers that capture the target domain’s visual style. We generate content-centric pseudo-captions for each unlabeled target image with a general captioner, then remove target-style leakage from these captions using a lightweight text refiner so they describe content without domain cues. We introduce one or a small set of learnable special tokens that represent the target domain style and optimize them via textual inversion: when appended to the refined captions, the resulting text embeddings are trained to maximize CLIP-style similarity to the paired target images with an InfoNCE loss over mini-batches. This distills domain style into the text side without touching the base encoders and can be extended to multiple tokens by clustering target images and learning one token per cluster.

Next, we add parameter-efficient cross-modal “bridger” adapters inside the frozen image and text encoders to restore fine-grained interaction that often breaks under shift. Each adapter has a small cross-attention interactor and a convolutional zoom layer to inject spatial priors. To avoid full fine-tuning, we drive these adapters with a tiny visual hypernetwork that takes pooled target-image features and the learned domain-token embedding as input and generates per-sample adapter prefixes and low-rank updates. We train only the domain tokens, adapters, and hypernetwork using the pseudo-caption contrastive objective above, and we reweight samples with an online difficulty scorer so that reliable, informative pseudo-pairs dominate training. To harden grounding, we synthesize dispreferred negatives per image by removing or noising the most text-relevant regions identified by attention; we then apply a pairwise preference loss that enforces higher similarity for the clean image than for its region-dropped counterpart with the same caption, explicitly discouraging language-only matches.

We further regularize visual grounding without labels by maximizing a visual prompt dependency signal during training. For each pseudo-caption decoding step on content words, we compare the model’s token distribution with and without the image and add a penalty when predictions become language-prior dominated. This pushes the adapted model to depend on visual evidence even in domains where language priors are misleading. At inference, we enable a training-free selective patch routing module that progressively filters to the most text-relevant visual patches across scales, which improves robustness to background shifts and reduces hallucinations without touching the backbone.

Deployment is lightweight: at test time, we prepend the learned domain token(s) to user prompts (or select the closest token by nearest-neighbor against the target image in feature space), keep the base encoders frozen, and run selective patch routing. When the target stream is heterogeneous, we select or mix domain tokens per image via the clustering head. The entire procedure requires no target-domain labels and uses only unlabeled target images plus self-generated, style-neutral pseudo-captions.
B.rationale:
Unseen domain shift often manifests as systematic visual style differences that break text–image compatibility learned on web photos. By learning a small number of domain tokens via textual inversion and always appending them to prompts, we explicitly compensate for target style on the text side, restoring cosine alignment between text and target images without altering the base encoders. Parameter-efficient, cross-modal adapters—modulated per image by a hypernetwork—reintroduce early bidirectional interactions and spatial inductive bias that help when target domains (e.g., medical, satellite) differ in texture and layout, while keeping the adaptation footprint tiny.

Because pseudo-captions are noisy, we combine three safeguards: (i) sample reweighting from training dynamics to emphasize reliable pairs; (ii) preference learning with region-dropped dispreferred examples to force reliance on image evidence over language priors; and (iii) a visual-dependence regularizer that penalizes degeneration into language-only guesses. Finally, training-free selective patch routing at inference provides an additional robustness layer under shift. Together, these pieces maintain cross-modal alignment under label-free target shifts and reduce hallucination without needing any target annotations.

C.method:
We propose CURA-VLA: Curriculum, Unlabeled, Region-aware Alignment for Vision–Language Adaptation. Starting from a web-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP/LLM-based), we freeze the text tower to preserve general semantics and adapt the visual side plus the projection layers using only unlabeled target images. To capture fine-grained cues prevalent in medical or satellite imagery, we replace the single visual stream with a mixed visual encoder that fuses CLIP features with a texture-strong DINOv2 stream and processes inputs at any resolution via tiling. Each target image is partitioned into overlapping high-resolution tiles; local features from tiles and global features from the full image are concatenated and linearly projected into the original embedding space, ensuring compatibility with the frozen text tower. Strong style randomization (grayscale, histogram equalization, contrast/illumination shifts, speckle/Poisson noise, blur, and mild geometric jitter) is applied during adaptation to widen the visual support without labels.

We learn a domain encoder to schedule a source-to-target curriculum without target labels. Concretely, from source image–text pairs, we train a content encoder to be class/text-discriminative and a separate domain encoder to remove content via a confusion objective while remaining reconstructive, yielding a style/domain feature space. For each unlabeled target image, we compute its mean L2 distance to source domain features to estimate a domain gap and sort target instances from easy (closest) to hard (farthest). Adaptation proceeds in stages, progressively including farther target instances. At each stage we optimize three losses: (1) an image–text contrastive loss on the original source pairs to anchor alignment; (2) a domain-adversarial confusion loss between source and the current target subset in the domain feature space to strip style-specific shift; and (3) an alignment regularizer that encourages directional similarity and bounded-magnitude drift between image and text representations across layers of the multimodal projector.

To align to target content without labels, we generate pseudo-captions and preference signals in a self-supervised, robustness-first manner. For each target image, we sample multiple candidate captions from the base VLM under different augmentations and tilings, and retain the consensus nucleus (lexical units appearing across views) as a pseudo-caption, optionally expanded with domain terms retrieved from an external, unlabeled corpus (e.g., Wikipedia/biomedical abstracts/remote sensing glossaries) most relevant to the consensus tokens. We pair each image with this pseudo-caption as a soft positive in a low-temperature InfoNCE objective. To create hard negatives and emphasize precise grounding, we automatically synthesize textual foils by antonym/hypernym substitutions, attribute swaps, and number reversals filtered by entailment/plausibility checks; these serve as in-batch hard negatives. In parallel, we form region-aware preference pairs by identifying salient regions with class-agnostic visual saliency (e.g., DINO/DINOv2 attention or Grad-CAM on the CLIP image tower) and injecting localized perturbations (noise or erasing) while keeping the pseudo-caption fixed; the clean image–caption pair is preferred over the perturbed one. We optimize a multimodal preference loss that increases the score of preferred over dispreferred pairs, sharpening attention to content-bearing regions without any human labels.

Training interleaves source anchoring, curriculum-scheduled target adaptation, and preference-contrastive learning. Early stages emphasize easy targets and high-confidence pseudo-captions; later stages introduce harder targets and stronger hard negatives while decaying the weight of pseudo-caption losses to prevent drift. Throughout, we retain small-batch source pairs to stabilize the shared embedding space. The result is a target-robust visual projector that preserves cross-modal agreement with the frozen text tower, attends to domain-salient details, and remains compositionally precise under unseen shifts—all achieved without target-domain labels.
C.rationale:
The method maintains cross-modal alignment under unseen domain shift by (i) adapting only the visual pathway with a mixed, any-resolution encoder that captures domain-specific local texture and structure while keeping the text tower as a semantic anchor; (ii) using a curriculum over unlabeled target images based on a learned domain/style space to avoid catastrophic drift and to cover heterogeneous, compound target domains; and (iii) enforcing alignment through self-consistent pseudo-captions, hard textual foils, and region-aware preference signals that do not require any human annotations. Source contrastive training is retained during adaptation to anchor the shared space and prevent overfitting to noisy pseudo-labels.

The region-perturbation preferences and compositional hard negatives explicitly stress correct grounding and discourage plausible-but-wrong matches, a common failure under domain shift. The alignment regularizer directly controls representation geometry across modalities, linking internal similarity to downstream performance. Together, these components use only unlabeled target images (and optionally unpaired domain text) to preserve and sharpen image–text agreement in new domains such as medical or satellite imagery.

C.paraphrase_essence: Mappings learned on a source distribution are brittle: under unseen domain shift, correspondence between inputs and targets degrades, and adaptation is hard because target-domain labels are unavailable.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is cross-modal alignment degradation when a web-trained VLM encounters shifted visual distributions (medical, satellite) without any target-domain labels. The method addresses this through several mechanistically traceable steps: (a) freezing the text tower while adapting only the visual pathway preserves the semantic anchor and directly targets the visual distribution mismatch; (b) the curriculum scheduling based on domain gap estimation progressively exposes the model to harder target instances, directly engaging the unsupervised adaptation constraint; (c) pseudo-caption generation via augmentation consensus provides self-supervised alignment signals without labels; (d) domain-adversarial confusion loss strips style-specific shift in feature space; and (e) region-aware preference learning sharpens grounding to domain-salient features. Each component has a traceable causal pathway from the identified failure mode to a resolution mechanism. Minor gaps exist: the pseudo-caption quality for highly specialized domains (e.g., radiology) may be unreliable from a web-trained VLM, and the domain encoder's ability to cleanly separate content from style without labels is assumed rather than proven, but these are effectiveness concerns rather than validity gaps. The overall mechanism directly and plausibly engages the failure mode.

D.method:
We propose Interventional, Anchor-guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (I-AUDA) for vision–language models under unseen image-domain shift with no target labels. Starting from a web-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP-like), we freeze the entire text tower and the text-to-image temperature, and adapt only the image tower and the final projection with low‑rank adapters (LoRA). Adaptation is driven by two complementary signals obtainable from unlabeled target images: (i) counterfactual visual interventions with known edit semantics, and (ii) anchor-guided self-labeling against a fixed bank of generic text prototypes. Both are regularized to prevent representation norm drift and unimodal collapse.

Counterfactual visual interventions: For each target image, we generate K edited variants using label-free, content-preserving but attribute-changing transforms: attribute editing (e.g., color swaps via palette transfer; geometric flips and controlled relocations/copy–paste of objects with Poisson blending and low IoU to originals) and count-varying cutouts that randomly remove a fraction of salient instances. We keep an edit log describing which attributes changed. From the frozen text encoder we precompute a small dictionary of attribute prototypes (colors, spatial terms, sizes, textures, numerals). For each original–edit pair, we impose two losses: (a) counterfactual attribute consistency, which increases the image–text similarity to the edited attribute prototype(s) and decreases it for the pre-edit prototype(s); and (b) invariance, which preserves similarities to prototypes unrelated to the edit. This forces the adapted image encoder to respect visual evidence that truly changed while remaining stable to nuisance factors, maintaining cross-modal geometry tied to interpretable text atoms without any target labels.

Anchor-guided self-labeling: We assemble a generic prompt bank (1–5k universal anchors spanning shapes, materials, colors, textures, discrete numbers, common object supercategories) and keep their text embeddings fixed. For each target image (and its edits), we retrieve the top-m anchors by cosine similarity and treat them as pseudo-positives in an InfoNCE objective against the remaining anchors as negatives, with hard-negative mining across closely scored anchors. To prevent overconfident drift and embedding-norm explosion under shift, we add batch-wise zero-centering of the image–text logits and a magnitude alignment penalty that matches the first/second moments of image embeddings to those measured on the source domain (or a held-out source slice), while leaving the text distribution untouched. This keeps the adapted image space co-scaled with the fixed text space and stabilizes temperature-scale.

Region- and object-centric alignment: To avoid background shortcuts common under shift, we extract N region proposals per image (e.g., a generic proposer/segmenter) and compute multiple learned attention vectors over proposals to form an object-centric mixture embedding. We then apply the same anchor-guided contrastive loss at the region level, aligning each proposal to its best-matching anchors and encouraging the global image embedding to be a weighted mixture of region embeddings. This multi-object alignment reduces reliance on backgrounds and enforces fine-grained cross-modal consistency where it matters.

Modality-balance diagnostics and training control: During adaptation we compute a performance-agnostic multimodality contribution score on a small probe set of unlabeled target images using zero-shot prompts from the anchor bank. When vision contribution dips (unimodal collapse to text priors) or surges excessively (instability), we upweight the counterfactual and region losses or trigger early stopping. This provides an online guardrail to maintain healthy cross-modal alignment without any target annotations.
D.rationale:
Domain shift often perturbs low-level appearance and context statistics more than high-level semantics, causing the image tower to misalign with a fixed text space and fall back on language priors. By freezing the text tower and adapting the image side with lightweight LoRA, we preserve source semantics while giving the visual encoder just enough flexibility to re-anchor to the fixed text geometry. Counterfactual edits provide label-free, targeted supervision: because we know exactly what attribute we changed, we can enforce increases/decreases in similarity to the corresponding text prototypes and preserve similarity elsewhere, directly countering spurious correlations learned on the web.

Anchor-guided self-labeling complements this with dense, generic supervision from a fixed prompt bank, pulling target images toward stable, domain-agnostic text anchors while BSR-style zero-centering and norm alignment prevent overconfidence and norm dispersion that otherwise hurt OOD generalization. Object-centric alignment further mitigates background bias—common in medical and satellite imagery—by aligning at the region level. Finally, a performance-agnostic modality-contribution monitor keeps the adaptation process from collapsing to text priors or drifting, maintaining cross-modal alignment without any target-domain labels.

The method addresses this by (a) freezing the text tower and adapting only the image side with LoRA, directly targeting the asymmetric drift problem; (b) counterfactual visual interventions with known edit semantics that enforce attribute-level image-text consistency without labels, directly countering spurious correlations; (c) anchor-guided self-labeling against a fixed generic prompt bank that pulls target image embeddings toward stable text anchors; (d) norm/moment alignment to prevent scale mismatch between adapted image embeddings and the fixed text space; and (e) region-centric alignment to avoid background shortcuts specific to medical/satellite imagery. query_text:
Object detectors fail on rare classes with few or no labeled
instances; how can we detect and localize long-tail or novel
objects without per-class supervision?

A.method:
We propose NAUTILUS: a class-agnostic, language-aligned detector that learns to detect, localize, and optionally name long-tail and novel objects without per-class supervision. NAUTILUS is trained on unlabeled images (optionally with weak image-level text like captions or alt-text) and consists of three coupled modules: a high-recall class-agnostic proposal generator, a language-region alignment teacher–student for open-vocabulary scoring, and a prototype discovery head for naming-agnostic novel-object detection. The network uses a frozen or slowly-updated vision backbone (e.g., self-supervised ViT) to provide general features and avoids any class-specific bounding-box annotations.

(1) Class-agnostic objectness and localization. We first train a proposal module to produce boxes/masks purely from objectness cues. Starting from self-supervised features (e.g., DINO-like attention maps, edges, and motion if video is available), we derive soft objectness masks and generate proposals with high recall. The module is optimized with self-consistency across strong augmentations and multi-scale views: proposals that persist under geometric/color changes are encouraged via IoU-consistency and equivariance losses, while a small denoising head refines box coordinates using an auxiliary objective that aligns to boundaries from unsupervised saliency/edge maps. An EMA teacher provides temporal ensembling of objectness to stabilize training; low-confidence regions are kept as unlabeled foreground to preserve recall, yielding a category-agnostic localizer.

(2) Language–region alignment without per-class labels. To score proposals against an open vocabulary, we perform multi-instance contrastive learning between proposal crops and phrases extracted from weak text (captions, alt-text) or a broad, automatically curated phrase pool. For each image, an LLM parses phrases (nouns, attributes, and relations), and a text encoder embeds them. A region encoder embeds proposals. We then solve an optimal-transport matching that softly assigns phrases to a subset of proposals under coverage constraints, yielding pseudo region–phrase pairs. The EMA teacher filters pairs using calibrated uncertainty: a pair is accepted only if similarity is high and stable across augmentations and neighboring frames/images. The student is trained with: (i) region–phrase contrastive loss (multi-positive InfoNCE), (ii) hard negative mining from confusable phrases, and (iii) localization refinement losses guided by teacher boxes. This produces a detector whose classification head is parameterized by text embeddings at inference, enabling open-vocabulary scoring without ever seeing per-class box labels.

(3) Prototype discovery for novel classes and long-tail balancing. Beyond text alignment, we maintain a memory bank of region embeddings from confident foreground proposals and cluster them online into visual prototypes using entropy-regularized clustering. Each prototype acts as a classification anchor for detection queries; some prototypes are linked to phrases if their text similarity consistently exceeds a threshold, while the remainder are treated as unnamed novel categories (e.g., novel_k). Training batches are reweighted to upsample rare prototypes, countering long-tail imbalance without labels. We regularize the detector with prototype consistency: proposals matched to the same prototype across views must produce similar embeddings and boxes. Optionally, to suggest human-readable names, we aggregate the captions of images contributing to a prototype and query a language model offline; names are not used as supervision.

Inference. Given an image, NAUTILUS first generates high-recall proposals, then scores each region jointly with (a) text-conditioned logits for any user-provided or automatically-curated vocabulary via the shared vision–language space, and (b) prototype logits for label-free novelty. Regions with strong text alignment are output with their phrase labels; high-objectness regions with weak text alignment but strong prototype evidence are emitted as novel_k. Non-maximum suppression (or set-based decoding if using a transformer head) fuses overlapping detections. The result is a detector that can localize and optionally name rare and unseen objects, or flag them as novel when naming is uncertain.
A.rationale:
NAUTILUS removes the need for per-class box supervision by decoupling localization (category-agnostic objectness) from recognition (language- and prototype-based scoring). The proposal module learns where objects are via self-supervised cues and augmentation consistency, ensuring high recall even for unseen categories. Recognition is achieved by aligning region embeddings to phrases using weak, readily available text and contrastive training, so long-tail or truly novel categories can be scored at test time by providing their names or attributes—or flagged as novel when no phrase fits.

The prototype discovery component addresses the extreme long tail by creating label-free visual anchors that upweight rare modes during training and provide a fallback recognition path when language is insufficient or ambiguous. EMA-based pseudo-label filtering and cross-view stability control noise, while balanced sampling mitigates dominance of frequent patterns. Together, these choices yield an open-world detector that localizes and recognizes beyond supervised vocabularies, detects novel objects, and scales with uncurated web data without requiring per-class annotations.

novelty.reasoning: The searches reveal that while individual components of NAUTILUS exist in prior work (weakly supervised phrase grounding, region-phrase contrastive learning, open-vocabulary detection, class-agnostic proposals with self-supervised features, EMA teacher-student), no single published paper combines all three coupled modules as proposed: (1) self-supervised class-agnostic proposal generation with augmentation consistency/equivariance losses, (2) optimal-transport-based region-phrase alignment from weak text with EMA pseudo-label filtering, and (3) entropy-regularized online prototype clustering with long-tail rebalancing for novel object naming. The closest works (GLIPv2, CoDA, UniDetector) require some form of box annotations or predefined base categories, and none combine OT-based region-phrase matching with prototype discovery and fully annotation-free training. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing specific named design choices (OT matching + EMA filtering + prototype discovery head with entropy-regularized clustering) not found together in the literature, though individual mechanisms are known.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that object detectors fail on rare/novel classes due to lack of per-class labeled instances, requiring detection and localization without per-class supervision. The method addresses this through three coupled mechanisms: (a) class-agnostic proposal generation using self-supervised cues that avoids any class-specific box annotations, directly engaging the localization aspect; (b) language-region alignment via weak text supervision and contrastive learning, enabling open-vocabulary scoring without per-class labels; and (c) prototype discovery that clusters visual embeddings into unnamed novel categories and reweights rare prototypes, directly addressing the long-tail imbalance. The causal chain from problem to solution is traceable: no per-class supervision → objectness from self-supervised features → recognition from weak text alignment → novel object fallback via prototypes. Minor gaps exist in how the optimal-transport phrase-to-proposal matching handles truly novel objects with no textual description, but the prototype discovery module explicitly covers this case.

B.method:
Build a two-branch, open-world detector around a frozen, general-purpose vision backbone and a learned, class-agnostic proposal head. First, train the proposal head to predict objectness and boxes without relying on per-class labels. To bootstrap supervision when boxes on head classes are scarce, add an auxiliary saliency branch that produces objectness seeds and refine them online (e.g., with CRF) to supervise the proposal head. Optimize the classification (object vs. background) with an image-dependent ranking objective to cope with extreme imbalance, and, when videos are available, aggregate features over neighboring frames with motion compensation to stabilize objectness for small/rare instances. The result is a high-recall, class-agnostic proposal generator that localizes “things” regardless of category.

On top of these proposals, extract region embeddings with a frozen self-supervised or vision-language encoder (e.g., DINO/CLIP). Maintain a memory bank of proposal features and perform prototype discovery with an alternating procedure: (a) assign proposals to a dynamic set of fine-grained prototypes using neighbor-consistency in feature space and an entropy regularizer to avoid collapse; (b) update a lightweight classifier over these prototypes while inferring a coarse–fine mapping that routes all prototypes to the single coarse label “object.” Optionally, for base categories with labels, allow prototypes to attach to known classes when their similarity exceeds a threshold; otherwise they are treated as novel (unknown) prototypes. This discovers and stabilizes fine-grained groups that correspond to long-tail or novel objects without per-class supervision.

Turn the current proposal+prototype system into a teacher and train a student detection head with prediction-guided self-training. For each image, score proposal regions by a quality metric that multiplies objectness with agreement cues (e.g., augmentation/temporal IoU consistency and prototype confidence). Select top-scoring regions across scales and fit a smooth weighting over them; use these weighted pseudo boxes and prototype IDs to supervise the student’s box regression and prototype classification. Distill region features from the frozen encoder to the student’s head only on these key regions to avoid noise amplification, and iterate teacher–student updates over unlabeled data.

At inference, output boxes with either (i) base-class names, when a prototype is confidently mapped to a known class, or (ii) stable discovered prototype IDs marked as unknown. If names are desired for the unknowns, perform zero-shot naming by comparing each prototype centroid to text prompts with a frozen vision–language encoder; only attach a label when similarity clears a calibrated threshold, otherwise keep the unknown tag. This yields open-world detections that localize and group novel objects while remaining robust to long-tail frequency and lacking per-class supervision.
B.rationale:
The pipeline removes the reliance on per-class supervision by separating objectness (where are objects?) from semantics (what are they?) and anchoring both to frozen, broadly transferable features. A class-agnostic proposal head, aided by adaptive saliency and video aggregation, supplies high-recall localization irrespective of category. Prototype discovery over frozen features then provides fine-grained groupings for rare/novel objects using neighbor consistency and entropy regularization instead of labels, while a coarse–fine mapping keeps training stable under only the coarse “object” supervision.

Self-training is made reliable by focusing learning on the teacher’s most trustworthy regions via a prediction-guided weighting scheme, preventing the student from overfitting noisy pseudo labels. Finally, an image-dependent ranking loss combats extreme foreground–background imbalance typical in long tails. Together, these components enable detection and localization of long-tail and truly novel objects, with optional zero-shot naming but without requiring per-class supervision during training.

C.method:
Build a two-stage, open-vocabulary detector that separates class-agnostic localization from semantics and learns alignment without any per-class bounding-box supervision. Stage 1 is a generic objectness proposal module (e.g., an RPN or center-based head) trained to propose boxes for any object-like region. Stage 2 is a text-aligned region head: each RoI is encoded into a visual embedding that is directly comparable to text embeddings (e.g., CLIP space). At inference, the system supports two modes: (a) class-agnostic detection that returns objectness-ranked boxes for novel objects, and (b) text-conditioned open-vocabulary detection where a user-supplied phrase or a large vocabulary is matched to boxes via embedding similarity.

Train the semantic head with weak image–text supervision and matched-only losses. For each image–caption pair, extract candidate noun phrases and generate top-K class-agnostic proposals. Compute a similarity matrix between RoI visual embeddings and phrase embeddings, then perform bipartite (Hungarian) matching to select a sparse set of region–phrase positives. Apply a contrastive/text-alignment loss only to the matched pairs and with in-batch negatives from other images; treat all unmatched RoIs and phrases as unlabeled rather than hard negatives to avoid penalizing true-but-unannotated objects. To strengthen localization under weak labels, feed the regression features into the classification branch through a lightweight deformable convolution that samples around the current box; this bridges classification gradients into the regression representation so text alignment sharpens boxes even without per-class box supervision.

Inject semantic guidance and structure cues to make rare or novel categories more localizable. Fuse normalized text embeddings into the RoI attention blocks so attention weights spatially emphasize features consistent with the query phrase, improving grounding for unseen classes. Add auxiliary, training-only structure heads (edge and monocular depth) whose features modulate RoI features via attention; supervise these heads with off-the-shelf pseudo-labels. These cues encourage shape- and boundary-aware features that generalize beyond appearance statistics of head classes.

Improve box quality without labels via decoupled distillation and large-scale self-training. Distill localization from a class-agnostic teacher (e.g., salient-object or objectness/centerness predictor): apply regression/feature imitation only to the regression stream and attention maps, and keep classification/text alignment separate, preventing signal dilution. Then mine pseudo-boxes at scale: run the model over unlabeled images with a broad vocabulary, keep predictions that are high-similarity and stable under perturbations, and fine-tune with the same matched-only alignment plus the localization distillation. At test time, return either class-agnostic boxes for discovery or text-filtered boxes; optionally cluster the box embeddings to surface coherent novel categories without naming them.
C.rationale:
This design avoids per-class supervision by learning objectness and localization separately from semantics, aligning regions to language using only weak image–text signals. The matched-only loss with bipartite assignment prevents the common failure mode where missing labels force the model to treat true rare/novel objects as negatives, while Hungarian selection yields clean, non-duplicated positives. Bridging classification and regression lets semantic gradients improve box refinement despite the absence of box-level labels for each class, and decoupled distillation supplies a strong localization prior without injecting category bias.

Semantic-guidance attention and structure-enhancement auxiliaries make the model rely on text-consistent and boundary-aware cues rather than head-class appearance, boosting generalization to long-tail and unseen objects. Finally, self-training on high-confidence open-vocabulary predictions broadens coverage and calibrates the detector on novel categories without ever using per-class annotations, delivering a practical path to detecting and localizing long-tail or new objects.

D.method:
We propose an open-world region–text aligned detector trained without per-class box labels. A category-agnostic, set-prediction detector produces K region proposals per image using non-parametric object queries with Hungarian-matched supervision on objectness. The detector is trained end-to-end on image–text pairs: captions are parsed into noun phrases and projected with a text encoder; region features are projected with a visual head. A region–word contrastive loss aligns boxes with phrases that co-occur in the image, while a box regression head is updated jointly.

To make weakly supervised localization learnable, we couple the classification and regression branches: features from the regression path drive a lightweight deformable modulation in the classification head so gradients induced by region–text alignment improve spatial aggregation. Inside each candidate region, we further refine boxes by attending from the region token to its local patches, guided by the most similar phrase embedding; this region-to-local fusion sharpens boundaries when only image-level text is available. Training uses set prediction losses for objectness and box geometry, plus region–phrase contrastive losses.

To surface long-tail and novel objects without labels, we combine grounding-aware filtering and uncertainty-driven mining. For each candidate region, we compute a visual-dependence score that measures how much the aligned phrase prediction relies on the image versus the language prior; low-dependence alignments are down-weighted or rejected to prevent spurious text matches. We also estimate predictive uncertainty via Monte Carlo dropout over the open-vocabulary logits and objectness; high-uncertainty but high-objectness regions are queued for harder augmentation, consistency regularization, and clustering to form novel prototypes. Cluster centroids are weakly named by retrieving nearest text embeddings from a large vocabulary; names are optional—detection and localization proceed even when clusters remain unlabeled.

At inference, given any text vocabulary (including zero-shot prompts), we score each region against the text embeddings and return boxes with the best-matching phrases. Regions that are high-objectness yet low-similarity to any provided label are flagged as Unknown with their boxes and cluster ID. The set-prediction design avoids NMS and produces diverse, non-duplicated boxes, which helps recall rare instances. The system supports continual open-world updates by periodically fine-tuning on buffered high-uncertainty regions, requiring no per-class annotations.
D.rationale:
Aligning regions directly to language embeddings removes the need for per-class box supervision, enabling zero- and few-shot transfer to rare or unseen categories. However, weakly supervised training often fails to localize; coupling classification and regression so that localization signals flow into the classifier, and refining with region-to-local fusion, improves spatial precision when only image–text pairs are available. Using a set-prediction detector yields category-agnostic proposals without heuristic NMS, increasing coverage for long-tail instances.

Novelty and tail coverage further improve when training focuses on the right examples: uncertainty-guided sampling elevates hard, potentially out-of-distribution regions for stronger learning or discovery, while grounding-aware filtering suppresses language-prior hallucinations that would otherwise bias the open-vocabulary head toward frequent names. Together, these pieces detect and localize long-tail or novel objects without per-class supervision, while remaining compatible with optional zero-shot naming via text prompts.

query_text:
Online RL is expensive but offline data suffers from
distribution mismatch between behavior and target policy;
how can we learn a strong policy from logs alone while
avoiding extrapolation errors?

A.method:
We propose Support-Constrained Pessimistic Implicit Q-Learning (SC-PIQL), an offline-only algorithm that combines a calibrated estimate of dataset support with pessimistic value learning and trust-region advantage-weighted policy improvement. The key idea is to never rely on action-state pairs that the logs do not plausibly support and to make value estimates pessimistic whenever uncertainty or support is low. The method trains three modules in parallel: a behavior density/support estimator, a conservative Q–V critic that penalizes out-of-support and high-uncertainty actions in its targets, and a policy updated by advantage-weighted regression under an explicit KL trust region to the behavior.

First, fit a conditional behavior model mu(a|s) and a calibrated support score sigma(s,a). Learn mu with a high-capacity conditional density model (e.g., autoregressive flow or diffusion policy) on logged (s,a). Calibrate sigma by combining (i) the behavior model’s tail probability (or energy) at (s,a) and (ii) a k-nearest-neighbor or local KDE score on the dataset around s to reflect local coverage; map this combined score into [0,1] via temperature-scaled Platt calibration on a held-out split, where positive examples are real (s,a) pairs and negatives are synthetics sampled from mu and from a broad proposal (uniform or noisy). Freeze sigma and define an OOD penalty psi(s,a) = -log(max(eps, sigma(s,a))).

Second, learn a pessimistic critic. Train V(s) by expectile or quantile regression on dataset actions to avoid maximizing over out-of-support actions. Train an ensemble of Q(s,a) with distributionally robust Bellman targets: the next-state bootstrapped value uses a soft maximum over a candidate set drawn from a mixture of mu, the current policy, and a broad proposal, and subtracts two penalties at the next state: (a) an epistemic uncertainty term from the Q-ensemble variance and (b) the OOD penalty psi(s',a'). Add a conservative regularizer that pushes down Q for non-data actions by minimizing the gap between the dataset action-values and values of actions sampled from the mixture (stronger weight for low-sigma actions). This yields targets that are optimistic only within well-supported regions and pessimistic elsewhere, curbing extrapolation.

Third, improve the policy with support-weighted advantage regression under a KL trust region. Form per-sample weights w(s,a) = exp((Q(s,a) - V(s))/alpha) multiplied by sigma(s,a)^k (k ≥ 1), which amplifies advantages on supported actions and suppresses unsupported ones. Update the policy by maximizing the weighted log-likelihood of dataset actions subject to an average KL(pi(·|s) || mu(·|s)) ≤ ε constraint enforced with a Lagrange multiplier; start with small ε and anneal upward as the critic stabilizes, effectively moving from behavior cloning toward advantage-driven improvement. Use early stopping and hyperparameter selection via offline policy evaluation (e.g., doubly robust with learned density ratios clipped by sigma) on a validation split. The result is a strong, conservative policy that stays close to the data manifold while still improving over the behavior where the logs provide confident evidence.
A.rationale:
Extrapolation errors arise when the critic or policy assigns high value to actions with little or no support in the logged data. SC-PIQL tackles this at both the value and policy levels: the critic is explicitly pessimistic outside the data manifold using uncertainty and an OOD penalty, while the policy is only allowed to upweight advantageous actions in proportion to calibrated support and is constrained to remain close to the behavior via a KL trust region. Expectile-based value learning further avoids explicit maximization over unsupported actions.

This combination addresses distribution mismatch without requiring online interaction: it leverages advantage signals present in the logs yet conditions every improvement step on measured coverage. By decoupling improvement strength (alpha, ε) from support confidence (sigma) and pessimism (beta, lambda), the method can adapt across datasets with varying coverage, yielding robust gains over behavior while avoiding the classic failure modes of offline RL.

novelty.reasoning: The searches reveal several related works: PIQL (2025) combines IQL with a support constraint but uses projection-based parameters rather than calibrated density/KNN support scoring; CPED (NeurIPS 2023) uses flow-based density estimation for behavior policy with constrained policy optimization; various papers use ensemble uncertainty penalties or OOD penalties in offline RL. However, no paper surfaces the specific combination of (1) Platt-calibrated support scoring via KNN/flow density with synthetic negatives, (2) ensemble Q-learning with both epistemic uncertainty and OOD penalty in Bellman targets, and (3) support-weighted advantage regression under an explicit KL trust region with annealing. The individual mechanisms are known but their joint application in this exact form is not found in the literature. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with searches returning prior work on individual mechanisms but not their combination as proposed.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is distribution mismatch between behavior and target policy in offline RL, specifically extrapolation errors when the learned policy queries state-action pairs not covered by the logged data. The method directly addresses this through three coordinated mechanisms: (a) a calibrated support estimator sigma(s,a) that explicitly identifies which (s,a) pairs are covered by the logs, (b) a pessimistic critic that penalizes out-of-support and high-uncertainty actions via OOD penalties and ensemble variance, and (c) a policy improvement step that weights advantages by support scores and constrains the policy to stay near the behavior distribution via a KL trust region. Each mechanism can be traced causally to the failure mode: the support estimator identifies the problematic region, the pessimistic critic prevents value overestimation there, and the constrained policy update prevents the policy from exploiting those overestimated values. There are no significant gaps in the causal chain from problem to solution.

B.method:
Conformal Pessimistic Quantized Offline RL (CPQ-ORL). We first turn the continuous (or large discrete) action space into a compact, state-conditional candidate set learned purely from logs. Fit a behavior model p_b(a|s) and a quantizer g(s) that outputs K candidate actions from demonstrations (e.g., via action codebooks or a VQ head trained on logged (s,a)). Using a held-out calibration split, compute a per-state action score q(s,a) (e.g., behavior density or kNN-based pseudo-likelihood) and calibrate a threshold κ by conformal prediction to define a high-coverage, in-support action set C(s) = {a ∈ g(s): q(s,a) ≥ κ}. This yields a small, enumerably safe action set per state with finite-sample coverage guarantees; if C(s) is empty, back off to the top-1 behavior action or relax κ slightly to maintain feasibility.

Train a conservative critic over the quantized actions using a discrete CQL-style objective. For each dataset transition (s,a,r,s'), evaluate Qθ(s,·) on all candidates in g(s) and minimize TD error on the logged action while adding a log-sum-exp regularizer over candidates to penalize high Q on unobserved actions. To make policy improvement pessimistic and variance-aware, maintain an ensemble (or a Bayesian head) to estimate mean and epistemic standard deviation over Q, and form a lower-confidence-bound Q_LCB(s,a) = E[Q](s,a) − β·Std[Q](s,a). Use Q_LCB in Bellman targets and in action selection at s' by maximizing only over C(s'), i.e., y = r + γ max_{a'∈C(s')} Q_LCB(s',a').

Optimize a discrete policy πφ(a|s) supported only on C(s). Train πφ off-policy from logs by maximizing expected Q_LCB with two stabilizers: (1) clipped importance-corrected advantages using behavior probabilities (from the behavior model projected onto g(s)), akin to V-trace to reduce bias-variance under distribution shift; and (2) a KL regularizer to the behavior π_b(a|s) restricted to C(s), keeping the learned policy close to the data where value estimates are reliable. In practice, this reduces to advantage-weighted regression on logged actions with capped weights, while also learning logits for all candidates in C(s). At evaluation, act greedily with respect to Q_LCB within C(s) (or use a low-temperature softmax) to remain conservative.

Select hyperparameters (K, β, κ, KL weight, clip levels) via offline model selection using a conservative OPE stack: fit FQE/FQI critics on a separate validation split and report bootstrapped CIs; optionally use truncated-DR estimators with the behavior model. Choose the model maximizing a lower confidence bound on predicted return. The full pipeline requires no online interaction and directly addresses extrapolation by jointly restricting, calibrating, and pessimistically valuing actions.

Optional multi-log extension: when logs come from related sub-populations (users/tasks), place a shared prior over critic parameters and separate per-group statistics to decompose uncertainty. This yields tighter LCBs on well-covered substructures while remaining pessimistic on sparse groups, further improving sample efficiency and robustness.
B.rationale:
Extrapolation errors in offline RL arise when the policy queries actions outside the logged support and the critic overestimates their values. CPQ-ORL tackles this on three fronts. First, action quantization converts the problem into a small, enumerable candidate set, enabling exact conservative penalties over all plausible actions and eliminating reliance on continuous-action sampling. Second, conformal calibration builds a state-conditional, finite-sample-guaranteed support set C(s) from the logs, so the policy never optimizes over clearly out-of-distribution actions. Third, pessimistic Q-learning with lower-confidence bounds suppresses value inflation on weakly supported candidates, ensuring policy improvement is biased toward reliably estimated returns.

Off-policy training is stabilized with clipped importance corrections and a KL-to-behavior term, reducing mismatch between the logged distribution and the evolving policy while still allowing improvement within the safe set. Conservative OPE for model selection completes the loop, choosing policies that have high returns under pessimistic evaluation, thereby learning strong policies from logs alone while explicitly avoiding extrapolation.

off-policy distribution matching as a KL regularizer to the behavior

However, no paper found combines all three key mechanisms together: (1) demonstration-based action quantization/codebooks to define a compact candidate set, (2) conformal prediction calibration to provide finite-sample coverage guarantees on that action set, and (3) pessimistic LCB ensemble offline RL operating exclusively over the conformal-calibrated quantized action set. The closest papers (HACO, ConformalDQN) use conformal prediction for action masking but without action quantization from demonstrations; DLL uses diffusion-generated candidates with LCB but without conformal calibration. The proposed method also adds V-trace importance weighting, KL regularization, and conservative OPE model selection as a complete pipeline. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing the specific design choice of conformal-calibrated quantized action sets as the support constraint for pessimistic offline RL.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is distribution mismatch causing extrapolation errors when the learned policy queries out-of-distribution actions and the critic overestimates their values. The method addresses this through three layered mechanisms: (1) action quantization restricts the action space to a compact candidate set derived from logged data, (2) conformal prediction calibrates a state-conditional support set C(s) with finite-sample coverage guarantees, ensuring the policy only optimizes over in-support actions, and (3) pessimistic Q-learning with LCB estimates suppresses value overestimation on weakly supported candidates. Each mechanism directly engages the extrapolation failure mode — the first prevents querying OOD actions structurally, the second provides statistical guarantees on support coverage, and the third adds pessimism within the allowed set. The causal chain from problem (extrapolation errors from OOD actions) to solution (restrict, calibrate, penalize) is traceable at each step, with importance-weighted policy training and conservative OPE completing the offline-only loop. Minor gaps exist in how the quantizer g(s) handles states poorly covered in logs and whether conformal coverage translates cleanly to value estimation quality, but these are minor relative to the overall fit.

C.method:
ECLIPSE: an offline-only, pessimistic policy learning pipeline that combines (1) contrastive energy-based value regularization to actively suppress out-of-support actions, (2) interventional support estimation from multi-logger logs to correct distribution mismatch, and (3) lower-confidence-bound (LCB) policy improvement grounded in uncertainty. We model the soft Q-function as a negative energy Eθ(s, a) and train it with a conditional noise-contrastive objective over actions: for each logged state s and positive action a+, we draw k negatives a− from a learned proposal qφ(a|s) (plus a small uniform prior to catch blind spots) and minimize an NCE loss that classifies a+ vs {a−}. This self-normalizing EBM objective pushes up the energy of actions not supported by the data while keeping dataset actions low-energy. We combine this with standard TD learning, adding a CQL-like conservative regularizer implemented via the same negative samples, yielding a single-pass update that both bootstraps values and penalizes unseen actions.

To align training with the true behavior support, we estimate a context-dependent propensity model μ(a|s). When logs come from multiple historic behavior policies (e.g., versions over time or A/B variants), we harvest implicit interventions: identify state–action pairs appearing under different policies with different propensities and fit μ via an AllPairs-style objective that balances exposure across policies. These propensities weight the NCE negatives and the conservative penalty, amplify suppression of clearly out-of-support actions, and cap gradient influence from rare, high-variance regions. When only single-policy logs exist, μ is learned by behavior cloning and regularized with entropy to avoid overconfidence.

For policy improvement, we take a pessimistic route: compute an uncertainty-aware LCB Q̂LCB(s, a) = Q̂(s, a) − λ·σ(s, a), where σ comes from a lightweight uncertainty module (ensembles for single-task, or a hierarchical Bayesian head when logs span related tasks). We then optimize the actor with an offline, every-step preference-style objective: treat pairs (a+, a−) at each state as a preference where the margin depends on the LCB advantage ALCB(s, a) = Q̂LCB(s, a) − V̂LCB(s). Minimizing this stepwise preference loss is equivalent to an on-policy policy gradient under a shaped reward, giving a principled offline surrogate for improvement. Practically, we realize this as advantage-weighted behavior cloning with weights w(s, a+) = exp(ALCB/β), clipped by a function of μ(a+|s) to prevent drift outside the behavior support, and a small KL(π||μ) penalty for additional safety.

Training proceeds by alternating value and policy updates in a single dataset pass: sample minibatches, perform TD+contrastive EBM updates on Q, refresh μ and qφ periodically (qφ is trained to mimic π with exploration noise but is frozen during the critic update inside each step), and update π via the LCB-weighted preference/advantage objective. For model selection without online interaction, we use a doubly-robust OPE estimator that incorporates the interventional propensities; we early-stop and tune λ, β by maximizing the OPE LCB, not the mean, to remain conservative. The resulting policy is strong within the logged support and avoids extrapolation errors by construction.
C.rationale:
The core failure mode in offline RL is value overestimation for actions the behavior policy rarely or never took. ECLIPSE addresses this in three layers. First, the energy-based, conditional NCE training explicitly contrasts logged actions against diverse negatives at each state, pushing down Q for unsupported actions instead of relying solely on implicit regularization; this mirrors conservative Q-learning but with an efficient, sample-based partition surrogate that scales to continuous actions. Second, when multiple loggers are available, interventional propensity estimation recovers context-dependent support and reduces exposure bias, letting us reweight both the contrastive and improvement objectives toward truly supported regions. Third, LCB-based policy improvement uses uncertainty to remain pessimistic in poorly covered areas, while a stepwise preference-style actor update provides an offline surrogate to the on-policy gradient, focusing improvement on actions with demonstrably positive, conservative advantage.

Together, these pieces turn logs into a reliable training distribution: the critic learns to distrust OOD actions, the actor stays near the behavior support yet exploits conservative advantages, and evaluation/tuning respects uncertainty. The result is a strong policy learned from logs alone that avoids extrapolation errors without any online data collection.

C.paraphrase_essence: When training must rely solely on logged data collected under a different behavior, optimizing for a stronger target decision rule is unreliable due to distribution shift and out-of-support extrapolation, while obtaining on-policy data is costly.

The closest related work includes: CQL for conservative Q-learning, Fisher-BRC connecting score matching/EBM to critic regularization, MC-ETM using EBMs for transition models, HierOPO for hierarchical LCB in multi-task bandits, and CPBM/AllPairs for context-dependent propensity estimation in ranking.
