validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that continuous, entangled representations cannot support compositional reasoning over novel combinations because they lack discrete, reusable components. The FQC method directly addresses this by (a) factorizing representations into object/attribute/relation candidate units, (b) discretizing them via residual vector quantization with separate codebooks and de-entanglement losses, and (c) adding a logic-execution layer that composes discrete IDs symbolically with backtracking and soft unification. The causal chain is traceable: entanglement is addressed by mutual information penalties between aspect codes and separate codebooks; discreteness is achieved via VQ; reusability and novel recombination are enabled by the symbolic executor and compositionality curricula with held-out compounds. The only minor gap is that the soft matching to tolerate quantization noise could partially re-introduce entanglement, but this is a minor concern in an otherwise well-specified mechanism.

D.method:
Build a Discrete Skill Library around a base encoder by factorizing and gating. Split representation learning into two coordinated encoders: a skill encoder that is trained to be maximally predictive of task targets and a context encoder that is adversarially regularized to be non-predictive of those targets while both together reconstruct the input. The confusion objective severs task-relevant content from nuisance factors without discarding information, so candidate skills are isolated in the skill encoder while context remains available for robustness.

Attach a bank of lightweight skill modules (e.g., adapter blocks) and learn to activate a sparse subset per example using hard-concrete gates with an L0-style sparsity penalty. The gating network reads the skill-encoder features and outputs near-binary selections, yielding discrete reuse decisions. To stabilize module identities, maintain a prototypical memory for each module that aggregates the encoder features seen when that module fires, trained with a prototypical contrastive loss so each module’s prototype becomes a persistent, reusable anchor. A domain/context-aware mixer uses the context encoder to interpolate between direct features and the appropriate prototype mixture when distribution shifts are detected, keeping skills stable and portable.

To support composition, represent the selected skill set with an injective, order-invariant multiset embedding that uniquely encodes which modules are active and with what strengths, ensuring that different combinations map to distinct composition codes. Feed this code, plus a small residual of direct features, to the task head. During routing, train an auxiliary sparse-attention pathway alongside a standard dense path over tokens/entities so the model learns to focus on a few relevant modules and inputs without sacrificing training stability; the dense path guarantees convergence while the sparse path sharpens discrete selection.

Enable fast adaptation and novel recombination by learning tiny, per-module residual adapters when moving to new tasks rather than altering module cores. Adapter magnitudes act as a signal of transfer difficulty and guide when to instantiate new modules versus reuse existing ones. Use a simple compositional curriculum: start with single-skill examples, then grow to pairs and higher-order combinations; where available, incorporate object- or instruction-level supervision that enumerates atomic capabilities to align modules to natural sub-tasks. At inference, the gating yields a small set of modules, the prototypical memory provides stable anchors, and the injective multiset embedding composes them into a code the head can reliably generalize over to unseen combinations.
D.rationale:
Discrete, reusable components arise from two complementary pressures: near-binary gating that enforces selection sparsity and a prototypical memory that stabilizes each selected component as a persistent anchor. Adversarial confusion with reconstruction pushes task signal into the skill pathway while preserving completeness, reducing entanglement between skills and nuisance context. The auxiliary sparse attention further sharpens component selection without destabilizing training, encouraging small, interpretable subsets to be used per instance.

Compositional generalization is supported by an injective multiset embedding that preserves the identity of which components are active irrespective of order, ensuring that novel combinations map to unique, well-structured codes. Lightweight per-module residual adapters let the system adapt to new tasks by minimally extending or reweighting existing skills, promoting reuse rather than relearning. The curriculum and optional object/instruction supervision provide gentle pressure to align modules with atomic capabilities, making recombination over unseen mixes natural and reliable.

query_text:
Complex sequential tasks have nested goal structure but flat
policies struggle with credit assignment over long horizons;
how can we learn or impose a hierarchy that decomposes the
task and accelerates learning?

A.method:
We propose a two-level framework that learns a hierarchical decomposition directly from interaction, then exploits it to shorten credit assignment. First, learn a compact, controllable latent representation z(s) with self-supervised objectives that jointly predict future embeddings, infer actions (inverse dynamics), and separate slow-varying from fast-varying factors. Maintain an online k-nearest-neighbor transition graph in this latent space and run change-point detection on trajectories to segment behavior where latent dynamics or controllability shifts. From the graph, score candidate states for subgoal status using bottleneckness and controllability criteria, then cluster them into a small, adaptive library of subgoal prototypes with novelty thresholds that allow the set to grow as exploration expands.

Second, instantiate a goal-conditioned low-level policy and termination model that execute temporally-extended skills aimed at each subgoal prototype. Train these skills off-policy with intrinsic rewards defined by shaped distance-to-goal in latent space, a sparse success bonus inside an epsilon-ball around the prototype, and a diversity regularizer that encourages distinct termination distributions across skills. Use hindsight relabeling to convert off-target rollouts into successful experiences for nearby goals, and learn termination to fire at achievement or upon predicted failure to avoid wasting steps. Safety and stability are handled by constraining actions to a learned feasible set and by penalizing high-uncertainty regions during early training.

Third, train a meta-controller that operates on the subgoal graph as a semi-MDP: at decision times it selects the next subgoal for the low-level policy to pursue, receives option-level transitions, and updates from returns aggregated over skill executions. To tighten credit assignment, use a return decomposition that attributes global reward changes to the chosen subgoal via a difference-in-differences baseline, and supplement with a potential-based shaping term given by the change in a learned value over the subgoal graph. Maintain separate replay buffers for option-level and primitive-level updates, and learn a model over the subgoal graph to support planning and lookahead when useful.

Finally, apply an adaptive curriculum over the graph: early on, sample subgoals that are close in latent distance and high in feasibility; as competence grows, expand to longer multi-subgoal chains discovered by graph search. Periodically prune or merge redundant subgoals using success rates and representational proximity, and admit new subgoals when encountering novel bottlenecks. The entire system trains end-to-end: representation and subgoal discovery update online, skills refine with intrinsic and hindsight signals, and the meta-controller optimizes over short-horizon semi-MDP transitions, yielding accelerated learning on long-horizon, nested-goal tasks.
A.rationale:
Long-horizon credit assignment is hard because sparse rewards arrive far from the actions that matter. By discovering subgoals as structural bottlenecks in a learned latent transition graph, then training goal-conditioned skills to reach them, the method compresses the horizon into a sequence of few, semantically meaningful decisions. The meta-controller learns over option-level transitions with explicit termination, turning credit assignment into a semi-MDP problem with far shorter effective depth.

Intrinsic shaping in latent space, hindsight relabeling, and a difference-based return decomposition provide dense, correctly targeted learning signals for the low- and high-level policies without requiring manual task sketches. The adaptive curriculum and dynamic subgoal library keep the hierarchy aligned with the environment’s nested structure, while planning on the subgoal graph improves exploration and sequencing, leading to faster and more stable learning than a flat policy.

novelty.reasoning: The proposed method is a highly composite system combining: (1) self-supervised latent representation with slow/fast factor separation and inverse dynamics, (2) online k-NN transition graph with change-point detection for trajectory segmentation, (3) bottleneckness + controllability scoring for subgoal candidates, (4) adaptive subgoal prototype library with novelty thresholds, (5) goal-conditioned skills with diversity regularization, hindsight relabeling, and learned termination, (6) meta-controller with difference-in-differences return decomposition and potential-based shaping over a subgoal graph, and (7) adaptive curriculum with graph-search-based chain expansion and subgoal pruning/merging. Searches surface related work on latent landmark graphs (HILL), probabilistic subgoal representations, slow dynamics for subgoal learning, DADS, and goal-conditioned HRL with planning — but no paper combines change-point detection for segmentation, controllability-scored bottleneck subgoals, diversity-regularized skills with learned termination, AND a difference-in-differences return decomposition in a single framework. Individual mechanisms appear separately in the literature, but the specific combination — especially the change-point/controllability subgoal scoring, the diversity regularizer across skills, and the difference-in-differences credit attribution at the option level — has no direct published match. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines multiple known mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with at least one named design choice (difference-in-differences baseline for option-level credit assignment) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode identified in the query is that flat policies struggle with credit assignment over long horizons due to nested goal structure in complex sequential tasks. The method directly addresses this by (a) discovering subgoals as bottlenecks in a learned latent transition graph, which imposes hierarchical decomposition; (b) training goal-conditioned low-level skills with intrinsic rewards and hindsight relabeling to shorten effective horizons; and (c) training a meta-controller over a semi-MDP with explicit return decomposition (difference-in-differences baseline and potential-based shaping) to tighten credit assignment at the option level. Each component causally engages the failure mode: subgoal discovery creates the hierarchy, skills compress temporal credit assignment within each subgoal segment, and the meta-controller's return decomposition directly attributes rewards to the correct subgoal choice. The causal chain from problem to mechanism is traceable at each level with no significant gaps.

B.method:
Construct a two-level agent that plans over an abstract task graph while executing with a library of learned options. First, learn a compact state representation and cluster it to form coarse regions and bottlenecks; connect them into an abstract graph whose nodes represent contexts (e.g., rooms/stages/regimes) and whose edges capture admissible transitions. Equip the graph with a small set of Boolean predicates that detect progress events (e.g., entered region R, acquired key, opened door). When task instructions or specifications are available, compile them into a progress automaton; otherwise, learn a simple reward machine from rollouts to encode likely subgoal orderings. The high-level policy acts on the product of the abstract graph and the progress automaton, choosing subgoals (next region/predicate to satisfy). The low level is a library of option-policies that aim to achieve these subgoals; discover them by turning continuous actions into state-dependent, discrete macro-action candidates and then training goal-conditioned controllers that terminate when the target predicate flips true. A lightweight router selects the top-k option experts conditioned on the current latent state to reduce gradient interference and specialize execution.

Train the hierarchy in two decoupled phases before joint fine-tuning. Phase A (cold-start): pretrain options with imitation or goal-conditioned RL using a verifier that labels when a subgoal is completed; in parallel, infer abstract trajectories (region/predicate sequences) from data and distill them into the high-level planner with a KL-regularized objective. Phase B (planner-first): improve only the high-level planner with multi-step RL, rewarding subgoal completeness computed by a verifier that checks option outcomes; mask low-level execution details from the planner loss to avoid diluting its gradients. Phase C (joint): unfreeze options and continue off-policy training with a hierarchical replay buffer. Regularize each option with a shared hierarchical prior so options that solve similar subgoals warm-start one another; update this prior across options/tasks so its uncertainty shrinks as the library grows, improving sample efficiency.

Stabilize credit assignment and exploration with targeted coupling between levels. Provide the low level with an auxiliary guidance reward that encourages trajectories consistent with the current high-level subgoal, but activate it only when the high-level is on a plausible path (as judged by the abstract graph and verifier). Learn a state-dependent Lagrange multiplier to trade off guidance versus exploration so the low level is not over-constrained off the correct path. To avoid plan collapse on multimodal tasks, maintain a small ensemble of high-level planners via particle-based variational updates; sample or hedge over particles at decision time to cover distinct subgoal orderings while keeping them close to a prior for stability.

Enable fast transfer and scaling across tasks by abstracting and sharing transition structure. Represent each discovered option by a transition template that summarizes its typical state-change pattern and reward, letting the agent match and adapt options across environments even when state/action spaces differ. Use these templates to initialize option policies and dynamics models in new tasks and to decide when an unseen transition merits a new option. Throughout, use off-policy data and importance-corrected updates at both levels, and keep the option library small and load-balanced via router regularization to prevent expert collapse.
B.rationale:
Long-horizon credit assignment is reduced to short-horizon decisions by making the high level optimize progress through an automaton over abstract regions/predicates and by rewarding verified subgoal completions. The low level learns to achieve these verifiable subgoals, so gradients align with immediate, well-shaped signals rather than sparse end rewards. Decoupling planner and executor training further isolates objectives and prevents gradient dilution, while an on-track guidance signal with a learned trade-off stabilizes the interaction between levels.

Sample efficiency improves via multiple mechanisms: state-dependent discretization turns continuous control into discrete macro-choices that admit exact value maximization; a mixture-of-experts router allocates capacity to specialized behaviors and reduces gradient conflict; an ensemble of planners preserves diverse strategies without collapse; and hierarchical priors and transition templates share structure across options and tasks, shrinking uncertainty as experience accumulates. Together, these elements learn or impose a task hierarchy that decomposes complex goals and accelerates learning in practice.

The method directly addresses this by constructing a two-level hierarchy: a high-level planner operating over an abstract task graph and progress automaton (decomposing nested goals into subgoal sequences), and a low-level option library trained to achieve verifiable subgoals (converting sparse long-horizon rewards into dense short-horizon signals). C.method:
We propose Hierarchical Look-ahead with Skill Effect Models and Process Preferences (HiL-SEPP), a training-and-planning framework that turns long-horizon, nested-goal problems into short-horizon decisions with strong, step-level credit. HiL-SEPP maintains an explicit, structured world-state for high-level reasoning, learns a compact library of reusable goal-conditioned skills, equips each skill with a learned effect model (terminal state, success probability, and cost), and plans over sequences of skills and subgoals via look-ahead search. To assign credit to intermediate choices, it augments RL with process-level preference learning built from shared-prefix rollouts, and it optionally uses outer-loop scaffolding to select environment constraints or curricula that accelerate inner-loop learning.

Stage A — Structured high-level state: Represent the task at the high level with a persistent, object-centric memory that tracks entities, relations, and task progress flags (e.g., which subtasks are complete, resource counts, latent timers). A lightweight "reader" updates this memory from observations and recent actions; a "writer" applies state deltas triggered by executed skills. All planning and subgoal proposals operate on this memory, enabling closed-loop replanning and robustness to partial observability.

Stage B — Skill learning and effect modeling: Learn a small set (e.g., 5–15) of goal-conditioned skills (options) such as navigate(to g), reach(g), grasp(o), place(o, g), transfer(o, g). Train each with off-policy RL and hindsight relabeling to master diverse subgoals, and learn its critic for value estimates. In parallel, fit a per-skill effect model that, given the current high-level state and the skill’s parameters, predicts (i) the terminal high-level state (coarse transition), (ii) success probability, and (iii) execution cost. Use object-centric encodings with a GNN head to support varying scene compositions; calibrate success probabilities for reliable pruning.

Stage C — Look-ahead planning over skills and subgoals: At decision time (and periodically during training for guided exploration), run a search (e.g., Weighted A*) in the space of (skill, subgoal) tuples. From the current memory state, sample candidate subgoals per skill, prune low-probability branches using the success model, roll out the effect models to predict successor states, and score partial plans by accumulated predicted cost, distance-to-goal heuristics, and (optionally) leaf skill critics. Execute only the first skill of the best plan to limit model error, then update the memory and replan—yielding hierarchical, closed-loop control that compresses the effective horizon to skill terminations.

Stage D — Process-level preference credit: To resolve ambiguous credit among alternative subgoals or decompositions, generate shared-prefix branches at the same high-level state by sampling multiple candidate subgoals for a chosen skill. Score these short rollouts with a learned progress/reward model trained to prefer branches that more reliably increase goal proximity or downstream returns. Construct preference pairs only among branches that share the same prefix, and optimize a high-level policy or subgoal proposal network with a margin-based pairwise loss, combining this with standard RL updates. This yields stable, step-level supervision aligned with long-horizon success.

Optional Stage E — Scaffolding and curriculum: Add an outer loop that selects environment scaffolds (e.g., fixtures, reset poses, obstacle placements, or task parameter ranges) to maximize the inner loop’s learning speed and success. Treat scaffold selection as a black-box optimization over a short horizon of inner-loop episodes, and gradually anneal scaffolds away as skill competence and planning reliability improve.
C.rationale:
Decomposing behavior into reusable goal-conditioned skills collapses long-horizon credit assignment into a sequence of short-horizon skill choices with clear terminations, while learned effect models let a planner evaluate and prune many decompositions without expensive trial-and-error. Executing only the first skill of a planned sequence limits model error accumulation and enables rapid re-planning as the world state evolves. Maintaining an explicit, structured state across decisions gives the high-level policy a stable abstraction for nested goals, temporal constraints, and latent variables.

Flat end-to-end rewards rarely tell which subgoal choice was decisive; shared-prefix, process-level preference learning supplies dense, step-level supervision that directly compares alternative decompositions at the same decision point, improving subgoal selection and option grounding. Finally, outer-loop scaffolding reduces exploration burden and funnels uncertainty during early learning, yielding faster skill acquisition and more reliable high-level planning—together accelerating convergence on complex, nested-goal tasks.

The method directly addresses this through multiple interlocking mechanisms: (a) hierarchical decomposition into goal-conditioned skills with effect models that compress long horizons into short skill-level decisions, (b) look-ahead planning over skill/subgoal sequences that explicitly handles nested goal structure, (c) process-level preference learning using shared-prefix rollouts that provides dense step-level credit signals to resolve the credit assignment problem, and (d) structured high-level state representation that tracks task progress and enables closed-loop replanning. D.method:
We propose Hierarchical Information-Guided Options (HIGO), a two-level architecture that learns subgoal abstractions and trains a high-level planner over them while low-level option policies execute the subgoals. The planner operates over a compact, discrete API of subgoal predicates (e.g., reach(x), pick(y), open(z)) or option IDs, and emits a subgoal plus a termination budget. Option policies run at high frequency to realize the subgoal and return structured feedback (success/fail, confidence, side-effects). A learned verifier maps short execution traces to a scalar completeness score for the issued subgoal, standardizing feedback and allowing the planner to be optimized separately from motor control.

HIGO discovers reusable subgoals by segmenting raw trajectories with a stepwise information-gain signal. We train a trajectory-outcome supervisor that predicts task success (or return) from partial trajectories. For each step, we compute the reduction in predictive uncertainty when that step is revealed; peaks in this information gain mark bottlenecks and natural subtask boundaries. We cluster high-IG states across episodes to form subgoal prototypes and fit lightweight symbolic or function-like signatures to each cluster (automatically labeling arguments from nearby objects when available). To capture nested and overlapping roles of states, we endow the high-level state embedding with K latent facets and treat the active facet as a latent variable; different facets correspond to different subgoal roles available in the same physical state.

Training proceeds in two decoupled phases. Option policies are trained first with intrinsic rewards from the verifier: an option receives dense shaping as it approaches its prototype and a completion bonus when the verifier marks the subgoal complete; termination is learned via success or timeout. In parallel, we cold-start the planner from macro-trajectories implied by the IG-based segments and then fine-tune with RL using only planner-targeted rewards: the external task reward plus subgoal-completeness scores aggregated over the option’s execution window. During planner updates, we sample multiple candidate subgoals per decision point (nearest prototypes, language-matched candidates, or facet-conditioned ones), rank them by verifier confidence and IG, and update with a filtered distribution that emphasizes reliable extremes while de-emphasizing ambiguous mid-confidence choices. Planner gradients are computed only on planning actions; low-level execution losses are masked to avoid gradient dilution.

At execution time, the planner runs at a lower frequency, selecting a subgoal and active facet; the corresponding option executes until success/termination. The verifier’s structured feedback closes the loop, enabling replanning on failure. HIGO updates continually: new rollouts refresh the supervisor and verifier, subgoal prototypes are merged or split based on usage and IG stability, and the facet gating network is refined by sampling the active facet per decision and updating only the selected facet and option. This yields a compact, evolving library of options and an increasingly competent planner over long horizons.
D.rationale:
Long-horizon credit assignment becomes tractable when decisions are made at a macro level with clear, verifiable milestones. By discovering subgoals at steps that most increase predictability of success (information-gain peaks), HIGO aligns its hierarchy with the task’s intrinsic bottlenecks rather than arbitrary time slicing. The verifier turns sparse end rewards into reliable intermediate signals, so options can learn quickly and the planner can be optimized on planning quality alone without interference from motor learning noise.

The latent-facet state representation allows a single physical state to participate in multiple nested goals, which is common in complex tasks. Rank-based filtration over candidate subgoals further improves sample efficiency by training the planner on the most reliable supervisory signals and avoiding overfitting to noisy, mid-confidence choices. Altogether, the modular interface, IG-guided discovery, verifier-shaped option learning, and filtered planner optimization produce a practical hierarchy that accelerates learning and improves robustness on complex sequential tasks.

query_text:
Generating intermediate reasoning steps before the final
answer sharply improves LLM accuracy; how can we apply
analogous intermediate-computation patterns to vision,
reinforcement learning, or recommendation models?

A.method:
We propose Commit-Check-Execute (CCE), a unified intermediate-computation framework that inserts an explicit, compact planning layer between perception/policy and final prediction. CCE has three learned components that share a backbone encoder: (1) an Intermediate Generator (IG) that emits a short, discrete or low-dimensional "plan"; (2) a Verifier (V) that scores plans for faithfulness to the input and estimated downstream utility; and (3) an Executor (E) that conditions on the selected plan to produce the final output (answer, action, or ranking). Training starts with multi-task supervision on both the main objective and weakly supervised intermediate targets; inference performs sample-and-select over multiple plans to exploit the verifier, akin to self-consistency in LLMs.

For each domain, the plan is a structured bottleneck. Vision: IG predicts a scene sketch composed of object slots, relations, and optional stepwise operations in a small DSL; E executes the sketch to answer VQA, localize referring expressions, or classify with part-aware reasoning. Weak supervision is bootstrapped by object detectors, relation extractors, or differentiable program executors to produce pseudo scene-graphs/programs. Reinforcement learning: IG emits subgoal waypoints and skill choices at a coarse cadence; E is a low-level policy conditioned on the active subgoal/skill. Hindsight relabeling and trajectory segmentation provide pseudo subgoals; a lightweight world model in V estimates reachability and value of plan candidates. Recommendation: IG outputs an intent sketch (e.g., category, facets, price band, diversity/novelty constraints); E is a reranker that scores candidates under the intent. Pseudo-intents are mined from queries, session transitions, and item taxonomies.

The learning objective combines task loss with plan supervision and execution-consistency: L = L_task + λ L_plan + β L_consistency. L_plan aligns IG with pseudo-labels; L_consistency penalizes mismatches between executing the plan (via E or a differentiable proxy/world model) and observed outcomes. After an initial teacher-forced phase, we add a self-improvement loop: IG samples K plans, V scores them (faithfulness + predicted utility), E executes the top plan, and we log tuples (input, plan, outcome). Successful plans are upweighted via advantage-weighted updates; poor but faithful plans inform IG/V calibration. KL regularization keeps plans short and discrete for interpretability and compute control.

At inference, CCE allocates extra compute to the planning layer: sample M diverse plans with nucleus sampling or beam search; prune with V to a small set; optionally run shallow rollouts (RL) or counterfactual ranking estimates (rec) to refine scores; execute the best plan or aggregate via mixture-of-plans. We evaluate across domains with paired ablations: no-plan baseline, latent-plan without verifier, and full CCE. Metrics include final accuracy/return/NDCG and intermediate quality (scene-graph F1, subgoal reachability, intent-prediction calibration).
A.rationale:
CCE mirrors the accuracy gains of LLM chain-of-thought by externalizing hard-to-learn computation into short, verifiable plans that can be sampled, scored, and revised before committing to a final action. The explicit plan bottleneck decomposes perception/policy into interpretable subproblems, while the verifier provides a learned selection mechanism that rewards plans that both fit the input and are likely to succeed downstream. This enables self-consistency-style improvements without requiring ground-truth rationales.

Domain-specific plan choices make the intermediate both causal and checkable: scene sketches can be executed against image features; subgoals can be simulated with a world model or checked via reachability; intent sketches can be evaluated with off-policy estimators. Weak supervision and a self-improvement loop make the approach practical at scale, and the sample-and-select inference exploits additional compute where it matters—on the intermediate reasoning—yielding robustness and better credit assignment, especially in long-horizon RL and multi-constraint recommendation.

novelty.reasoning: The searches reveal no published work that combines all three CCE components (Intermediate Generator + Verifier + Executor as a unified framework) applied jointly across vision, RL, and recommendation domains. Individual mechanisms exist: hierarchical RL with subgoal generation and world models is well-established; neuro-symbolic VQA with program execution is known; process reward models for LLM reasoning are active. However, no paper surfaces that (1) unifies these into a single named framework with shared backbone, (2) applies the same commit-check-execute pattern across all three domains simultaneously, or (3) combines the specific training objective (L_task + L_plan + L_consistency) with a self-improvement loop and sample-and-select inference across vision/RL/recommendation. The closest match is the general pattern of plan-generate-verify (CADMorph) but for a completely different problem. This matches the Score 4-5 range: the combination of mechanism families (discrete plan bottleneck + learned verifier scoring faithfulness+utility + executor + self-improvement loop + cross-domain unification) has no surfaced published joint application. The cross-domain unification and the specific three-component architecture with the consistency loss and self-improvement loop represent a non-trivial combination not found in literature, but the individual components (hierarchical RL, neuro-symbolic VQA, intent-based recommendation, process reward models) are all well-known. The novelty lies in the unified framing and the specific combination, which matches the Score 4 anchor.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that non-language modalities (vision, RL, recommendation) lack analogous intermediate-computation mechanisms to chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, which limits their accuracy on complex tasks. The proposed CCE method directly addresses this by introducing a structured planning layer (IG → V → E) that explicitly generates, verifies, and executes intermediate "plans" before producing final outputs — mirroring the chain-of-thought pattern. The mechanism is domain-specific and traceable: scene sketches for vision, subgoal waypoints for RL, and intent sketches for recommendation each serve as the intermediate computation bottleneck, with weak supervision and self-improvement loops providing practical grounding. The causal chain from problem (no intermediate computation in non-LLM models) to solution (explicit plan generation, verification, and execution) is clearly articulated with minimal gaps, though the verifier's ability to reliably score plan quality without ground-truth rationales remains a minor assumption.

B.method:
We propose Intermediate-Compute Programs (ICP): a unified, step-wise planning-and-execution loop that makes intermediate computation explicit across vision, reinforcement learning, and recommendation. ICP represents a prediction as a short latent program of domain primitives (steps) and runs a propose–execute–evaluate loop until a halting condition. Concretely, a step generator proposes the next operation conditioned on the current trace; a domain executor runs the operation to update the state and exposes local progress signals; and a controller decides to continue, backtrack, or answer. Domain instantiations: in vision (e.g., VQA, referring segmentation), steps are neuro-symbolic operators over a scene graph (detect, filter[attribute], relate[left_of], count, compare); in RL, steps are options/subgoals with termination predicates; in recommendation, steps are intent hypotheses and slate-construction operations over candidates and a knowledge graph.

Training proceeds in two phases. First, a cold-start stage synthesizes interleaved reasoning–retrieval traces and backward-chained decompositions from goals into subgoals using a teacher and domain templates, converting them into step-wise supervised pairs by conditioning on the partial trace and predicting the next step or stop. Second, we align the policy with verifier-in-the-loop step rewards and state-dependent preferences: for each proposed step, the executor computes a local progress score and supplies per-step advantages; in parallel, we collect step preference data (winning vs. rejected next steps) and optimize a step-level DPO objective with a dynamic value margin that reflects the difference in estimated downstream value between the two next-states. This couples faithful, locally validated progress with credit assignment that respects future utility. Retrieval is interleaved during both stages: the policy can call out to a catalog/skill/KG retriever to acquire relations, skills, or item facets, which are fed back into the next-step context.

Inference-time planning uses guided sampling to explore a small set of diverse step programs efficiently. We learn twist/value functions that approximate the expected terminal objective from partial traces and apply twisted SMC or MCTS-style search to focus computation on promising partial plans while keeping unbiased estimates of sequence quality. A short local beam with verifier feedback filters invalid/inapplicable steps early. We then run self-consistency over K candidates and aggregate answers or slates by majority/score-weighted voting. To avoid spurious or verbose steps, a per-step information-gain gate measures whether adding a step increases predictive information about the target; low-IG steps trigger pruning, backtracking, or early stopping. The final confidence is calibrated from two factors: uncertainty (entropy over sampled outcomes) and fidelity (the measured drop in predicted utility when ablating top-contributing steps in the chosen plan), yielding a logit-free calibrated confidence.

Executors and progress signals are domain-specific but follow a shared template. Vision: a program executor maps operators to calls into detectors, segmenters, and spatial-relation checkers; the progress metric is the fraction of satisfied predicates along the program and agreement between predicted and executed traces. Retrieval brings in KG attributes/relations for ambiguous attributes, expanded via relation-centric graph sampling. RL: the executor is the simulator; steps are options that run for a short horizon with a termination check; local progress is softplus(V(s_t) − V(s_{t+1})) and shaped goal-distance reductions, with one-step look-ahead rollouts to filter inapplicable subgoals. Recommendation: the executor is a counterfactual response model that scores short-term utility and long-term value; steps hypothesize user intent facets, retrieve relation-linked item pools, and assemble slates; progress is uplift in predicted utility and constraint satisfaction (diversity, coverage) under offline-policy constraints.

Practical recipe: synthesize 1–3 high-quality traces per instance for SFT; then alternate short online verifier-in-the-loop fine-tuning with trace refresh to keep exploration on-policy. At inference, sample 5–20 candidate programs with twist-guided search and small beams, gate by information gain, and vote. Log per-step traces and executor feedback for auditability and continual improvement.
B.rationale:
Chain-of-thought helps language models by externalizing latent computation into verifiable, step-wise progress. ICP transfers this pattern by (1) making intermediate structure explicit as short programs over domain primitives, (2) ensuring each step is executable and locally validated by a domain verifier, and (3) training and decoding with mechanisms that reward informative, forward-progressing steps rather than only terminal outcomes. This reduces search complexity, combats spurious correlations, and provides dense learning signals in settings with sparse or delayed rewards.

Verifier-in-the-loop rewards and dynamic, state-dependent preference learning align the policy to steps that truly advance goals, while twist-guided search, self-consistency, and information-gain gating make inference robust and sample-efficient. Importantly, ICP does not require verbose natural language thoughts: latent, executable steps with a world-model/executor suffice, enabling practical instantiations in vision, RL, and recommendation without sacrificing faithfulness or efficiency.

The result is a general, plug-in intermediate-computation protocol: add a compact step language, an executor with local progress, step-wise alignment, and guided search. This mirrors why CoT works in LMs—structured decomposition plus verifiable progress—while respecting each domain’s constraints and tooling.

The method proposes ICP, a unified propose–execute–evaluate loop that explicitly instantiates domain-specific intermediate steps (neuro-symbolic operators for vision, options/subgoals for RL, intent hypotheses for recommendation), trains them with step-level rewards and preference learning, and decodes with guided search and information-gain gating. The mechanism directly engages the failure mode by constructing domain-appropriate "reasoning steps" (latent programs over primitives), ensuring each step is locally validated by a domain executor (analogous to verifiable CoT steps in LLMs), and providing dense intermediate supervision rather than only terminal rewards. C.method:
We propose Process Supervision Across Modalities (ProSAM): a controller–scratchpad–tool loop that makes models produce and verify intermediate computations before final predictions. A unified controller emits step types (e.g., localize, caption, check-relation; propose-skill, set-subgoal; generate-facet, retrieve, rerank) and their arguments, writes the results to a task-specific scratchpad, and invokes modality-appropriate tools (vision localizers/captioners/logic checkers; planners/skills/simulators; retrievers/rerankers/propensity estimators). Training is two-stage: (1) cold-start stepwise supervision from teacher-generated or annotated traces that interleave reasoning with tool calls; (2) process-optimized fine-tuning with step-level rewards that encourage good intermediate computations even when the final answer is unavailable or sparse.

Vision instantiation (Visual Scratchpad Reasoning): given an image and query, the controller iterates: (i) localize question-relevant regions and masks; (ii) generate concise, question-guided region captions; (iii) parse captions and detections into structured predicates; (iv) resolve conflicts such as occlusions; and (v) run a probabilistic logic layer that fuses visual and textual predicates to update answer support. A backward-chaining policy selects which predicate to verify next to close proof gaps. Step rewards include mask IoU against grounded-evidence supervision, increases in logic support for correct answers, and reductions in occlusion conflicts; the loop halts when logic confidence saturates. Faithfulness is audited by executing the intermediate logic program or consistency checks (e.g., running the same reasoning over the actually localized regions) and correlating trace faithfulness with task accuracy to guide training and early stopping.

Reinforcement learning instantiation (Planner-Only, State-Tracking Control): the controller maintains an explicit world-state scratchpad and proposes a hierarchical plan as a sequence of skills and subgoals. Before acting, it performs light look-ahead with coarse learned dynamics and success predictors to score candidate plans. Execution proceeds skill-by-skill; after each skill terminates, the world-state is updated from observations and the planner replans if needed. We optimize only the planner with process rewards that shape whole trajectories toward the goal (potential-based path feedback) and add a completeness reward that verifies whether the proposed actions cover all required steps/tools to achieve the subgoal. This decoupling improves credit assignment on long horizons while preserving optimality; look-ahead prunes low-probability branches to keep search efficient.

Recommendation/IR instantiation (Facet-Then-Retrieve–Rerank): the controller builds an explicit facet scratchpad for a query (sub-intents/entities/attributes) and alternates between: predicting the next facet to cover, writing an expansion query, retrieving with multi-view document embeddings that capture distinct evidence, and performing listwise reranking. A coverage vector over facets is updated after each iteration and used as a stopping criterion. Training uses listwise reranker orderings as supervision to update retriever/query encoders, and historical logs from multiple rankers to learn a contextual examination-bias model so that process losses (coverage, recall@k per facet) and click-derived signals are debiased. At inference, the loop stops when marginal coverage gain falls below a threshold or a budget is reached, producing both final rankings and the intermediate coverage report.

Across all domains, cold-start traces are built from available supervision: grounded masks and Q–A links for vision, demonstrations/skill libraries for RL, and query facet mining plus historic rankers for IR. Process fine-tuning uses KL-regularized objectives to stay close to cold-start behavior while improving step choices. Outputs always include the scratchpad artifacts, enabling auditing, ablations, and selective execution of only the most impactful steps under compute budgets.
C.rationale:
Chain-of-thought helps LLMs by decomposing problems into verifiable sub-steps, interleaving computation with information gathering, and enabling process-level supervision. ProSAM carries these principles to vision, RL, and recommendation by making intermediate computations first-class citizens: scratchpads record what was localized, planned, or retrieved; tools execute and check these steps; and training rewards the quality of intermediate processes, not just end answers.

In vision, explicit grounding and logic layers make spatial and relational reasoning observable and correctable, while occlusion-aware iteration addresses failure modes of one-shot feed-forward inference. In RL, separating planning from acting with trajectory-shaped and completeness rewards fixes credit assignment and improves sample efficiency on long horizons. In recommendation, factorizing relevance into facet coverage plus listwise ordering and propensity correction turns an opaque end-to-end scorer into a monitored, iteratively improving pipeline. Faithfulness audits close the loop, ensuring that better processes correlate with better outcomes and providing actionable signals for early stopping and debugging.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that vision, RL, and recommendation models lack analogous intermediate-computation mechanisms to chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, leading to opaque, one-shot predictions without verifiable sub-steps. The proposed method (ProSAM) directly addresses this by introducing a controller–scratchpad–tool loop that forces each domain to produce explicit intermediate computations: localize/caption/logic steps for vision, hierarchical planning with world-state tracking for RL, and facet decomposition with iterative retrieval for recommendation. Each instantiation maps a specific failure mode (one-shot feed-forward inference, long-horizon credit assignment, opaque end-to-end scoring) to a concrete mechanism (backward-chaining logic, potential-based process rewards, coverage-vector stopping). The causal chain from problem to method is traceable in each domain, with two-stage training (cold-start traces + process-level rewards) directly operationalizing the "intermediate supervision" principle from chain-of-thought. Minor gaps exist in how the unified controller generalizes across modalities without domain-specific retraining, but the core mechanism-to-failure-mode alignment is clear and detailed.

D.method:
We propose a cross-domain framework, Deliberate Intermediate Computation (DIC), that mirrors chain-of-thought by explicitly generating, checking, and selecting over intermediate hypotheses before committing to a final prediction. DIC has three universal stages: (1) Hypothesis generation: produce K structured intermediates tailored to the domain (e.g., visual-text consistency scores, scene/planning programs, affordance maps; policy sketches/options; recommendation rationales). (2) Cheap verification and filtration: score these intermediates with fast, domain-grounded validators (e.g., image–text agreement checks, planner feasibility, discrepancy-to-ground-truth distributions, value-model scores) and filter/reweight them. (3) Decision: select the best surviving hypothesis or aggregate them, and return both the action/answer and an optionally stored intermediate trace for auditing and training.

Vision and VLM. At inference, a light consistency head first estimates whether the text query aligns with the image. If alignment is high, a standard VLM answer is emitted; otherwise, DIC escalates to structured intermediates: (a) generate a symbolic plan scaffold (e.g., PDDL task or functional program) from the image+query, run a planner/executor to validate syntactic correctness and plan existence, and use the executable plan to produce the answer; and/or (b) for manipulation or affordance-style tasks, predict contact-point heatmaps and post-contact trajectories as the intermediate, then score them with simple geometric/physics checks. Among K candidate scaffolds, a verifier ranks and filters infeasible or text-biased candidates, and the final answer is taken from the highest-scoring feasible intermediate. Training uses multitask loss: supervised targets for scaffolds (when available), contrastive/auxiliary losses for image–text alignment, and standard task loss on the final answer; gradients do not flow through external planners, but their scores supervise the filtration head.

Reinforcement learning and robotics. DIC adds an option-sketch generator that samples K diverse, short-horizon policy sketches via a latent z and a controllable diversity mapping f(s) to focus diversity where useful (e.g., gait patterns, contact timing). Each sketch is rolled out briefly or evaluated with a value/constraint model; a filtration module (rank-based or threshold/reweighted by predicted return magnitude) keeps only high-quality, low-noise candidates. For language-conditioned or long-horizon tasks, an additional symbolic plan (e.g., PDDL) can be generated and checked for feasibility before execution, serving as an extra gate. Policy improvement uses the filtered set to update with PPO-style objectives and reward reweighting; at deployment, the same generate–filter–select loop executes the best validated sketch, improving reliability under noisy rewards and distribution shift.

Recommendation. DIC wraps standard two-stage retrieval/ranking with budgeted, rationale-driven reranking only when needed. A base ranker proposes a slate; a discrepancy head computes a per-session hardness score by measuring Earth-Mover (Wasserstein) distance between the model’s predicted click distribution and observed feedback patterns (or uncertainty proxies). If the slate is easy, we return the base ranking; otherwise, we invoke a rationalizing reranker that jointly predicts fine-grained relevance and generates short, structured rationales/aspect attributions for each item. This reranker is trained on high-quality supervision built by multiple-pass scoring with consensus selection and diverse hard negatives, yielding stable, fine-grained labels. Online, only hard slates/items are reranked; the final slate and optional rationales are emitted, and the discrepancy signal feeds continual adaptation.

Across domains, DIC is compute-aware: the light validator gates escalation to heavy intermediates, and K (number of hypotheses) is tuned to a latency budget. Stored intermediates enable self-diagnostics (e.g., plan failed syntax, contact map inconsistent with geometry) and targeted retraining where validators frequently reject hypotheses.
D.rationale:
Chain-of-thought boosts LLMs by externalizing latent computation into verifiable intermediate steps, then selecting a consistent answer. DIC instantiates the same principle for vision, RL, and recommendation by: (i) factoring predictions into explicit, domain-meaningful intermediates (plans, affordances, policy sketches, rationales), (ii) introducing cheap, targeted validators that can reject or downweight unreliable hypotheses, and (iii) allocating extra compute only to hard cases. This turns black-box end-to-end inference into a generate–check–decide loop that reduces ungrounded shortcuts (e.g., text bias in VLMs), dampens reward-model noise in RL, and improves fine-grained ranking on difficult recommendation slates.

Each domain component borrows a mechanism proven effective in its native setting—symbolic planning validation and affordance decomposition for vision/robotics, filtration and controlled diversity for RL, and consensus-labeled, rationale-aware reranking plus discrepancy-triggered selection for recommendation—and repurposes it as the intermediate-computation scaffold. The result is higher accuracy and robustness with interpretable traces, while keeping overhead bounded via selective escalation.

query_text:
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?

A.method:
We propose a streaming, tiered memory architecture that decouples long-term storage from token-to-token attention and constrains compute by design. The model processes inputs (text, video, or mixed) in fixed-size segments with local, block-sparse attention for short-range reasoning. In parallel, a learned event encoder detects salient changes and compresses each segment into a small set of event slots (semantic vectors, lightweight textual/visual summaries, and pointers to the raw segment). These slots are written to a two-tier external store: a fast, small episodic buffer that keeps recent high-resolution slots, and a slower, larger semantic store that holds consolidated, deduplicated, and clustered summaries. Both tiers are indexed for approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval over multi-view keys (content, entities, time, modality).

At write time, a compute-aware controller enforces strict budgets. A salience/surprise module (trained to predict future loss deltas) scores candidate events; only the top few are written per segment. Periodically, a consolidation pass merges redundant slots, promotes persistent threads (entities, storylines, goals) to the semantic store, and attaches cross-references (who/what/where/when) plus minisketches that allow quick contradiction checks. Aging and reinforcement control forgetting: slots decay unless re-retrieved or boosted by downstream utility signals, keeping memory compact without catastrophic loss of rare-but-crucial facts.

At read time, every transformer block emits a small number of retrieval queries distilled from its hidden states (and, for video, from object/scene trackers). An ANN index returns top-k relevant slots from the episodic/semantic stores under a fixed read budget. The model performs cross-attention only over these few slots, not over the full past, and uses a learned gate to fuse memory with local context. A lightweight verifier compares memory claims against current evidence and, if uncertainty remains, can spend a small extra budget to fetch the raw segment referenced by a slot. This produces an O(window + k) per-step attention cost while enabling access to arbitrarily distant information via constant-size reads.

Training couples language/vision modeling with memory objectives. We apply masked or span-denoising losses that force dependence on distant information placed outside the local window; success requires correct retrieval. A contrastive loss sharpens keys so that slots compete against hard negatives from similar contexts. Distillation aligns memory-augmented predictions with a more expensive teacher that occasionally attends globally on short excerpts. Read/write policies are optimized under a FLOPs-aware utility objective (accuracy minus compute cost), using differentiable top-k approximations with straight-through estimators, and an information-bottleneck regularizer to encourage concise slots. Evaluation tracks long-range QA accuracy, temporal consistency, contradiction rate, retrieval precision/recall, and compute (FLOPs and latency) across long documents, hour-scale videos, and multi-episode tasks.
A.rationale:
This method maintains effective distant memory by externalizing long-term information into compact, searchable slots and restricting the core model to inexpensive local attention. Retrieval brings only a handful of highly relevant summaries into the computational spotlight, preventing quadratic growth while preserving access to far-away facts. Event-driven compression reduces redundancy and encodes the right temporal abstractions for videos, documents, and interactive episodes.

Forgetting is addressed through consolidation, reinforcement by downstream utility, and a verifier that can re-open the raw context when summaries are insufficient, yielding both robustness and bounded compute. The training objectives ensure that the model learns to write what will later be useful and to read only when it improves predictions, creating an end-to-end system that scales to very long contexts with near-constant memory access cost.

However, none of these papers combine all the key mechanisms proposed here: (1) event-driven salience/surprise scoring for selective writes, (2) a two-tier episodic+semantic external store with consolidation, deduplication, and aging/RL-based forgetting, (3) per-transformer-block retrieval queries with a learned gate and a verifier that can re-open raw segments, (4) multi-view ANN indexing (content, entities, time, modality), and (5) a FLOPs-aware utility objective with information-bottleneck regularization and contrastive key sharpening — all trained end-to-end for long documents, videos, and episodes. validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is twofold: (a) quadratic attention cost as context grows, and (b) forgetting of distant information. The method directly addresses both: quadratic cost is resolved by restricting core attention to fixed local windows (block-sparse, O(window)) and replacing full-context attention with O(k) cross-attention over retrieved slots from an external store, making per-step cost O(window + k) regardless of total context length. Forgetting is addressed by the tiered external memory (episodic + semantic stores), salience-driven write policies, consolidation passes, and reinforcement-based slot retention — ensuring distant but important information persists and remains retrievable. The causal chain from problem to mechanism is traceable at each step: local attention eliminates quadratic scaling, ANN retrieval provides access to distant information at constant cost, and the memory management pipeline (write budgets, consolidation, aging) prevents both overflow and catastrophic forgetting. There are no significant gaps; the method is detailed and each component maps onto a specific aspect of the failure mode.

B.method:
Segmented Sparse Recurrent Memory (SSRM). Stream the input (video frames, long documents, or dialogue turns) and first segment it into coherent units that share intent/topic/state. For text and dialogue, run a lightweight, structured segmentation-and-state tracker that emits typed slots (intent, entities, constraints, temporal markers); for video, detect shots/events and attach compact attributes (actors, actions, scene, timestamps). For each segment, build a compact memory unit composed of: (i) a semantic key produced by rewriting the segment into a short, skill/event/constraint description, and (ii) a value that concatenates a single “event token” embedding of the segment’s core content with the small set of slot/value fields. Maintain a dual-store memory: a small, fast short-term cache for the recent past and a longer-term store for distant segments.

Use a selective write policy to control memory growth and avoid redundancy. Within each new segment, score candidate facts/events for salience and novelty; only write items that change the tracked state or that will likely be needed later (e.g., deadlines, foreshadowed entities, unresolved references). Periodically perform rehearsal-based consolidation: prompt the model to self-synthesize distilled summaries of older clusters (e.g., “one-paragraph recap of constraints and unresolved references for segments S1…Sk”), then replace multiple fine-grained items with the distilled summary when it preserves answerability on held-out probes. Choose what to keep via diversity-aware selection (k-means over keys) so the long-term store remains small but covers distinct themes.

Adopt a sparse read path and near-linear sequence backbone. Process the current step with local attention over only a short sliding window, augmented by a lightweight recurrent state that summarizes everything seen so far. Before each layer, run a tiny predictor on the current hidden state to pre-score memory keys and prefetch only the top-k memory units for cross-attention; skip loading the rest. Within the local window itself, progressively prune low-utility tokens layer-by-layer using an attentivity score to the current query/start token, so deeper layers operate on a much shorter token set. This combination turns the quadratic self-attention on long inputs into: local attention over a pruned window, cross-attention to O(k) retrieved memories, and an O(d) recurrent update—yielding near-linear compute and memory.

Prevent forgetting via access-driven consolidation and prioritized refresh. Track accesses and estimated loss impact for each memory unit; when budget pressure arises, retain items that either (a) are frequently read or (b) would most hurt model loss if removed, and merge or evict others via rehearsal distillation. Separate the short-term cache from the long-term store so online updates don’t destabilize well-validated distant memories; periodically “mixed-train” the predictor and pruning thresholds by replaying a small buffer of high-impact long-term items alongside fresh segments, without touching the frozen base model. In deployment, this yields a steady-state loop: segment and write sparsely, rehearse to condense, read via predictor-gated retrieval plus token pruning, and carry global context through the recurrent state—maintaining effective access to distant information without exhausting compute.
B.rationale:
The core cost reducer is replacing global quadratic attention with a triad: (1) a recurrent state that carries long-horizon summaries at O(Td), (2) local attention on a short, progressively pruned window, and (3) cross-attention only to a handful of predictor-selected memory units. This keeps per-step compute close to linear while preserving access to distant details through structured, compact memory values and a reliable read gate. The predictor avoids loading irrelevant memories, mirroring selective activation ideas that cut bandwidth/compute.

Forgetting is addressed by writing only state-changing facts into memory, separating short- and long-term stores to avoid drift, and periodically distilling older clusters via self-synthesized rehearsal so essential long-range information survives under a tight memory budget. Diversity-aware selection prevents the store from filling with near-duplicates, and impact-based retention keeps units that matter for loss and answerability. Finally, by keying memory on rewritten skill/event descriptors and segmenting the stream into typed states, retrieval remains targeted and robust, mitigating the retrieval-induced degradations seen with naive long-context augmentation.

C.method:
We propose a query-guided, multi-granular indexed memory (MiG-IM) that replaces quadratic full-context attention with a compact, searchable memory organized at three levels: (1) persistent entity/topic slots that summarize long-range structure, (2) a core-token cache that stores only the most influential tokens from each window along with their positional indices, and (3) a multi-vector segment index that enables fast approximate retrieval of relevant windows. A lightweight streaming writer compresses each incoming window (frames, text chunks, or trajectory steps) into these memories under a fixed token budget; a reader then pulls only the subset needed for the current query/token, injecting them as cross-attention keys/values or prefix KV-caches.

Writer. For each new window, we first compute a cheap salience map using the model’s own cross-attention with gradient-based attribution to the current goal or next-token state; we keep only top-m core tokens and their positions for the cache, optionally preceded by core-frame filtering in video to remove redundancy. In parallel, we maintain a small bank of entity/topic slots (e.g., 16–64) updated online: we align prior slots to the current window (ego-motion warping for video; anchor-position alignment for text), then update each slot with an uncertainty-guided EMA so that high-uncertainty observations adjust slots more, while a heavy-tailed aggregator suppresses outliers. Capacity is controlled by a FIFO/TTL policy and conflict-aware merging when slots are similar.

Indexer. Each window also produces k viewer embeddings by prepending learned viewer tokens and pooling their hidden states. These k embeddings are stored in an ANN index and point to the window’s slots and core-token cache. Because each segment contributes multiple views, the index remains ANN-friendly while covering diverse semantics without late interaction.

Reader. At inference (or during training with long inputs), we compute a compact query embedding from the current hidden state/prompt and retrieve top-K segments via the ANN index. We then perform two-stage sparse attention: (a) slot routing, where the model attends to retrieved entity/topic slots to estimate which segments/entities matter; (b) token zoom-in, which uses the routing weights plus gradient-based relevance to select a small budget (e.g., 128–512) of core tokens across those segments. Only these tokens are materialized as keys/values and concatenated to the current layer’s memory for cross-attention. For videos, slot alignment (warping) keeps distant evidence coherent across time; for text, stored positions preserve long-range ordering so the model can reconstruct dependencies.

Training. We train the writer and reader jointly with three signals: (1) retrieval contrastive loss that aligns query embeddings to their originating viewer embeddings; (2) masked-span/next-token objectives conditioned only on the retrieved memory to pressure the system to keep and fetch the right evidence; and (3) a distillation term from a short-context full-attention teacher to stabilize optimization. The selection steps use differentiable relaxations (e.g., Gumbel-top-k) during training and hard top-k at inference. Budgets for slots, core tokens, and retrieved segments are fixed, making compute predictable and near-linear in current window size plus a small constant memory cost.
C.rationale:
Quadratic attention is avoided because the model never attends over the full past; it retrieves a tiny, query-relevant subset. Token-level summarization stores only high-utility tokens with their positions, preserving the structure needed for long-range reasoning without the cost of replaying full sequences. Entity/topic slots provide a persistent, coarser memory that resists forgetting by carrying forward aligned summaries across many windows; uncertainty-aware, robust updates keep this memory informative yet stable.

Fast multi-vector indexing ensures we search long histories efficiently and select only segments likely to matter, while two-stage attention (slot routing then token zoom-in) concentrates compute where it pays off. Temporal/positional alignment of memory prevents drift so distant evidence remains usable. Together, these components maintain effective recall of long-range information under strict compute budgets, and the training objectives couple storage, retrieval, and usage so the memory learns to support downstream tasks directly.

D.method:
We propose Sparse State-Augmented Memory (SSAM), a chunked encoder with a small, persistent external memory and a hierarchical sparse-attention graph that keeps global context accessible at near-linear cost. Inputs (tokens for documents, clips for videos, steps for episodes) are partitioned into fixed-size windows. Each window is encoded with a parallel local–global block: a linear-time local branch (cgMLP or depth-wise conv) captures fine structure, while a restricted self-attention operates only within the window to preserve short-range dependencies. The windows emit a few summary tokens (centroids) that serve as nodes in a global sparse graph. Instead of full quadratic attention, SSAM constructs a KNN graph over these centroids and a compact bank of persistent memory slots, and performs attention only along these edges, enabling long-range propagation with O(N) edges per layer.

Memory writing is selective and causal. After encoding a window, candidate writes are formed by projecting its summaries into key–value pairs. A rationale scorer, conditioned on the current task/query embedding, assigns importance to each candidate and to existing memory slots using relation-aware keys; scores are normalized across the neighbors of each semantic head so that common or recent topics do not dominate by default. Only the top-K candidates per head update memory via a gated write (e.g., GRU-like interpolation); other candidates are softly merged into their closest existing slots to prevent memory bloat. For streaming inputs, updates occur at discrete intervals, and each update uses only evidence within the current interval to maintain strict causality.

Reading from memory is sparse and token-aware. At inference time, the model first routes each query/token to a small set of relevant memory slots via the KNN graph; a lightweight controller then produces token- and time-specific masks that reweight cross-attention to those slots, amplifying crucial distant evidence and suppressing distractors. The encoder also applies a very low-cost inter-window mixing step (depth-wise convolution over reassembled windows) that diffuses information across adjacent windows without triggering global attention, further reducing the need for expensive long-range interactions.

To avoid forgetting while bounding compute, SSAM performs periodic memory consolidation and protection. Older slots are clustered and distilled into compact summaries with self-supervised alignment losses, while slots with consistently high rationale scores are pinned (no overwrite) for a configurable horizon. A replay buffer of a few recent, mid-term, and protected slots is always exposed to the reader, ensuring that distant but salient facts remain retrievable. Overall complexity scales as O(N·w + N·K + M·K), where w is window size, K is the number of sparse edges per node, and M is the memory size, keeping compute near-linear in sequence length while preserving access to long-range content.
D.rationale:
SSAM targets both sources of failure in long-context modeling: quadratic attention cost and recency bias. By confining full self-attention to small windows, using depth-wise conv for cheap cross-window diffusion, and replacing global dense attention with a KNN graph over window centroids and a compact memory, the method avoids quadratic blow-ups while still enabling information to traverse long distances. The persistent memory and rationale-weighted writing prevent important but distant content from being overwritten by recent context; token-aware masked cross-attention ensures that only a handful of highly relevant slots are consulted per query, preserving bandwidth and improving robustness.

The causal, discretely-online update rule makes SSAM suitable for videos and embodied episodes, while consolidation and pinning mitigate catastrophic forgetting without unbounded growth. Together, these components provide a principled way to maintain effective access to distant information under tight compute budgets across modalities.

query_text:
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?

A.method:
Propose a Budgeted Hierarchical Conditional Computation (BHCC) architecture that activates only a small, relevant subnetwork per input (and per token). Start from a standard backbone (e.g., Transformer). Replace each dense feed-forward block with a bank of experts grouped into a few coarse banks. Attach a two-stage router: (1) a coarse, sequence-level gate that selects one bank using a cheap summary (e.g., mean/max-pooled hidden state plus task metadata), and (2) a fine, token-level gate that routes each token to its top-k experts within the chosen bank. The router is a tiny MLP with low-rank projections and temperature-controlled Gumbel-Top-k during training; at inference it becomes hard top-k with capacity limits per expert. To keep routing overhead negligible, compute the coarse gate once per layer per sequence, and the fine gate as a single linear projection followed by a sparsifying top-k.

Train the network end-to-end with four coupled objectives: (a) the primary task loss; (b) a differentiable compute-budget regularizer that penalizes the expected FLOPs activated per sample toward a target budget (computed from the router’s soft assignment under Gumbel-Softmax); (c) a load-balancing loss to distribute tokens across experts and avoid expert collapse; and (d) distillation from a strong dense teacher at both logits and selected intermediate features to preserve accuracy under sparsity. Use a curriculum that gradually lowers the FLOPs target and increases routing temperature sharpness, plus expert dropout and stochastic capacity to encourage redundancy and robustness.

At inference, enforce strict top-k per token and per-layer capacity to bound latency, with an overflow policy that routes excess tokens to the next-best expert or to a shared fallback expert. Implement block-sparse execution by packing parameters of each expert contiguously and batching tokens per expert into fused matmuls; this yields high utilization with gather/scatter primitives. Keep the router in reduced precision and cache its coarse decisions across micro-batches with similar context. Quantize expert weights (e.g., int8) and use static kernel variants per (k, capacity) to eliminate dynamic-shape overhead.

Augment width sparsity with dynamic depth via risk-controlled early exit. Attach a tiny halting head after selected layers that predicts calibrated correctness risk from the current hidden state. If the predicted risk is below a target threshold, terminate and skip remaining layers; otherwise continue. Calibrate thresholds on a held-out set to meet a desired accuracy target, and include a last-layer “panic” re-evaluation path for low-confidence cases. Combined, hierarchical routing (which experts) and early exit (how many layers) deliver conditional activation across both width and depth.
A.rationale:
Hierarchical routing ensures the router’s overhead stays small while focusing compute where it matters: a cheap coarse gate narrows the candidate experts, and a lightweight token-level gate activates only a few experts per token. The compute-budget regularizer turns sparsity into an explicit optimization target, so the model learns to meet a FLOPs cap rather than relying on ad hoc pruning. Load balancing prevents expert starvation and stabilizes training, while distillation transfers competence from a dense teacher, maintaining accuracy despite aggressive sparsity.

Block-sparse execution, capacity constraints, and static kernels translate the conditional structure into real latency gains on hardware, addressing the practical gap between theoretical and realized speedups. Early exit adds dynamic depth control: many inputs need only shallow processing, so halting reduces compute further without sacrificing reliability thanks to calibrated risk thresholds. Together, these components conditionally activate only relevant subnetworks at inference, cutting compute while preserving quality.

MultiPL-MoE uses token-level and segment-level routing but for a different purpose. The combination of hierarchical two-stage MoE routing with explicit FLOPs budget regularization and calibrated early exit represents a non-trivial combination with no direct end-to-end match in the literature, matching the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with individual mechanisms found separately but not their full combination.

The method directly addresses this through hierarchical conditional computation: a two-stage router (coarse bank selection + fine token-level top-k expert routing) activates only a small subnetwork per input/token, with a compute-budget regularizer explicitly penalizing excess FLOPs. The causal chain is traceable — sparse routing reduces activated parameters, the budget regularizer enforces a FLOPs target, block-sparse execution realizes actual latency gains, and early exit adds depth-wise conditional computation. The mechanism engages every dimension of the failure mode (width via MoE routing, depth via early exit, hardware realization via block-sparse kernels).
