There are no significant gaps; each component maps directly onto a specific aspect of the failure mode.

C.method:
We propose a two-stage pipeline that first discovers shortcut features at the concept level and then synthesizes calibrated counterfactual pairs that surgically break these shortcuts while preserving the causal evidence for the label. Stage A (discover and localize) annotates each example with candidate non-causal concepts (e.g., background, author, brand, demographic, color) using an automatic concept tagger; we quantify shortcut risk per concept using a concept-conditioned performance gap (e.g., Bias@C: accuracy with vs. without the concept across labels) and a dependency score that measures how much the model’s predictive distribution changes when the concept is masked or neutralized. To avoid conflating true evidence with nuisance context, we run one-at-a-time, minimally invasive interventions: mask or neutralize a single concept region/span and test whether the ground-truth label remains entailed while the model’s prediction changes—mark such concepts as nuisance shortcuts and record their spatial/textual extents via grounding tools (e.g., segmentation for images or span tagging for text) so we can target them precisely.

Stage B (generate calibrated counterfactuals) produces, for each (example x, shortcut concept c), two kinds of edits. (1) Label-preserving break: we preserve the localized causal evidence while perturbing only the shortcut concept region/span c using targeted transformations—e.g., for images, segment c and replace with randomized textures, non-correlated backgrounds, or attribute swaps; for text, rewrite to remove or invert c while maintaining the core semantics that determine the label. We calibrate perturbation strength per concept by selecting transformation parameters so that the model’s logits shift by a target KL distance from the original, ensuring consistent, minimally sufficient edits that do not over-perturb semantics. (2) Dispreferred foil: we ablate or corrupt the minimal causal evidence region while leaving the shortcut intact, creating a hard negative that should not receive the ground-truth label (or is explicitly marked unanswerable). Both edits are accepted only if they pass automatic checks: semantic similarity to x stays high for label-preserving breaks; foils reduce evidence coverage while maintaining shortcut presence.

We train with a multi-term objective on the augmented dataset. A standard supervised loss is applied to originals and label-preserving breaks (same label). An invariance loss encourages the model to produce similar predictions and intermediate representations for x and its label-preserving break (e.g., symmetric KL on outputs plus a contrastive pull in representation space). A preference loss ranks originals and label-preserving breaks above their foils, teaching the model that shortcut-only inputs are dispreferred even when they are superficially plausible. Loss terms are weighted by the dependency score so examples where the model relied most on the shortcut receive stronger invariance and preference signals. This focuses capacity where spurious reliance is greatest while avoiding over-regularizing clean cases.

In practice, we bootstrap discovery and localization with weak supervision: concept tags from an LLM for text, region proposals/segmentations for vision, and question–evidence links where available. For visual domains without ground-truth evidence, we use automatic segmenters to mask backgrounds or co-occurring objects as initial shortcut candidates and refine them with the one-at-a-time intervention test. When multiple data acquisition pipelines or preprocessors exist (e.g., alternate crops, styles, or sensors), we harvest natural counterfactual pairs by finding the same content that appears with and without the shortcut across versions and add them to Stage B. We evaluate robustness by (i) the drop in Bias@C across identified concepts, (ii) accuracy under controlled interventions that toggle shortcuts, and (iii) reduced model dependence on shortcuts as measured by the dependency score on held-out data.
C.rationale:
Shortcut reliance persists when models can explain labels using non-causal features that co-occur with true evidence. By first measuring concept-level bias and model dependence under one-at-a-time interventions, we separate nuisance context from causal evidence and precisely localize what to edit. Generating label-preserving counterfactuals that destroy only the shortcut removes the crutch while keeping the task signal intact; pairing these with foils that remove evidence but keep the shortcut provides a clear contrast that teaches the model the direction of preference: evidence, not shortcut, should drive predictions.

Calibrating perturbation strength by a target KL shift avoids brittle or overly strong edits and makes counterfactuals comparable across concepts and inputs. Weighting the invariance and preference losses by the measured dependence concentrates learning where it matters most. Together, these elements produce a training signal that explicitly penalizes shortcut use and rewards invariance to non-causal features, improving interventional robustness without requiring exhaustive manual annotations.

C.paraphrase_essence: When training data exhibits spurious correlations, models adopt non-causal shortcuts, so predictions fail under interventions or distribution shifts that break these shortcuts.

D.method:
We propose a counterfactual data generation and training pipeline that explicitly targets shortcut features while keeping causal evidence fixed. For each task, we first enumerate candidate non‑causal attributes (e.g., style, background, named entities, surface forms, textures) using simple schema/metadata and lightweight probes. We then attach to every original example a set of minimally edited counterfactuals that alter only these non‑causal attributes while preserving the causal content and label. The pipeline offers modality‑specific generators: for text, we apply composable semantic edits (tense/voice, argument swaps, keyword deletion/expansion, name/institution substitutions) under constraints that keep the gold label unchanged; for images, we use diffusion inpainting/editing with prompts that pin the object/class tokens while varying context/style/background; for tabular data, we conditionally resample or swap the identified spurious fields from matched strata so that the target remains label‑consistent.

To ensure counterfactuals truly break shortcuts rather than introduce new artifacts, we score each generated example with a graded reward and a model‑centric hardness signal. The graded reward aggregates: (i) label‑consistency under a rule/teacher check, (ii) the measured change in the targeted spurious attribute(s), and (iii) realism/fluency. In parallel, a lightweight score net co‑trained with the target model on frozen features outputs a per‑sample weight that reflects training dynamics (harder samples receive lower weights that translate into higher difficulty). We combine the two into a single counterfactual value used for both selection and loss reweighting, preferentially sampling high‑quality, high‑impact counterfactuals that most strongly expose shortcut reliance.

Training couples standard supervised loss with an invariance objective across the counterfactual group of each example. Concretely, for every anchor and its counterfactuals sharing the same causal label, we add: (a) a consistency term that brings logits or a designated representation layer into agreement across the group; (b) a group‑robust reweighting that emphasizes the worst‑performing environment among the group; and (c) a contrastive margin that pushes apart representations of different labels. We implement this with a two‑phase schedule: first, optimize label accuracy alongside distribution‑level agreement across counterfactual sets to stabilize the causal decision boundary; second, continue sampling diverse counterfactuals of the same anchor and maintain the consistency loss while allowing wide variation along the spurious axes, encouraging invariance without sacrificing coverage.

The procedure runs iteratively. After each training epoch, we regenerate or expand counterfactuals for anchors whose predictions remain sensitive to spurious edits, refresh the graded rewards, and update the score net from recent training dynamics. This closes the loop: early stages emphasize broad, easy edits to carve out obvious shortcuts; later stages concentrate on hard, model‑specific failure modes. For large, already‑deployed models, the same pipeline can drive targeted fine‑tuning on a compact, high‑value counterfactual set to reduce specific shortcut failures without regressing overall performance.
D.rationale:
Shortcuts persist because training data entangle causal and non‑causal features; models latch onto the easiest signals. By generating counterfactuals that surgically vary only the non‑causal attributes while preserving the causal evidence and label, we create controlled environments where any change in prediction directly indicates shortcut reliance. Scoring counterfactuals with both a graded quality/relevance reward and a model‑derived hardness metric prioritizes the most informative examples, improving data efficiency and focusing learning on invariances that matter.

The invariance objective then operationalizes the causal assumption: within each counterfactual group sharing the same causal label, predictions and key representations should be stable despite wide variation along spurious axes. A two‑phase schedule disentangles stabilization of the decision boundary from broadening spurious diversity, preventing the usual accuracy–diversity trade‑off. Iterating the generate–score–train loop uses the model itself as a filter, progressively discovering and neutralizing its own shortcuts, and targeted fine‑tuning enables practical mitigation on large models with limited compute and data.

query_text:
Deep models are overconfident even when wrong, making
downstream decisions unreliable; how can we calibrate
predicted probabilities to reflect actual likelihood,
especially under distribution shift?

A.method:
We propose Shift-Aware Adaptive Calibration (SAAC), a post-hoc, per-input calibration pipeline that combines importance-weighted learning, dynamic temperature-and-bias correction, and a conformal wrapper for coverage under distribution shift. Train the task model as usual, then freeze it and construct a small calibration head that ingests the model's logits and a few shift indicators (e.g., softmax entropy, maximum probability, last-layer features, Mahalanobis distance to training features, and optionally ensemble variance if available). The head outputs a nonnegative temperature T(x) and a classwise bias vector b(x), producing calibrated probabilities via softmax((logits + b(x)) / T(x)). To capture heterogeneity in shifts, use a mixture-of-experts variant: K small calibrators with a gating network that mixes their outputs based on the same shift indicators.

Estimate target-aware importance weights without target labels. Train a domain discriminator in the model's representation space to distinguish source validation data from unlabeled target samples. Convert the discriminator scores into density-ratio estimates w(x) approximating p_target(x)/p_source(x). Fit the calibrator (and, if used, the gating network) by minimizing a weighted objective on the labeled source validation set that approximates the target risk: weighted negative log-likelihood plus a differentiable soft-ECE or Brier regularizer, with mild monotonicity constraints to discourage rank inversions. To address class-prior shift, estimate target class priors via a confusion-matrix-based or EM-style adjustment and add a log-prior offset to logits before calibration.

If a small labeling budget is available in the target domain, perform a one-shot refinement. Select points to label by maximizing an acquisition score that combines domain weight w(x), high predicted confidence, and large calibration residuals on a proxy metric (e.g., disagreement between global temperature scaling and the dynamic calibrator). Fine-tune the calibrator on this tiny labeled target set together with the weighted source validation set, using early stopping and weight decay to prevent overfitting.

Wrap the probabilistic outputs with a weighted split-conformal layer to obtain finite-sample coverage guarantees under covariate shift. Split the source validation set into fit and calibration folds, reuse the density-ratio weights, and compute a global or class-conditional scaling factor that minimally perturbs the calibrated probabilities so that weighted empirical coverage matches the desired level. For classification, implement a Mondrian (class-conditional) scheme: adjust scores so that the induced prediction sets achieve target-weighted coverage per class; for point probabilities, apply a lightweight odds-scaling or Venn-Abers-style monotone map fit on the calibration fold with weights. Expose both point probabilities and, when needed, a calibrated prediction set at a user-specified risk level.

Deploy with online adaptation. Continuously update the domain discriminator on a stream of unlabeled target data and periodically re-estimate weights and the conformal scaling factors; refresh the calibrator only if drift metrics (e.g., weighted ECE on a small rolling labeled buffer or unsupervised proxies) breach thresholds. Provide a calibration report each epoch that includes weighted ECE, classwise ECE, Brier score, NLL, and conformal coverage, enabling downstream systems to trust probabilities or switch to prediction sets when shifts intensify.
A.rationale:
Overconfidence under shift arises because post-hoc calibrators trained on in-domain data optimize the wrong distributional objective. SAAC targets the right objective by reweighting the source validation loss to approximate target-domain risk using density-ratio estimates from a domain discriminator in representation space. The dynamic, per-input temperature-and-bias correction leverages shift indicators to correct confidence adaptively, overcoming the limitations of a single global temperature and handling heterogeneous subpopulations via a mixture-of-experts.

Even with careful weighting, finite-sample effects persist and shift can be misestimated. The conformal wrapper adds a distribution-agnostic safety net: weighted split-conformal and class-conditional (Mondrian) calibration provide finite-sample coverage guarantees under covariate shift, letting users choose between calibrated point probabilities and validated prediction sets. Optional few-shot labeled refinement and online updates close the loop when limited target labels appear or drift evolves, keeping probabilities reliable for downstream decisions.

novelty.reasoning: The searches reveal that individual components of SAAC are known: importance-weighted conformal prediction under covariate shift is well-established (multiple 2025 papers), input-dependent temperature scaling exists (fault diagnosis paper), and density-ratio-based domain adaptation is standard. However, no paper surfaces the specific end-to-end pipeline combining (1) a learned per-input temperature+bias calibration head with shift indicators, (2) a mixture-of-experts gating over calibrators, (3) domain-discriminator-derived importance weights used to train the calibration head via weighted NLL+soft-ECE, (4) class-prior EM adjustment, (5) active few-shot refinement with a combined acquisition score, and (6) a Mondrian weighted split-conformal wrapper — all integrated into one post-hoc calibration system with online adaptation. The combination of mechanisms (particularly the MoE calibrator trained with discriminator-derived weights + conformal wrapper + active labeling acquisition) has no direct published match. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines multiple known mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing named design choices (MoE calibrator with shift-indicator gating, discriminator-weighted soft-ECE training objective) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is overconfidence under distribution shift — calibrators trained on source data optimize the wrong objective and produce unreliable probabilities when the test distribution differs. The method directly engages this failure mode through multiple traceable mechanisms: (a) importance-weighted calibration using density-ratio estimates to approximate target-domain risk from source validation data, (b) per-input dynamic temperature and bias correction using shift indicators (entropy, Mahalanobis distance, etc.) to handle heterogeneous shifts, (c) a conformal wrapper with density-ratio reweighting to provide finite-sample coverage guarantees under covariate shift, and (d) online adaptation to track evolving drift. Each mechanism causally addresses a specific aspect of the failure mode — the weighting corrects the distributional mismatch in the training objective, the dynamic head handles input-level heterogeneity, and the conformal layer provides guarantees when estimation is imperfect. The causal chain from problem to solution is traceable at each step with minimal gaps.

B.method:
Shift-Aware Confidence Calibration (SACC) is a plug-in pipeline that yields reliable probabilities both in-distribution and under distribution shift, for both white-box neural models (with logits) and black-box LLMs (without logits). It has three components: (A) build a strong base post-hoc calibrator on a small labeled set, (B) make calibration shift-adaptive via routing to per-shift calibrators and selective aggregation, and (C) continually maintain calibration online as the environment drifts.

A. Base post-hoc calibrators. For models with logits, fit a simple parametric calibrator (temperature or vector scaling) by minimizing NLL on a held-out calibration split, and back it with a monotone nonparametric layer (isotonic regression) mapping the model’s top-1 score to empirical correctness. Concretely, compute the model score s(x)=max_k softmax(z(x))_k (or the logit margin), then learn an isotonic function g such that p_calib(x)=g(s(x)) is well aligned to observed accuracy. For multi-class probability vectors, optionally apply vector/Dirichlet scaling first, then finalize with an isotonic map on the top-1 confidence to correct residual miscalibration. For black-box LLM classification (e.g., multiple-choice QA) where logits are unavailable, compute a logits-free confidence score using a Uncertainty–Fidelity decomposition: estimate question uncertainty from the entropy of K sampled answers, and elicit per-option fidelity via targeted counterfactual prompts that probe how strongly the model stands by each choice; combine them into a scalar confidence score, then pass this score through the same isotonic mapping learned on a small labeled set to output calibrated probabilities.

B. Shift-aware routing and selective aggregation. Train a lightweight shift detector on penultimate-layer embeddings (or sentence embeddings for LLM tasks) to produce a soft assignment over C shift clusters discovered from historical and synthetic OOD streams. Maintain a separate calibrator per cluster (same forms as in A). At inference, given x, obtain routing weights r_c(x) from the shift detector and compute each cluster’s calibrated output p_c(x). Aggregate via a confidence-aware selector: learn a small meta-aggregator that outputs mixture weights w_c(x) using features such as r_c(x), each calibrator’s self-reported confidence p_c,max(x), and their recent rolling reliability (e.g., windowed Brier score/ECE on feedback). The final probability is p(x)=∑_c w_c(x)·p_c(x). When the aggregator is uncertain or detects low fidelity across all experts, trigger selective abstention or escalate for labeling. This selective, confidence-aware mixture prevents a mis-specified single calibrator from dominating under shift.

C. Continual recalibration under drift. In deployment, collect sparse labels or delayed outcomes. Maintain a small replay buffer per shift cluster that preferentially stores recent calibration errors. Periodically (e.g., nightly or when ECE exceeds a threshold), update only the calibrators and the meta-aggregator on these buffered examples, leaving the base predictor frozen to avoid catastrophic forgetting. Use streaming-friendly isotonic updates or refit with early stopping, and re-estimate per-cluster temperature/vector scaling. The shift detector can be refreshed using a dynamic OOD stream generator that blends in-distribution with evolving OOD clusters to keep routing stable. Track time-resolved metrics (ECE, Brier score, NLL) overall and per cluster, plus an overall effectiveness criterion combining instant fixes and retention, to decide when to widen abstention or request more labels.

Evaluation and deployment notes. Evaluate on held-out ID and diverse OOD benchmarks; report reliability diagrams, overall ECE and per-cluster ECE, and coverage–risk trade-offs under selective prediction. For LLM MCQA, use K≈20–50 samples to stabilize uncertainty estimates and a fixed fidelity chain template; verify robustness across decoding temperatures. In high-stakes settings, set guardrails: minimum confidence for action, calibrated risk-based triage, and automatic re-calibration triggers when rolling ECE exceeds a policy threshold.
B.rationale:
Deep models are often overconfident because their score scales are mismatched to true correctness, and this mismatch worsens under shift. SACC fixes scale locally with strong, label-efficient post-hoc calibrators (temperature/vector scaling plus isotonic) and extends beyond white-box models by using a logits-free confidence signal that decomposes uncertainty and fidelity, enabling calibrated probabilities even for black-box LLMs. This addresses overconfidence without retraining the base predictor.

Under distribution shift, a single global mapping rarely suffices. SACC’s shift detector and per-cluster calibrators tailor the correction to the current regime, while the selective, confidence-aware aggregator dynamically emphasizes the most reliable calibrator for each instance. Continual recalibration closes the loop: as drift evolves, calibrators are refreshed using recent errors without destabilizing the base model, maintaining trustworthy probabilities over time.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: logit-free uncertainty–fidelity decomposition for confidence
selective, confidence-aware expert aggregation
dynamic OOD stream design and continual refinement loop
isotonic regression as a practical probability calibrator

Additionally, for black-box LLMs it uses the UF decomposition (uncertainty + fidelity) from seed paper 2404.02655 as a logits-free confidence score fed into isotonic calibration. Searches found no paper that jointly combines all three of these components (shift-aware routing to per-cluster calibrators + confidence-aware meta-aggregation + continual recalibration) for the calibration-under-shift problem. Individual components are known: temperature scaling, isotonic regression, and continual learning are all established; the UF decomposition for LLM calibration is from the seed paper. However, the specific combination of shift-cluster routing + per-cluster calibrators + confidence-aware selective aggregation + continual replay-based recalibration as a unified pipeline for calibration under distribution shift has no direct match in the literature. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing named design choices (confidence-aware meta-aggregator, per-cluster calibrators with routing) not present in the surfaced literature, though individual components are known.

The method directly addresses this through three mechanistically traceable components: (A) post-hoc calibration via isotonic regression and temperature/vector scaling to align confidence scores to empirical accuracy, (B) shift-aware routing to per-cluster calibrators with a confidence-aware aggregator to handle distribution shift specifically, and (C) continual recalibration using buffered errors and periodic updates to handle ongoing drift. C.method:
We propose Shift-Aware Lipschitz–Conformal Calibration (SALCC), a two-layer calibrator that couples a feature-conditioned, Lipschitz-regularized mapper with an online conformal controller. First, collect a calibration set representative of expected deployment conditions (optionally mixing multiple environments or using mild augmentations to simulate plausible shifts). For each input, extract a compact feature vector that includes model evidence (e.g., top-1 softmax, margin, entropy), stochastic-prediction dispersion (variance/entropy over T test-time augmentations or MC-dropout passes), and an input shift score (e.g., Mahalanobis distance in penultimate-layer space or density-ratio estimate). Train a small calibrator gφ that maps these features and the raw logits to calibrated per-class probabilities. To avoid brittle overfitting to the calibration set, enforce a bounded-gradient constraint on gφ with a penalty that limits the Jacobian norm with respect to the input scores/features, ensuring the mapping is Lipschitz and monotone in class scores. Optimize gφ with a proper scoring rule (Brier/NLL) and optionally group-aware weighting for rare or OOD-like bins.

When logits are unavailable or unreliable (e.g., black-box models), decompose confidence into uncertainty and fidelity signals. Estimate uncertainty as the normalized entropy of the empirical answer distribution from K stochastic passes; estimate fidelity for the predicted class as its stability under small, semantically preserving perturbations (e.g., weak augmentations, prompt paraphrases, or dropout masks), aggregating how consistently the class remains preferred. Combine them as p_base = (1 − Uncertainty) · Fidelity, and feed p_base (quantized and embedded) alongside any accessible scores into gφ. This preserves a logit-free path to calibrated probabilities while remaining robust to temperature or decoding changes.

Wrap gφ with an online conformal controller that maintains target coverage under drift. Maintain a sliding window of recent predictions with outcomes; compute nonconformity scores s = 1 − p̂(y|x) using gφ’s output, and track the (1 − α)-quantile q of these scores with a decaying step-size update. At inference, shrink probabilities by a soft mask calibrated by q: for top-1 reporting, output p_final = max(0, p̂ − q) / (1 − q); for decision-making with abstention, form a prediction set containing classes whose cumulative calibrated mass exceeds 1 − κ, where κ is updated online to hit the desired 1 − α coverage. The softmasking preserves ordering while correcting global miscalibration; the windowed, decayed updates adapt to gradual shift, and a periodic batch recalibration can refresh q and κ when labels arrive in batches.

Deployment: start with an initial gφ fitted on historical data; enable the conformal layer in “warmup” with conservative α (e.g., 0.05) and gradually relax once the window stabilizes. Log ECE/adaptive-ECE on held-out slices identified by shift scores, and, if needed, instantiate per-slice calibrators (small heads sharing φ) to capture heterogeneous drift while keeping the Lipschitz constraint shared. For safety-critical uses, expose both p_final and the conformal prediction set; trigger abstention or human review when sets are large or p_final falls below a floor.
C.rationale:
Calibration fails under shift when post-hoc mappers overfit to a single validation distribution and when raw scores lack reliable uncertainty. The bounded-gradient, feature-conditioned mapper stabilizes the score-to-probability transformation, preventing sharp overconfidence on nearby but shifted distributions and allowing the calibrator to exploit informative, shift-sensitive features beyond a single logit. The uncertainty–fidelity decomposition supplies a robust base confidence even without logits, mitigating temperature/decoding artifacts and tying confidence to observed answer stability.

Conformal control provides distribution-free, online correction of residual miscalibration by adjusting a small number of thresholds to restore desired coverage as the environment drifts. The soft, set-valued wrapper yields actionable guarantees (coverage of the true label) while retaining scalar probabilities for ranking and cost-sensitive decisions. Together, these layers deliver calibrated probabilities that better match empirical correctness both in-distribution and under gradual distribution shift.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: bounded-gradient robustness (Lipschitz) for stability across distribution shifts

learning correctness from embedded confidence features

C.paraphrase_essence: Predicted probabilities are miscalibrated and overconfident relative to true outcomes, especially under distribution shift, undermining the reliability of downstream decisions.

novelty.reasoning: The proposed SALCC method combines three distinct mechanisms: (1) a feature-conditioned, Lipschitz-regularized neural calibrator using shift-sensitive features (Mahalanobis distance, MC-dropout dispersion, entropy), (2) an uncertainty-fidelity decomposition for logit-free calibration (directly from the UF Calibration paper), and (3) an online conformal controller with sliding-window quantile tracking for coverage under drift. Searches found: conformal prediction combined with post-hoc calibration (Adaptive Set-Mass Calibration paper), learnable conformal prediction with context-aware nonconformity scores (LCP paper), and online conformal prediction for time series (AFOCP). However, no paper was found that jointly combines Lipschitz-regularized feature-conditioned calibration (using shift scores like Mahalanobis + MC-dropout dispersion) with an online conformal controller as a two-layer system for distribution-shift-robust probability calibration. The uncertainty-fidelity decomposition component is directly borrowed from a published paper (UF Calibration, seed 3), which reduces novelty somewhat. The Lipschitz constraint on calibrators is not found in the calibration literature specifically. The combination of a Lipschitz-regularized feature-conditioned mapper + online conformal wrapper for calibration under shift has no direct published match, matching the Score 4 anchor — combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, though individual components exist separately.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is overconfidence under distribution shift — models assign high probabilities to wrong predictions, and this miscalibration worsens when the deployment distribution differs from training. The method addresses this through three traceable mechanisms: (a) a feature-conditioned calibrator that explicitly incorporates shift-sensitive signals (Mahalanobis distance, density-ratio estimates, stochastic dispersion) to adjust probabilities based on how far inputs are from the training distribution; (b) a Lipschitz regularization constraint that prevents the calibrator from overfitting to a single validation distribution and producing brittle, overconfident mappings on shifted inputs; and (c) an online conformal controller that tracks recent prediction errors in a sliding window and adjusts coverage thresholds as the distribution drifts. Each layer causally engages the stated failure mode: the shift score features directly detect distribution shift, the Lipschitz constraint prevents overconfident extrapolation, and the conformal layer provides distribution-free online correction of residual miscalibration. The causal chain from problem to mechanism is traceable with minimal gaps, though the interaction between the Lipschitz mapper and the conformal layer (and whether they might conflict or compound errors) is not fully elaborated.

D.method:
We propose Shift-Aware Coarse-to-Fine Calibration (SACoFiC), an end-to-end recipe that delivers reliable probabilities under distribution shift with negligible inference overhead. Off-line, we build a calibration library by fitting several complementary calibrators—global temperature scaling, vector/matrix scaling, Dirichlet calibration, and monotone spline/binning—using a domain-randomized validation suite that synthetically perturbs the original validation set to mimic plausible shifts (textures, lighting, viewpoints/augmentations for vision; style/noise/lexical for text; etc.). We train each calibrator to minimize a mixture of negative log-likelihood and a top-k–weighted term that emphasizes correcting the most confident predictions. To capture heterogeneity, we also learn group-wise calibrators on partitions derived from metadata (when available, e.g., region/scenario) or unsupervised clusters of backbone features. This yields a small library of candidate calibrators per group plus a robust global default.

At deployment, we apply calibration in a coarse-to-fine manner. Coarsely, we start from the globally trained temperature and fold it into the model’s final-layer weights/biases so there is no runtime cost. For each incoming batch, we infer a shift signature (e.g., via simple embedding clustering or lightweight OOD scores) to route examples to a group, and then adaptively choose the current best calibrator from that group’s library using an online upper-confidence-bound (UCB) selector that trades off exploitation of the historically best calibrator with exploration of alternatives. Finely, we run a lightweight “last-inch” correction on a trickle of target labels (or delayed feedback) using an online affine logit adjuster (vector scaling or classwise bias). The fine corrector updates via moment matching on low-order, unbiased statistics—aligning predicted and observed class marginals and, when available, a second moment of calibrated probabilities—to prevent drift and stabilize updates with very little data.

For label-shifted targets, we incorporate a fast prior-adjustment step (reweight calibrated class probabilities by the estimated target priors inferred from the labeled trickle) before the fine corrector, and we keep the entire stack monotone so ranking among classes is preserved. To prioritize decision-critical regimes, we keep the top-k emphasis during any small-batch target-domain re-fitting so that probabilities near the decision threshold and the top-1 class receive more weight than deep-tail probabilities. Throughout, we enforce efficiency: the coarse global temperature is weight-folded; group-specific affine adjustments are a single extra matrix–vector op; and the UCB selector only evaluates a tiny labeled batch per time window. When no labels are available, the system falls back to the global, domain-randomized calibrator and only performs unsupervised group routing.

Evaluation and operation are unified: we track NLL, ECE, classwise ECE, Brier score, and decision risk at target coverage across the domain-randomized suites and any observed target batches. Optionally, we expose a selective-prediction threshold on the calibrated maximum probability to guarantee a user-specified risk–coverage trade-off. The result is a stable, low-overhead calibration layer that remains accurate on-source, transfers across moderate unknown shifts, and adapts quickly with minimal labeled feedback when shifts are severe.
D.rationale:
Overconfidence under shift stems from a train–test mismatch and from calibrators fit only on the source domain. Domain-randomized calibration data broadens the fitting distribution so the chosen parameters remain valid across a family of plausible target shifts, reducing brittleness. A coarse-to-fine design provides stability (a strong global default) and rapid, low-data adaptation (a lightweight last-inch corrector) exactly where drift is observed. An online UCB selector over a small library of calibrators efficiently handles unknown or changing shifts by exploiting what works while still testing alternatives, without the cost of large ensembles.

Emphasizing the top of the predictive distribution during calibration directly targets the region that drives downstream decisions, improving decision reliability more than uniform weighting. Folding temperature and affine parameters into existing weights ensures the method is deployable in latency- and engine-constrained settings. Finally, using unbiased, low-order moment matching for the fine corrector yields stable updates from very small labeled samples, enabling continual calibration without overfitting or requiring backpropagation through the base model.

However, SACoFiC goes substantially further by: (1) maintaining a library of diverse calibrator types (temperature, vector/matrix scaling, Dirichlet, splines/binning) rather than a single regression network; (2) using a UCB bandit selector to adaptively choose among calibrators at deployment; (3) incorporating online moment-matching fine correction with label trickle; (4) folding coarse temperature into weights for zero-overhead inference; and (5) integrating label-shift prior adjustment. No single paper was found combining all these mechanisms — the UCB-based online calibrator selection, domain-randomized calibration library, and moment-matching fine corrector — for this problem. The individual components (temperature scaling, domain randomization for calibration, online adaptation) exist separately, but their specific combination as proposed here has no direct published match, matching the Score 4 anchor (combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem).

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is overconfidence (miscalibration) that persists or worsens under distribution shift, making predicted probabilities unreliable for downstream decisions. The method directly addresses this through multiple mechanistically traceable steps: (a) domain-randomized calibration broadens the fitting distribution to cover plausible shifts, directly targeting the train-test mismatch that causes miscalibration under shift; (b) the coarse-to-fine architecture provides a stable global default plus adaptive group-specific and online fine-tuning when shift is detected, directly engaging the "distribution shift" failure mode; (c) the UCB selector over a calibrator library handles unknown or changing shifts by dynamically routing to the best-performing calibrator; (d) the top-k emphasis during fitting directly targets overconfidence in the high-confidence regime that drives downstream decisions; and (e) moment matching for the fine corrector aligns predicted and observed marginals, a direct calibration mechanism. The causal chain from problem (overconfident predictions under shift) to method steps (domain randomization → group routing → online adaptation → moment matching) is traceable with minimal gaps. A minor gap is that the method's effectiveness under severe, unanticipated shifts relies on the domain-randomized suite covering the actual shift family, which is assumed but not guaranteed — however, this is an effectiveness concern, not a validity gap.

query_text:
Aligned text-image pairs are scarce in some domains while
unimodal data is abundant; how can we learn cross-modal
representations via contrastive objectives that exploit the
abundant unimodal data?

A.method:
Prototype-bridged semi-supervised contrastive alignment. First, learn strong unimodal encoders using only the abundant single-modality data: an image encoder with instance discrimination and multi-augmentation contrastive losses, and a text encoder with contrastive next-sentence/augment discrimination and masked token denoising. Normalize both embedding spaces and freeze lightweight momentum teacher copies for stability. From each unimodal corpus, cluster the embeddings online (e.g., k-means or sinkhorn-k-means with a memory queue) to obtain a bank of visual prototypes and textual prototypes, and maintain soft assignments of instances to prototypes.

Use the small set of aligned image–text pairs to synchronize the two prototype banks. Compute an alignment matrix between visual and textual prototypes by solving a regularized optimal transport/bipartite matching problem whose cost combines: (a) prototype–prototype cosine distances, and (b) co-occurrence evidence induced by how paired instances assign to prototypes. The result is a dense, nonnegative alignment matrix that gives, for any visual prototype, a probability distribution over textual prototypes (and vice versa), capturing many-to-many concept correspondences.

Exploit unimodal data via expected contrastive learning with soft positives. For any unimodal image, push its embedding toward a soft mixture of textual instances: map its visual prototype distribution through the prototype alignment matrix to obtain a distribution over textual prototypes, then to a distribution over text instances (via their prototype posteriors). Train a cross-modal InfoNCE objective where the positive set is weighted by these probabilities (soft-label contrastive), negatives are all other instances with probability near zero, and false negatives are down-weighted if they share prototypes. Symmetrically, train text-to-image. When true aligned pairs exist, include a standard hard contrastive loss. Add two auxiliary terms: (i) prototype–prototype contrast that pulls aligned visual/textual prototypes together; (ii) unimodal contrastive regularizers to preserve structure within each modality.

Adopt a curriculum and EM-like refinement loop. Begin with high-confidence prototype alignments and top-k pseudo-pairs mined from the soft distributions; gradually relax thresholds as the encoders improve. Every few epochs, recompute prototypes and re-estimate the alignment matrix using the latest embeddings and the growing set of confident pseudo-pairs. Use momentum teachers and large memory queues per modality for stable target distributions; apply cycle-consistency (image→text→image) and consistency under data augmentations/back-translations as additional constraints. Balance losses with adaptive weights so the scarce true pairs have strong influence early, while the expected-contrastive terms dominate as confidence in pseudo-labels grows.

At convergence, discard the prototype machinery and use the encoders for zero-shot retrieval and transfer. The encoders have been aligned to a shared space through a combination of a small number of ground-truth pairs and a large volume of unimodal data transformed into soft cross-modal supervision via prototype alignment.
A.rationale:
Unimodal self-supervision shapes high-capacity encoders with rich semantics from abundant data, but does not by itself align modalities. Prototype banks summarize the structure of each modality and provide a compact bridge for transferring alignment from a small paired set to the vast unpaired corpora. By learning a soft, many-to-many mapping between prototype spaces, the method propagates cross-modal correspondences beyond observed pairs while remaining robust to noise and polysemy.

Expected (soft-label) contrastive objectives let every unimodal instance contribute cross-modal learning without requiring a single hard match, mitigating confirmation bias from noisy pseudo-pairs. The curriculum with EM-style re-clustering, momentum teachers, and cycle-consistency steadily improves alignment quality, while false-negative suppression and auxiliary unimodal losses preserve intra-modality discriminability. Together, these design choices leverage abundant unimodal data to learn strong cross-modal representations under scarce pairing.

novelty.reasoning: The proposed method combines: (1) unimodal self-supervised pretraining with momentum teachers, (2) online clustering to build separate visual and textual prototype banks, (3) OT-based bipartite prototype-to-prototype alignment matrix synchronized via small paired data, (4) soft-label/expected InfoNCE for unimodal instances using prototype distributions as soft positives, (5) EM-style curriculum refinement with cycle-consistency and false-negative suppression. SOTAlign (2026) is the closest match — semi-supervised alignment of unimodal encoders via OT with small paired + large unpaired data — but it uses a linear teacher + OT divergence on relational structure, not prototype banks or soft-label contrastive objectives. OTPAL (2024) uses OT to align samples to prototypes but not prototype-to-prototype cross-modal alignment with soft InfoNCE for unimodal data. Set-CLIP uses distribution-level alignment without prototype banks. No search surfaced a paper combining prototype bank synchronization via OT alignment matrix + soft-label InfoNCE for unimodal instances + EM refinement for this problem. The method introduces multiple named design choices (prototype bank OT synchronization, expected contrastive with soft positives derived from prototype distributions, false-negative suppression via prototype posteriors) not found jointly in any surfaced paper, matching the Score 4-5 range. However, the individual mechanisms (OT alignment, prototype banks, soft-label contrastive, momentum teachers) are all known; the novelty is in their specific combination and the prototype-bridging framing. Since no paper combines prototype-to-prototype OT alignment matrix with soft-label InfoNCE for unimodal data in this semi-supervised cross-modal setting, this matches the Score 4 anchor — combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is the scarcity of aligned text-image pairs, which prevents standard contrastive cross-modal learning from exploiting the abundant unimodal data. The method's mechanism directly addresses this by (a) building strong unimodal encoders from unpaired data, (b) constructing prototype banks for each modality and aligning them via optimal transport using the small paired set, and (c) propagating cross-modal supervision to unimodal instances through soft prototype-based pseudo-positives in an expected InfoNCE objective. The causal chain is traceable: scarce pairs anchor prototype alignment → alignment matrix distributes cross-modal signal → every unimodal instance receives soft cross-modal supervision → encoders converge to a shared space. There are no significant gaps; each step mechanistically resolves the failure mode of lacking paired data by converting prototype-level alignment into instance-level cross-modal training signal.

B.method:
We propose Mixed-Source Contrastive Alignment (MiSCA), a dual-encoder framework that learns a shared embedding space for images and text by blending three supervision sources in every batch: scarce real pairs, abundant image-only data, and abundant text-only data. Two modality encoders (vision and text) feed projection heads into a common space. Training optimizes a unified contrastive objective composed of: (i) standard symmetric InfoNCE on the small set of real image–text pairs; (ii) positive-augmented contrast on pseudo-pairs synthesized bidirectionally from unimodal corpora; and (iii) unimodal contrastive regularizers that shape each marginal with strong self-supervision. The batch is constructed with a fixed cap on unimodal-to-paired ratio, and per-term weights and temperatures are tuned to balance gradients across sources.

To exploit image-only data, we attach a captioner (e.g., BLIP-family) to generate one or more candidate captions per image and keep only high-confidence candidates using a frozen VLM (e.g., CLIP) similarity as a filter. Each accepted (image, caption′) becomes a soft-positive in a symmetric InfoNCE that mixes with real pairs; its contribution is down-weighted by a confidence-derived scalar and a higher temperature. To exploit text-only data, we either (a) retrieve top-k images from the image pool using a frozen VLM and treat the highest-scoring retrieval as a soft-positive, or (b) when feasible, synthesize low-resolution images from text using a diffusion prior and treat (image′, text) as a soft-positive. We also learn a lightweight alignment transform A on text embeddings (low-rank or orthogonal-constrained) jointly with the encoders to reduce modality mismatch; A is applied to all text embeddings before projection. In parallel, we apply unimodal contrastive objectives (SimCLR-style for images with strong augmentations; SimCSE/back-translation for text) to maximize instance discrimination within each modality, improving the quality of the marginals used by cross-modal learning.

To better leverage unpaired data statistically, we place a distributional regularizer on the projected embeddings: each modality’s embedding distribution is modeled with a flexible mixture for its marginal, and a parametric copula couples these marginals into a joint. We optimize an ELBO that includes the copula log-likelihood on real (and high-confidence pseudo) pairs plus marginal terms on all unimodal samples. This encourages the encoders to learn marginals that are easy to couple and a dependency structure that reflects true cross-modal relationships, even when the pair-supervision is scarce.

We strengthen the contrastive signal with curated hard negatives. On the text side, we generate compositional foils (attribute swaps, antonyms, hypernym substitutions) for each caption and ensure plausibility via entailment checks; these become prioritized in-batch negatives. On the vision side, for a given caption we mine visually similar but semantically mismatched images from a memory bank using the current model; the hardest negatives (highest similarity yet label-mismatched) are emphasized via a margin-based variant of the contrastive loss. Optionally, we add an auxiliary captioning head trained to reconstruct captions from the same visual features used by contrastive learning, further tying visual embeddings to captionable semantics.

Training proceeds in two stages. Stage 1 bootstraps the encoders with unimodal contrastive pretraining and positive-augmented pseudo-pair contrast; the copula regularizer is already active but with a low weight, and A is learned. Stage 2 fine-tunes on the available true pairs with stronger hard-negative mining and increases the copula weight; pseudo-pairs remain in the mix with conservative weights. Throughout, we enforce anti-bias balancing: the ratio of unimodal to paired items per batch is capped, per-term loss weights are tuned to equalize gradient norms across sources, and temperatures are modality-specific. Evaluation and early stopping focus on cross-modal retrieval to ensure the representation remains aligned.
B.rationale:
MiSCA turns abundant unimodal corpora into effective cross-modal supervision by synthesizing or retrieving soft-positive counterparts and folding them into a symmetric contrastive objective, directly increasing the amount and diversity of alignment signals without requiring real pairs. Reliability weighting and temperature control prevent synthetic noise from dominating learning, while hardest-negative mining improves discrimination where real negatives are most informative. A small alignment transform on text embeddings corrects systematic modality skew that otherwise wastes supervision.

Beyond pairwise contrast, modeling the embedding marginals and their dependence with a copula lets the training use all unimodal samples to shape the modality-specific distributions, and uses the few real pairs to pin down cross-modal dependence. This distributional regularizer complements instance-level InfoNCE by encouraging globally compatible embeddings even when direct supervision is scarce. Finally, anti-bias scheduling and loss balancing counteract the known tendency of mixed-text/multimodal training to overfit to the more abundant unimodal data, preserving cross-modal grounding.

C.method:
We propose Pivot-Aware Semi-Contrastive Alignment (PASCA), a two-encoder framework that turns abundant unimodal data into cross-modal supervision. A vision transformer and a text transformer each produce K multi-view embeddings per input (via K learned viewer tokens) and a pooled global embedding. We establish a shared semantic pivot space built from a vocabulary of objects, attributes, and actions; both modalities learn to predict soft distributions over this vocabulary and to anchor their embeddings to a pivot vector constructed from it. With only a small set of true image–text pairs, we then contrastively align the modalities while expanding training signals from large image-only and text-only corpora.

Stage A (unimodal pretraining + pivot learning): On images, we apply strong intra-image contrastive learning across augmentations and train a concept head to predict a soft label distribution over the pivot vocabulary using detector/tagger pseudo-labels (objects, attributes) gathered at region and image levels; the loss is a KL divergence to soft targets plus focal weighting for tail concepts. On text, we combine masked language modeling with within-text contrastive learning: paraphrases are positives, and controlled semantic edits are hard negatives. A parallel concept head predicts the same pivot distribution from text using a tagger over noun phrases, attributes, and actions; we minimize KL to these soft targets. For both modalities, we form a pivot embedding by averaging learned concept embeddings weighted by the predicted distribution and apply a pivot-contrastive loss that treats an input’s global and view embeddings as positives to its pivot embedding and negatives to mismatched pivots. This step uses only unimodal data yet pushes images and texts that share concepts toward the same anchors.

Stage B (pseudo-pair mining and weighted cross-modal contrast): We retrieve pseudo image–text matches by nearest-neighbor search in the pivot space (cosine over pivot embeddings) and within the multi-view spaces (max over view-to-view similarities). To control noise, we fit a lightweight copula model on the scarce true pairs to estimate joint dependence between vision and text embeddings; its likelihood reweights candidate pairs. We further screen candidates with a caption-score and an optional LLM judge to drop false positives. We then optimize a cross-modal InfoNCE over a mixture of true pairs and pseudo pairs, using pair-specific positive weights given by the copula likelihood and judge confidence. Hard negatives include Tailor-style edited texts that flip key concepts for a given image and nearest-neighbor decoys that differ along at least one pivot dimension. Similarity between an image and a text is computed by max-pooling over all view-to-view dot products, preserving ANN-friendly retrieval.

Stage C (semantic auxiliaries and curriculum): To further inject captionable semantics, we attach a light captioning head to the text decoder side and train image-to-text generation on abundant pseudo-captions produced by an external captioner (filtered by pivot consistency). We also add a lexicon-translation auxiliary: aggregate image region features into segments and predict a distribution over pivot tokens corresponding to text words, and symmetrically map salient text spans to visual concepts; both use KL losses that specialize upper layers for cross-modal token mapping. Training follows a curriculum: start with only high-confidence pseudo pairs (high pivot similarity and copula score), then progressively relax thresholds and increase the weight of cross-modal contrast, while keeping unimodal and pivot losses active for stability. At inference, we use the pooled global embedding and multi-view max pooling for robust matching, with optional pivot-aware reranking.
C.rationale:
PASCA converts plentiful unimodal data into cross-modal supervision through three mechanisms: (1) unimodal contrastive and language modeling to learn strong encoders; (2) a shared, softly-supervised semantic pivot that both modalities predict from their own data, making them commensurate without needing pairs; and (3) pseudo-pair mining guided by the pivot space and a copula-based joint model to safely expand contrastive alignment beyond the scarce labeled pairs. The multi-view design increases the chance that at least one view aligns across modalities, which is especially important when supervision is sparse.

By mixing weighted cross-modal InfoNCE on a small set of real and carefully filtered pseudo pairs with continual unimodal and pivot objectives, the encoders remain semantically grounded while their spaces are pulled together. Auxiliary captioning and lexicon-translation losses further inject captionable structure so the learned embeddings encode concepts recoverable in language, improving transfer to retrieval and grounding even when aligned pairs are limited.

The method addresses this through three traceable mechanisms: (a) a shared semantic pivot space built from unimodal data alone that pushes both modalities toward common concept anchors without requiring paired data, (b) pseudo-pair mining via nearest-neighbor search in the pivot space combined with copula-based noise filtering to expand contrastive training beyond the scarce true pairs, and (c) unimodal pretraining stages that build strong encoders before cross-modal alignment. D.method:
We propose Pivot-augmented Contrastive Cross-Modal Learning (PiCoX), a semi/self-supervised framework that aligns images and text when only a small set of paired samples is available but large unimodal corpora exist. PiCoX builds a shared semantic pivot space and combines three complementary signals: (1) strong unimodal instance-contrastive learning within each modality, (2) pivot distillation that ties both modalities to the same semantic basis using abundant unimodal data, and (3) cross-modal contrast on pseudo-pairs mined by approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval in the pivot space. Real paired data, when available, are simply injected as hard positives to calibrate the alignment.

First, we define K semantic pivots (e.g., domain terms, curated prompts, or a learned prompt pool). A frozen text encoder produces pivot embeddings that parameterize a shared semantic basis; keeping these pivots fixed stabilizes training. Each image and text sample is projected onto this basis by computing similarities to all pivots, yielding a soft distribution over pivots via temperature-scaled softmax. This produces modality-specific but commensurate K-dimensional representations. We then perform pivot distillation with unimodal data: images learn to predict their own pivot distributions (from the image encoder), and texts learn theirs (from the text encoder), while both encoders are gently regularized to keep the pivot basis consistent. An approximate nearest-neighbor index is built over the pivotized text representations and reused throughout training and evaluation.

In parallel, we run unimodal contrastive objectives to fully exploit the abundant single-modality data. For images, SimCLR-style augmentations are used with an InfoNCE loss in image embedding space; for text, a SimCSE-style objective provides an analogous instance-discrimination signal. To encourage the pivot projections to carry aligned structure, we add a lightweight correlation regularizer that maximizes batchwise correlation between two augmentation views’ pivot logits within each modality and, later, between cross-modal pairs. This co-regularization makes the pivot space predictive and stable without requiring aligned pairs early in training.

Periodically, we mine cross-modal pseudo-positives using the shared pivot space: image samples query the text ANN index to retrieve the top-k nearest neighbors (and vice versa). These become soft positives in a cross-modal InfoNCE, with weights proportional to their pivot-space similarity, while all other in-batch samples serve as negatives. To target the primary source of modality mismatch, we add an angular alignment term that explicitly reduces the angle between the image and text embeddings of mined positives (optionally applying a small projection step for the most confident matches), avoiding over-constraining embedding norms. We adopt a precision-to-recall curriculum: start with strict similarity thresholds and small k to ensure high-quality pseudo-pairs, gradually relaxing thresholds and increasing k as encoders improve. A momentum teacher per modality stabilizes targets for both pivot distillation and pseudo-pair mining.

Training optimizes a weighted sum of unimodal contrastive losses, pivot distillation, cross-modal soft-contrastive loss on mined pairs, the angular alignment penalty, and the correlation regularizer. If a small set of real image–text pairs exists, we simply treat them as hard positives with higher weights and include their pivot-correlation term. At inference, we reuse the same pivotized representations and ANN index for cross-modal retrieval and transfer, ensuring that training and deployment objectives are aligned.
D.rationale:
The scarce-pairs setting weakens pure cross-modal contrastive learning. PiCoX circumvents this by first exploiting abundant unimodal data to learn strong within-modality invariances and by anchoring both modalities to a common, fixed semantic pivot basis. This pivot space acts as a low-leakage bridge: even without aligned pairs, each modality learns a distribution over shared semantics, enabling reliable pseudo-pair mining. Cross-modal contrast is then applied on these mined pairs with soft weights, progressively improving alignment as mining quality improves.

Three design elements make the approach effective: (1) fixed semantic pivots and a shared ANN index ensure stable training and deployment-aligned retrieval; (2) correlation regularization on pivot projections encourages consistent cross-modal structure without requiring ground-truth pairs; and (3) an angular alignment term directly combats modality gaps by aligning directions rather than magnitudes, which is especially robust in heterogeneous embedding spaces. Combined, these components let us leverage abundant unimodal corpora to learn robust cross-modal representations with minimal or no paired supervision.

query_text:
Masked-prediction pretraining transformed language and vision;
how can we adapt the same principle to other data types like
graphs, time series, or sensor streams, and what objective
design choices matter?

A.method:
We propose Generalized Masked Reconstruction Pretraining (GMRP), a unified masked-prediction framework for graphs, time series, and multi-sensor streams. GMRP exposes each modality through a common "structured token" interface that carries content (values/attributes), structure (topology or sensor identity), and geometry (time, position, or spectral index). Modality-specific encoders map tokens into a shared latent space: a Graph Transformer with structural encodings (e.g., Laplacian eigenvectors, random-walk features) for graphs; a Temporal Transformer/TCN hybrid with continuous-time positional features for regularly or irregularly sampled time series; and a cross-sensor transformer that handles asynchronous event tokens for heterogeneous sensor streams. A lightweight, modality-aware decoder predicts masked tokens from contextual embeddings while supporting probabilistic outputs. 

Masking is multi-granular and structure-aware. For graphs, we sample masks as node attributes, edges, or subgraphs obtained via short random walks or k-hop neighborhoods; we also mask structural signals (degree, centrality) to force topology-aware reasoning. For time series, we mask variable-length spans, random isolated points, and multi-resolution patches in both time and frequency (e.g., STFT patches), encouraging recovery of both local dynamics and seasonal components. For sensor streams, we perform channel-drop (entire sensor silencing), time-drop (temporal windows), cross-sensor span masks aligned by timestamps, and event jitter masks that mimic clock drift or packet loss. A curriculum increases mask rate and contiguity over training, and a corruption library injects realistic noise: time-warping and amplitude jitter for signals; edge rewiring and attribute noise for graphs; timestamp quantization and missingness bursts for sensors. 

Objectives are composite and distribution-aware. The core loss is masked-token negative log-likelihood under modality-appropriate heads: (a) continuous targets use heteroscedastic mixture density models or quantile regression (capturing multi-modality and uncertainty); (b) discrete/categorical attributes use cross-entropy; (c) edges use Bernoulli or relational distributions with class-imbalance handling. To capture long-range structure, we add auxiliary consistency terms: a spectral loss that matches graph Laplacian spectra on reconstructed subgraphs; a frequency-domain loss (e.g., log-magnitude STFT MSE) for signals; and local-global contrastive alignment (InfoNCE) between masked-region representations and ground-truth content to combat trivial averaging. We unify backcasting and forecasting by allowing masked regions to straddle past and future, bridging imputation and prediction. Optional diffusion-style denoising heads share the encoder and learn score fields for continuous signals, improving sharpness and uncertainty calibration on high-noise spans. 

Training proceeds with mixed-mini-batches from all modalities, sampling mask shapes and corruption types according to an adaptive scheduler that targets failure modes (e.g., increase subgraph masks when edge recovery lags). We regularize with invariance constraints matched to each domain: isomorphism-preserving augmentations for graphs; time-shifts and monotone time-warpings for series; sensor-identity permutations within known interchangeable groups. Pretraining scales with masked-token throughput and uses EMA target encoders for contrastive terms to stabilize learning. Downstream, we fine-tune or linear-probe on forecasting, anomaly detection, imputation, classification, and link prediction, optionally freezing the backbone and swapping decoders. Comprehensive ablations sweep mask granularity, mask rate, corruption family, target space (raw vs discretized codebook), and bidirectional vs causal context to map the objective design landscape.
A.rationale:
Masked reconstruction transfers to non-text domains when the mask shapes, corruption processes, and prediction targets reflect the domain’s structure and uncertainty. GMRP makes those choices explicit: structural masks (subgraphs, spans, channels) force reasoning over the true dependency graph; distributional heads and diffusion-style denoising prevent regression-to-the-mean on multi-modal or noisy targets; and auxiliary spectral/frequency losses preserve global properties that pointwise MSE cannot. This yields encoders that learn semantics aligned with downstream tasks like forecasting, anomaly detection, and link prediction. 

The key objective design levers are (1) granularity and geometry of masks (node/edge/subgraph; point/span/patch; channel/time/event), (2) corruption family that matches real missingness/noise (rewiring, time-warp, jitter), (3) target space and likelihood (discretized codes vs parametric distributions vs scores), (4) context directionality (bidirectional for imputation vs causal for forecasting), and (5) invariances appropriate to each modality. By systematically combining and ablating these levers, GMRP generalizes masked pretraining beyond language/vision while retaining the ingredients that made it successful: rich context modeling, uncertainty-aware prediction, and structure-preserving learning.

novelty.reasoning: The searches reveal that individual components of GMRP exist in prior work: graph masked autoencoders with subgraph/edge masking (Hi-GMAE, SimSGT), time series masked pretraining with frequency-domain losses (CRT, Frequency-Aware MAE, Wavelet MMR), and contrastive+masked objectives for graphs. However, no paper surfaces that jointly combines all of: (1) a unified structured-token interface spanning graphs, time series, AND heterogeneous sensor streams; (2) multi-granular structure-aware masking across all three modalities simultaneously; (3) composite objectives including spectral graph loss + frequency-domain signal loss + InfoNCE + optional diffusion denoising heads; (4) adaptive curriculum with a corruption library matching domain-specific noise; and (5) heteroscedastic/mixture-density prediction heads. The combination of these mechanism families for this exact problem (unified masked pretraining across graphs, time series, and sensor streams) has no direct published match, though individual pieces exist. This matches the Score 4 anchor — combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with several named design choices (structured token interface, modality-aware corruption library, diffusion denoising heads sharing the encoder) not present in the surfaced literature as a combined system.

validity.reasoning: The query asks how masked-prediction pretraining can be adapted to graphs, time series, and sensor streams, and what objective design choices matter. The failure mode is that naive transfer of masked prediction (designed for text/vision tokens) fails to capture the structural, temporal, and distributional properties specific to these other modalities. GMRP directly addresses this by (a) designing modality-specific structured token interfaces and encoders, (b) implementing structure-aware masking strategies tailored to each domain (subgraph masks, temporal spans, channel-drop), (c) using distribution-appropriate prediction heads (mixture density, quantile regression, Bernoulli), and (d) adding auxiliary losses (spectral, frequency-domain, contrastive) to preserve global structure that pointwise MSE misses. The causal chain from problem to method is traceable: each identified failure mode of naive masked prediction in non-text domains is addressed by a specific mechanism, and the ablation framework explicitly maps the objective design landscape the query asks about. There are no significant gaps in the causal chain.

B.method:
We propose Generalized Masked Conditional Modeling (GMCM), a single training recipe that adapts masked-prediction pretraining to graphs, time series, and multi-sensor streams by combining domain-aligned corruptions with three complementary objectives: probabilistic reconstruction, conditional energy-based discrimination, and marginalization over plausible replacements. For each modality we define a corruption operator that selects semantically coherent spans to hide: GraphMask stochastically masks node/edge attributes and removes small random-walk subgraphs while preserving connectivity; SeriesMask hides contiguous temporal spans, event-aligned windows, and/or frequency bands; SensorMask co-masks cross-channel subsets following real missingness patterns and intermittently drops entire devices. A modality-aware encoder (e.g., graph transformer/GNN with structural encodings; temporal transformer with relative time and channel embeddings) consumes the partially observed input and produces context features for the masked regions. Optional metadata (e.g., timestamp, location, device type) is injected as control codes to improve conditionality.

At the objective layer, we blend three losses. (1) Likelihood reconstruction: for continuous targets we model pθ(s|c) with heteroscedastic heads (Gaussian with learned variance, mixture density, or quantile regression) over each masked span s given context c; for categorical attributes we use cross-entropy. Span-lengths are sampled from a heavy-tailed distribution (short and medium windows predominate; occasional long windows) to encourage both local and long-range reasoning. (2) Conditional energy-based discrimination: inspired by ELECTRIC-style cloze, we learn a lightweight proposal qϕ(s~|c) that samples realistic replacements for all masked spans in one pass, then train an energy head Eθ(s|c) with conditional NCE so that true spans have lower energy than replacements. This avoids a large softmax/vocabulary and naturally fits continuous signals. Efficiency follows from replacing 15–30% of elements/spans at once and computing the NCE in a single forward pass. (3) Mask-and-marginalize: to reduce out-of-distribution artifacts from hard masking, we approximate marginalization over plausible replacements by drawing K candidates s~k ∼ qϕ(·|c) and optimizing a weight-of-evidence/variational objective that increases the probability gap between the true span and the candidate set while regularizing Eθ so small, reasonable edits do not cause large prediction swings.

We make masking structure-aware so the model learns to exploit repeated patterns. A parallel-structure miner periodically identifies homologous pairs (or sets) of spans: in time series via autocorrelation/seasonality and event detectors; in sensor networks via co-activation motifs across channels; in graphs via frequent subgraphs/isomorphic motifs. During training we jointly mask paired spans and add a consistency loss that aligns their latent predictions and uncertainties, encouraging in-context-style generalization from one instance of a pattern to another. Co-masking across dependent channels/nodes promotes learning cross-variable constraints (e.g., power balance across meters, conservation laws on graphs). A simple curriculum ramps the mask rate and span size, and mixes corruption types according to the data’s missingness profile.

To handle non-stationary streams, we add online refinement. At deployment, we track an error/energy stream: windows with high reconstruction error or high Eθ are buffered. Periodically, we update the model on this buffer with the same self-supervised losses plus a small rehearsal set from past data to prevent forgetting. We monitor retention vs adaptation with stream-wise metrics (instant error-fixing, knowledge retention, cumulative success), and throttle updates when drift subsides. Practically, we share parameters between the encoder and the proposal qϕ where possible, use in-batch negatives for NCE, and tune the loss mixture: begin likelihood-heavy for stability, then increase the energy and marginalization weights to improve discrimination and robustness.

Key design choices that matter: (a) corruption realism and granularity (span/motif-aware masking beats IID masking, and co-masking correlated channels/nodes helps); (b) output parameterization (mixtures/quantiles reduce mode-averaging on multi-modal signals); (c) proposal quality for replacements (learned, conditional qϕ outperforms static or batch-shuffled negatives and stabilizes energy-based training); (d) mask rate/schedule (15–30% with a long-span tail balances coverage and difficulty); (e) structure-aware auxiliary losses (paired-span consistency accelerates long-horizon competence); (f) online refinement policy (error-buffer sampling with rehearsal mitigates drift without catastrophic forgetting).
B.rationale:
Masked prediction is a conditional modeling principle, not a modality—what changes across graphs and continuous streams is how we hide information and how we score predictions. Likelihood-based reconstruction alone struggles with continuous, multi-modal targets and can miscalibrate under distribution shift; conversely, pure classification-style masking assumes a discrete vocabulary. Adding a conditional energy-based objective with realistic replacements removes the need for a large softmax, works naturally on continuous spans, and turns pretraining into density-ratio learning that transfers well. Approximating marginalization over plausible replacements reduces the train–test mismatch introduced by hard masking and discourages the model from learning shortcuts tied to artificial [MASK] artifacts.

Structure-aware masking and paired-span consistency target the mechanisms that made masked pretraining powerful in language/vision—learning from repeated local patterns—and port them to periodic signals and graph motifs. Finally, real sensor streams are non-stationary; integrating an error-driven online refinement loop preserves the utility of masked modeling under drift by updating where the model fails while rehearsing to protect prior knowledge. Together, these pieces preserve the core of masked pretraining—predict missing parts from context—while adapting the corruptions and objectives to the statistical realities of graphs, time series, and sensor data.
