D.method:
We propose a four-stage pipeline, Circuit Decomposition and Verification (CDV), that turns opaque activations into human-interpretable features and causally verified circuits you can edit. Stage 1 (Feature discovery) learns a sparse, overcomplete dictionary over layer activations collected from a curated, diverse input set. Each activation vector is reconstructed as a sparse nonnegative combination of feature atoms; to reflect polysemantic neurons, we also estimate per-context facet responsibilities p(facet | unit, context), allowing a single base unit to express multiple interpretable facets depending on input. A multi-expert analysis loop (linear/sparse probes, dataset search for nearest neighbors, feature-visualization, and concept-matching) independently proposes names and examples for each atom; a consensus module weights these signals to attach a provisional human label and confidence to every feature.

Stage 2 (Circuit induction) builds a directed, layer-respecting graph over discovered features that explains a target behavior (e.g., a logit, next-token, or internal subgoal). We start from a seed set (features with highest causal influence on the target under activation patching) and expand via a guided search: for each candidate upstream feature, we estimate an expected causal gain score by a small look-ahead—simulate ablate/add-back or replace-with-counterfactual interventions and predict the marginal improvement in target explanation given the current subgraph. Beam search with this gain metric concentrates computation on promising edges while enforcing sparsity and acyclicity constraints. Edges are kept only if their effects are robust across contexts and remain after controlling for confounds (e.g., via conditional patching on current subgraph states).

Stage 3 (Verification suite) stress-tests both the feature dictionary and the induced circuit. We quantify explanation fidelity with perturbation-based metrics: mask important features (set activations to a neutral baseline) and measure accuracy drop versus masking unimportant ones; compute prediction gaps under Gaussian perturbations targeted to important vs unimportant features; and assess relative input/representation stability of the circuit under benign noise. We run knockout/add-back tests on edges, counterfactual data where ground-truth causal structure is known, and holdout distributions designed to break spurious shortcuts. Only circuits that pass pre-registered thresholds on these metrics, including out-of-distribution generalization, are accepted.

Stage 4 (Correct-and-edit loop) automatically refines failed decompositions and supports safe edits. When a circuit fails a metric, we analyze which tests contributed most and propose structured updates: split or merge ambiguous atoms, adjust edge sets, or re-weight facet responsibilities; these proposals are generated by the same multi-expert tools and accepted by a consensus-weighted score. For behavior changes, we expose two edit paths: (a) parametric, inserting sparse low-rank adapters that scale specific verified edges or features; and (b) non-parametric, an online correction memory that, at inference, retrieves similar hidden states and applies a small, clamped steering along verified feature directions to compensate predictable errors. All edits are validated by re-running the verification suite plus task-level regression tests before deployment.

Optionally, when training is permitted, we add light auxiliary heads on intermediate layers to predict task-relevant signals with layer-wise losses. This makes features more directly predictive and keeps the early-layer atoms stable under partial computation, improving both the quality of discovered atoms and the reliability of early-exit circuit checks without changing the main task objective.
D.rationale:
CDV directly targets the three goals in the query. Sparse dictionary learning with per-context facet responsibilities decomposes superposed representations into human-interpretable atoms while acknowledging polysemy; multi-expert consensus reduces tool-specific bias when naming and validating features. Causal circuit induction via activation patching and a gain-guided search yields compact, directed subgraphs that explain how features compose to implement specific computations, rather than relying on correlational attributions.

The verification suite translates explanation quality into objective, stress-tested numbers by contrasting perturbations to important vs unimportant features, measuring stability, and validating on synthetic counterfactual worlds with known ground truth. The corrective refinement loop closes the gap between hypotheses and evidence, turning metric failures into actionable edits of the feature dictionary or circuit, and the two edit mechanisms provide safe, localized behavior changes aligned with the verified decomposition. Optional layer-wise auxiliary training further stabilizes intermediate features when modifications to training are allowed.

query_text:
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?

A.method:
Constrained Distillation with Orthogonal Low‑Rank Adaptation (CDOLA). Start from a pretrained model M_{t-1}. When new data D_t arrives, create a frozen snapshot T (the teacher) by copying M_{t-1}. Insert low‑rank adapters (or other parameter‑efficient modules) into a selected set of layers and freeze the base weights. Training minimizes a composite objective: (a) the supervised loss on D_t for the new task, (b) a functional preservation loss that matches the student’s outputs (and optionally intermediate features) to T’s outputs on the very same D_t inputs (temperature‑scaled distillation), and (c) a weight‑movement penalty that discourages changes along directions the previous model deemed important. The penalty uses an online, diagonal Fisher‑like importance estimate accumulated from gradients observed during training with T; it defines a quadratic trust region centered at the pre‑update weights to bound destructive drift without storing any prior examples.

To further reduce interference, constrain adaptation to an orthogonal subspace. Each training step computes gradients only for adapter parameters and projects them to be orthogonal to the top importance‑weighted directions (per layer) estimated from the Fisher sketch. This can be implemented with lightweight per‑layer diagonal rescaling or a small‑rank projection derived from a randomized SVD of the recent gradient covariance. The effect is that updates specialize in a subspace minimally overlapping with directions that implement prior skills, while still allowing sufficient capacity for the new task via the adapters’ low‑rank degrees of freedom.

CDOLA is memory‑light and can be run exemplar‑free. If a minimal memory budget is allowed, maintain a tiny prototype bank instead of raw data: per previously known class or behavior cluster, store a handful of penultimate‑layer feature means and variances (and optional calibrated logits). During training, add a prototype‑consistency term that encourages the student’s feature distribution, when routed by T’s predictions on D_t, to remain close to these anchors (e.g., via center loss or MMD on features). This compresses prior data into tens to hundreds of floating‑point vectors rather than full examples. If absolutely zero prior memory is required, skip prototypes; functional preservation still occurs by distilling T on D_t only, optionally augmented with data‑free synthetic anchors obtained by briefly optimizing random noise to match T’s batch‑norm statistics.

After convergence, consolidate: merge the learned adapters into the base weights with a rank‑capped update (e.g., SVD‑truncated merge) and prune residual adapter parameters to keep the footprint constant. Update the running importance estimate with an exponential decay to reflect the new solution, and discard the teacher snapshot. Repeat this procedure for each new data tranche. Practical recipe: set loss weights so that early training emphasizes distillation and the trust‑region penalty, then anneal toward the task loss; gate distillation by teacher confidence to avoid over‑constraining in regions where prior knowledge is unreliable; and monitor a KL‑divergence budget between student and teacher to adaptively scale the trust region.
A.rationale:
The method preserves prior capabilities without storing full historical data by enforcing two complementary constraints during adaptation: a functional constraint (distillation from a frozen snapshot on the new inputs) and a parameter‑space constraint (importance‑weighted trust region). Distilling on the very same new inputs supplies a continual, data‑free proxy for the old tasks’ behavior wherever the new distribution overlaps, while the trust region prevents large moves along parameter directions that are causally important for those behaviors, thereby reducing catastrophic drift even in out‑of‑distribution regions.

Limiting updates to orthogonal low‑rank adapters localizes plasticity into fresh subspaces with minimal overlap to parameters encoding old skills, further reducing interference while keeping compute and memory modest. Optional feature‑prototype summaries provide a tiny, privacy‑preserving memory that regularizes internal representations without retaining raw examples. The consolidation step ensures the model’s capacity does not grow unbounded across tasks. Together, these ingredients enable continual updates with little or no prior data retention and strong resistance to forgetting.

B.method:
Adapter-Replay Distilled Anchoring (ARDA). Start from a frozen base model θ0 and attach two lightweight, task-local modules: (i) small, post-FFN adapters (or LoRA low-rank updates) in each transformer block to hold the new knowledge, and (ii) an optional soft-prompt/knowledge adapter injected at the embedding layer to steer the model toward the new task. Keep a copy of θ0 as the anchor teacher T. When a new dataset Dt arrives, train only these lightweight modules while θ0 remains frozen. This isolates new information from the core weights and creates a clean mechanism to preserve prior skills via teacher matching.

To avoid storing prior training data, construct an ephemeral rehearsal set Rt on the fly by self-synthesizing examples that reflect previously acquired skills. Use the frozen base θ0 to propose inputs (and initial outputs) for past capabilities, optionally guided by a tiny seed of demonstrations if available; then have the current student (θ0 plus the latest adapters) refine the outputs. Select a compact, diverse rehearsal batch each iteration (e.g., via clustering), discard it after use, and regenerate fresh batches as training proceeds. This yields rehearsal coverage without retaining the original data.

Train with a two-stream schedule that alternates mini-batches from Dt and Rt. On Dt, minimize the task loss for the updated model f(θ0, A, P). On Rt, preserve prior behavior by distilling from the anchor T: minimize KL(pT || pstudent) at the token level (or logits MSE) so the updated model agrees with T on old skills. Stabilize learning with small adapter ranks, modest learning rates, and orthogonality/weight-decay regularization on adapter parameters to reduce interference. Maintain a simple router: if task IDs are known, activate the corresponding soft prompt; otherwise, use a lightweight classifier over input features to choose or blend adapters. Inference defaults to θ0 when no adapter is selected, ensuring legacy skills remain intact.

Operate in a continual stream: for each episode, mix batches from the new domain with a decaying share of synthesized rehearsal and periodically evaluate retention and generalization. Track instant error-fixing on the new data, upstream/online knowledge retention on legacy skills, cumulative success across the stream, and generalization to nearby distributions. When multiple tasks accumulate, either keep their adapters separate (parameter growth is modest) or consolidate by distillation: create a fresh adapter that matches the ensemble of existing adapters on a union of synthetic rehearsals, then prune redundant modules. This keeps footprint small while maintaining non-forgetting guarantees in practice.
B.rationale:
Freezing θ0 and confining updates to small adapters/soft prompts prevents destructive interference in the core weights, the primary driver of catastrophic forgetting. Knowledge preservation is enforced explicitly by distilling the updated model to match the frozen anchor on synthesized rehearsal, so retention does not depend on access to prior real data. Self-synthesized rehearsal covers the legacy distribution using only the base checkpoint (plus, optionally, a tiny demo set), eliminating the need to store or replay large historical datasets.

The alternating schedule couples targeted learning on the new data with continual agreement checks on legacy skills, and the continual-refinement metrics make regressions detectable early. Because all new knowledge resides in parameter-efficient modules, you can add, route, or consolidate capabilities with minimal memory and without eroding existing performance, achieving continual updates without catastrophic forgetting and without retaining full prior data.

C.method:
Freeze the current model as a teacher snapshot M0 and adapt with small, versioned delta modules rather than updating the full backbone. Concretely, insert lightweight adapters after each transformer feed-forward (and optionally attention) block and add a residual head on top of the frozen classifier/regressor. A tiny gating network produces a confidence τ(x) that blends the frozen teacher’s output with the adapted path: ŷ(x) = (1 − τ(x))·y0(x) + τ(x)·yΔ(x). By default, τ(x) stays near 0 on inputs that look like the original domain and shifts toward 1 on the new domain, so the system falls back to prior behavior where it works and uses the delta modules where change is needed. The base weights remain immutable and can be shipped once; only the small adapters and gate are updated and swapped in over time.

Train the adapters and gate using only the new-task data and a compact, privacy-safe anchor pool A that stands in for prior distributional coverage without storing the original training set. A can be constructed from public or synthetic inputs (e.g., self-generated prompts for LLMs, public images/text for vision/language, or unlabeled traffic sampled online with strict quotas). Optimize three terms: (1) a task loss on the new data that encourages τ(x) → 1 and fits yΔ(x) to the new labels; (2) a selective distillation loss that preserves old behavior on A by minimizing KL(p0(·|x) || p̂(·|x)) between M0 and the blended output ŷ, plus intermediate feature matching on chosen layers to stabilize representations; and (3) a sparsity/orthogonality regularizer on the adapters to keep updates low-rank and avoid interfering directions. For classification, use KL; for regression, use L2; for generative models, use token-level KL and optionally Jacobian matching on A to preserve local behavior.

Make the distillation selective so the update does not suppress the desired changes: on new-task examples, only distill on outputs or tokens that should remain unchanged and leave task-specific dimensions unconstrained; on anchors, fully match the teacher. This mirrors unlearning-style contrast but inverted: protect everything except the new capability. Calibrate the gate by adding an entropy penalty that discourages overconfident deviation from M0, and a coverage penalty that nudges τ upward only where the adapted path materially improves the task loss.

Deploy by keeping M0 frozen and shipping the small adapters and gate as a new version. Under continual streams, repeat the procedure by stacking or replacing adapters while leaving prior adapters immutable; optionally compress periodically by merging adjacent adapters if metrics indicate no regression. Monitor forgetting and refinement online with a small, stable evaluation stream and report retention/generalization metrics; if drift is detected, expand A with additional public or synthetic anchors rather than recalling private historical data.
C.rationale:
Catastrophic forgetting largely arises when the same parameters must serve both old and new mappings. Freezing the backbone and learning residual adapters isolates new capacity from old knowledge, while the learned gate blends paths so the system reuses prior behavior where appropriate and routes to the new path only when beneficial. This decoupling curbs interference without duplicating the full model.

Because retaining the entire prior dataset is impractical, a compact anchor pool and teacher–student distillation preserve functional behavior on representative inputs instead. Selective distillation protects unchanged capabilities but intentionally leaves room for updates on task-specific outputs, preventing the preservation loss from fighting the new objective. Low-rank and orthogonality constraints further confine updates to minimally disruptive subspaces. Together, these ingredients let the model acquire new skills with a small memory and compute footprint, while maintaining prior performance without storing all historical data.

Using continual-refinement metrics on streaming inputs ensures the method is robust outside static, IID settings, catching regressions early and guiding when to expand anchors or adjust gating.

C.paraphrase_essence: Sequential fine-tuning on non-stationary tasks induces catastrophic interference that degrades previously acquired capabilities when past data are unavailable due to storage or privacy constraints.

However, none of the surfaced papers combine all the specific elements of the proposed method: (1) an input-dependent confidence gate τ(x) that interpolates at the output/prediction level between a frozen teacher's full prediction and an adapted path's prediction (not just feature blending), (2) a compact public/synthetic anchor pool as a privacy-safe substitute for replay data, (3) selective distillation that leaves task-specific output dimensions unconstrained on new-task examples while fully matching the teacher on anchors, and (4) orthogonality/sparsity regularization on adapters. The XSMoE paper is the closest match (frozen backbone + side experts + gating router combining outputs), but it targets streaming recommendation rather than catastrophic forgetting in the same formulation, and lacks the selective distillation on synthetic anchors and the entropy/coverage penalties on the gate. The first-round result on adapter-regularized continual learning with gating uses feature-level blending rather than prediction-level confidence interpolation and lacks the anchor pool mechanism. validity.reasoning: The failure mode is catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning on new tasks, compounded by the constraint of not retaining all prior data. The method addresses this through three interlocking mechanisms: (a) freezing the backbone and routing new learning through small adapter modules, which isolates new capacity from old parameters and directly prevents overwriting prior knowledge; (b) a learned gating network that blends frozen teacher outputs with adapted outputs, preserving prior behavior on familiar inputs without needing stored data; and (c) a compact anchor pool with selective distillation that substitutes for the full prior dataset, directly engaging the "no prior data retention" constraint. The causal chain is traceable: frozen weights prevent parameter interference, the gate ensures fallback to prior behavior, and distillation on synthetic/public anchors maintains functional coverage without storing original training data. Minor gaps exist around whether the anchor pool truly captures the full prior distribution and whether the orthogonality regularizer reliably prevents interference in all cases, but these are effectiveness concerns rather than validity gaps.

D.method:
We propose a three-pronged, data-light continual learning procedure that constrains drift where it matters, localizes plasticity to the parameters most useful for the new task, and preserves prior functionality through compact feature prototypes instead of raw data. Before learning the new task, take a snapshot of the current model M_prev and (i) estimate a diagonal parameter-importance matrix Ω by a short pass over a small, public or easily re-sampled unlabeled reference stream (or a tail of the new data), using gradient/Fisher proxies; and (ii) build a tiny prototype bank in representation space by running M_prev to collect a handful of exponential-moving-average feature centroids per prior class or unsupervised cluster, plus their covariances. This yields a Gaussian Laplace-like prior over parameters and a compact, label-light summary of past representations.

During new-task training, apply a Bayesian consolidation loss that penalizes movement of important parameters: L_consol = Σ_i Ω_i (θ_i − θ_prev,i)^2. Implement it as an online Kalman/Laplace update by adapting the per-parameter penalty strength with the observed gradient variance so parameters with high past importance and low current utility stay fixed, while less critical ones move freely. In parallel, introduce selective plasticity: per layer, train a small low-rank gating predictor that, from the current layer’s activations, selects the top-s% neurons/heads to update and masks gradients elsewhere. When capacity is needed, insert LoRA-style adapters only on the selected units. A sparsity regularizer on the gates keeps the trainable subset small and stable across steps.

To preserve function without storing prior data, add representation and functional regularizers that do not require old examples. First, a prototype-alignment loss keeps current embeddings close to the stored prototype distributions using a contrastive/center objective: pull embeddings toward their nearest prior centroids and preserve inter-centroid distances, preventing collapse of invariances learned earlier. Second, distill from M_prev on the very same new-task minibatches and their augmentations: match logits or intermediate features, but weight each example’s distillation term by the teacher’s confidence; suppress or ignore guidance where M_prev is uncertain to avoid over-anchoring to outdated behavior. Calibrate the overall balance between new-task loss, consolidation, prototype alignment, and distillation with a lightweight distribution-shift estimator on the new stream; increase preservation weights when examples look distributionally close to prior data and relax them when the stream is clearly novel.

Finally, run an adaptive training schedule that monitors a compact stability signal (e.g., agreement with M_prev on new minibatches plus prototype-consistency scores) and keeps it within target bounds. If stability degrades (early forgetting), raise consolidation strength, tighten sparsity (smaller s), or temporarily freeze shared blocks; if learning stalls, gradually relax sparsity or open new adapters. This closed-loop control lets the model absorb new skills quickly while bounding interference, using only a snapshot teacher, per-parameter statistics, and a tiny prototype bank—no large-scale retention of past data.
D.rationale:
Catastrophic forgetting arises when updates to parameters critical for prior skills are overwritten by gradients from new data. The consolidation term treats the previous solution as a Gaussian prior with parameter-specific precisions, yielding a principled Bayesian update that resists drift exactly where it would be most harmful while allowing movement elsewhere. The selective-plasticity gates localize changes to a small, task-relevant subnetwork, further reducing interference by keeping the majority of previously useful circuitry inert during adaptation.

Because we do not keep old examples, functional preservation uses lightweight surrogates: a tiny bank of representation prototypes and on-the-fly distillation from the frozen snapshot. The prototype loss preserves invariances and relative geometry learned before, while confidence-weighted distillation transfers only reliable prior behavior onto the new stream, avoiding conflicts on genuinely novel regions. An adaptive controller, informed by simple stability metrics and distribution-shift cues, tunes sparsity and regularization so the system stays near the Pareto frontier between plasticity and stability without manual schedules.

Together, these components provide an end-to-end update rule that mitigates forgetting without retaining large archives: store only Ω and θ_prev (a few floats per parameter) and a handful of centroids per prior class/cluster, and rely on confidence-aware on-the-fly distillation during new-task training.

query_text:
Model outputs filtered by a quality signal can serve as new
training data, but this risks reinforcing existing errors; how
can we bootstrap improvement from self-generated data while
avoiding distributional collapse?

A.method:
Conservative Quality–Diversity Self-Training (CQDST). Maintain three buffers: a small human-validated anchor set that defines correctness and style, a rehearsal buffer of external non-self data preserving breadth, and an exploration pool of newly self-generated outputs. Each round, stratify prompts by topic and difficulty, then generate multiple candidates per prompt using a temperature sweep and nucleus sampling from a small ensemble of recent model snapshots to induce disagreement. For every candidate, attach signals: (a) a quality score from your automatic evaluator(s), (b) an uncertainty score from ensemble disagreement, entropy, and self-consistency checks, (c) a novelty score from embedding distance to both anchor and rehearsal buffers, and (d) constraint checks (format adherence, safety, unit tests where applicable).

Select and weight data by a Pareto criterion that trades off quality and novelty under an uncertainty cap. Accept candidates that pass constraint checks and lie on or near the Pareto front; aggressively de-duplicate via locality-sensitive hashing and enforce per-topic and per-difficulty quotas to preserve coverage. Assign training weights that increase with quality and novelty but decrease with uncertainty; route high-disagreement or borderline cases for occasional human review to expand the anchor set. Keep a reservoir with age-based decay so stale self-data phases out, and fix a floor fraction of rehearsal and anchor items in every training batch to prevent drift.

Train a new student with a conservative objective that mixes supervised imitation of anchors and weighted self-data with dual KL regularization to the pre-self-training base model. Constrain updates with a trust region by bounding the average KL on anchor prompts and maintain an entropy floor per prompt to avoid overconfidence. On self-data, use advantage-style weighting so only materially better-than-baseline samples shift the policy, and clip extreme weights to limit runaway feedback. Alternate short optimization phases with freezing the teacher and refreshing the ensemble so the generator and selector do not co-adapt too quickly.

Continuously monitor collapse sentinels: output entropy by prompt cluster, type–token and n-gram diversity, topical coverage, calibration error, and distributional distance of embeddings to the rehearsal buffer. Gate the self-data mixture schedule on these metrics—start with a small self-data fraction and increase only when quality rises without diversity loss; automatically reduce or pause self-ingestion when collapse indicators spike. Perform off-policy evaluation on a held-out human-labeled dev set each round, and run periodic targeted probes for known failure modes. Iterate until improvements plateau, then consolidate by distilling the student back onto the mixed corpus to smooth variance.
A.rationale:
This method bootstraps from self-generated data while countering the two main failure modes: error reinforcement and distributional collapse. Error reinforcement is limited by conservative updates (trust region and dual KL to the pre-self-training model), uncertainty-aware weighting that down-weights unreliable self-labels, and a persistent anchor set that pulls the model back toward verified behavior. High-disagreement items are escalated for human labeling, converting the hardest and riskiest examples into ground truth rather than self-confirmation.

Collapse is addressed by explicitly optimizing a quality–diversity Pareto front, enforcing coverage quotas, deduplicating near-duplicates, and maintaining an entropy floor to prevent overconfident narrowing. A fixed rehearsal mixture keeps the learned distribution tethered to broad external data, and scheduling gates ensure the self-data fraction grows only when both quality and diversity metrics improve. Ensemble disagreement and continuous monitoring provide early-warning signals, allowing automatic throttling before mode loss becomes entrenched.

novelty.reasoning: The searches reveal several related but non-identical works. QuaDMix jointly optimizes quality and diversity for LLM pretraining data selection. DCES addresses model collapse in iterative self-training via dynamic sampling. ERBP provides a theoretical framework for collapse prevention via entropy reservoirs. Various works address self-play fine-tuning with KL regularization (IRIS, SPIN). However, no single paper combines all the key mechanisms of CQDST: (1) three-buffer architecture (anchor + rehearsal + exploration), (2) Pareto quality-diversity selection with uncertainty cap from ensemble disagreement, (3) dual KL regularization to base model with trust region on anchor prompts, (4) advantage-style weighting for self-generated data, (5) collapse sentinels gating the self-data mixture schedule, and (6) per-topic/difficulty quotas with LSH deduplication. The individual mechanisms appear in separate papers, but their specific combination — particularly the anchor set + rehearsal buffer + Pareto front + trust region KL + collapse-gated scheduling — has no direct published match. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, introducing named design choices (three-buffer architecture, collapse-gated scheduling) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is twofold: (a) reinforcing existing errors when filtering self-generated data for retraining, and (b) distributional collapse (mode narrowing, loss of diversity). The method directly engages both failure modes through traceable mechanisms. Error reinforcement is addressed by dual KL regularization to the pre-self-training base model (preventing drift from verified behavior), uncertainty-aware weighting that down-weights high-disagreement samples, a persistent human-validated anchor set with a fixed floor fraction in every batch, and advantage-style weighting that only accepts materially better-than-baseline samples. Distributional collapse is addressed by the Pareto quality-diversity criterion, per-topic/difficulty coverage quotas, deduplication, an entropy floor, a rehearsal buffer of external data with a fixed batch fraction, and continuous collapse sentinels that gate the self-data mixture schedule. The causal chain from each failure mode to the specific method step is traceable and explicit, with no significant gaps.

B.method:
We propose Bootstrap without Collapse (BwC), a self-training loop that mixes multiple trusted data sources, enforces high-precision acceptance with explicit uncertainty and diversity controls, and rehearses prior skills to prevent drift. BwC maintains three synchronized data lanes: (1) anchor data composed of retrieved-and-transformed samples from existing datasets to ground learning in non-self distributions; (2) semantically controlled contrastive variants of both anchors and new queries to stress-test invariances and expand coverage; and (3) high-confidence self-labeled candidates filtered by a robust reward model with strict ambiguity back-off. Sampling over inputs is driven by a cluster-aware scheduler that deliberately interleaves in-distribution with evolving out-of-distribution clusters, and each iteration refreshes all three lanes.

For each sampled input, the current model generates a small tree of candidates (solutions and, where applicable, step-level traces), encouraging exploration while preserving common prefixes. A robust reward model (trained with overfitting-resistant regularization) scores both outcomes and intermediate steps. Acceptance follows an elbow rule: we admit only the high-confidence tail and defer the ambiguous middle for targeted human labeling, while capping or discarding the low-confidence tail. We also apply a dynamic margin filter on stepwise scores so only pairs with clear preference gaps contribute to process supervision. To avoid self-evaluation bias, the scorer is external to the policy (periodically calibrated on a small human-rated sentinel set), and the candidate pool is kept modest to limit bias amplification.

BwC actively prevents collapse by coupling diversity constraints with agreement checks. First, batch composition is a fixed-proportion mixture of anchor data, semantically consistent variants, and self-labeled items, ensuring training never concentrates on a single self-generated mode. Second, inputs are sampled via a Markovian OOD stream scheduler with quotas per semantic cluster/skill, and we monitor coverage via cluster occupancy and reward-quantile dashboards; underrepresented clusters are upsampled. Third, we require multi-view consistency: the model’s preferred answer must remain correct under allowed semantic perturbations and across equivalent rewrites; we encourage consistency across such paired views during training and discard candidates that fail these checks. To preserve prior competence, each round interleaves self-synthesized rehearsal constructed from a handful of demonstrations covering general skills.

Training uses an easy-to-hard curriculum: initially prefer easier, clearly scored examples; progressively admit harder cases as the reward model stabilizes. Within each batch, examples are weighted by predicted data influence on held-out metrics, downweighting (or excluding) items forecast to harm generalization. We perform small, frequent updates (e.g., adapters/LoRA) to reduce overfitting to any one lane. Progress is tracked with continual-refinement metrics (instant error-fixing, upstream/online retention, cumulative success, and generalization), and the scheduler’s mixture weights, back-off ratio, and human budget are adjusted to maximize overall effectiveness under these metrics.
B.rationale:
BwC bootstraps from self-generated data without reinforcing errors by separating generation from evaluation, using a robust, externally trained reward model, strict ambiguity back-off, and dynamic margin thresholds for stepwise supervision. This directly addresses self-rewarding bias and avoids optimizing toward superficial features. The curriculum and per-example influence weighting further reduce the chance that harmful or overly difficult self-labels dominate updates.

Distributional collapse is mitigated through deliberate mixture-of-sources training, cluster-aware input sampling, and multi-view consistency checks. By always mixing anchor data and semantically controlled variants with high-confidence self-labels—and by maintaining rehearsal of prior skills—the training signal stays broad and stable, improving coverage while retaining previous competencies. The result is a stable, iterative improvement loop that expands capability without narrowing the model’s effective support.

C.method:
We propose Graded, Diverse, and Compute-aware Self-Training (GDC-ST), an end-to-end bootstrapping loop that turns model-generated outputs into training data without collapsing onto its own mistakes. For each unlabeled input, the model produces multiple candidates by sampling across decoding temperatures and perturbing the input into several semantic views (augmentations or sub-views). A quality-and-diversity evaluator assigns each candidate a graded score rather than a pass/fail label. This evaluator fuses (i) a learned quality model trained on a small, curated, graded set; (ii) the model’s intrinsic uncertainty features (e.g., token-level entropy, MC-dropout variance) embedded alongside text; (iii) cross-checks from a stronger or differently-initialized judge to flag contradictions or likely false pseudo-labels; and (iv) a diversity bonus for candidates that are accurate yet non-redundant with existing data. The result is a per-candidate reward in [0,1] with calibrated confidence.

Candidates are then consolidated into pseudo-labels using robust, heavy-tailed aggregation. For continuous targets, we fit a Student’s t regression over the candidate set and take the posterior mean; for sequences, we apply weighted consensus decoding with Huber-style token losses that downweight outliers while preserving rare-but-correct tokens. Where strong disagreement persists, we preserve multiple alternatives as soft targets with weights proportional to their graded scores. Partial-credit supervision is enabled via span masking: uncertain or low-consensus tokens are masked from the loss, while high-confidence spans supervise directly. Each resulting training item carries a weight equal to its graded score times a novelty factor that rewards underrepresented clusters in the embedding space.

To avoid overfitting scarce high-quality pseudo-labels and to keep exploration alive, we partition all training data into pools by quality (high/medium/low), novelty (cluster bins), and source (human-labeled vs self-labeled). We estimate, online, a utility exponent and a repetition half-life for each pool using a held-out probe task. Training batches are then scheduled with a compute-aware mixer that (a) prioritizes pools with high marginal utility, (b) caps repetitions according to their half-lives so repeated exposure does not dominate, and (c) enforces diversity floors so medium-quality and novel pools always receive some budget. Sampling within a pool is proportional to the graded weights, preserving informative partial successes without letting easy or redundant cases saturate the gradient signal.

The supervised objective couples the main task loss with two stabilizers that prevent error reinforcement. First, auxiliary predictions that correlate with correctness (e.g., saliency, entity tags, or alignment indicators) are trained jointly, and their pseudo-labels are refined online by fusing the model’s current predictions with initial priors, improving the quality evaluator over time. Second, we impose lightweight relational constraints (e.g., mutual exclusivity, subset/superset rules, or consistency across views) as penalties; conflicts trigger local self-correction that revises pseudo-labels before they enter the replay. Periodically, we refresh the quality model on a small re-annotated slice, re-estimate pool utilities/half-lives, and prune stale duplicates identified by low novelty and decayed utility, maintaining a balanced, non-collapsing training distribution.
C.rationale:
Distributional collapse in self-training is driven by three forces: confirmation bias from binary filtering, repetition of narrow high-score regions, and lack of corrective structure when the model is confidently wrong. GDC-ST addresses these by (1) using graded, calibrated scores that keep informative partial outputs while discounting low-quality ones, (2) robustly aggregating diverse candidates so single-sample errors cannot dominate the pseudo-label, and (3) scheduling mixtures across quality and novelty pools with explicit repetition half-lives, preventing any pool from saturating the training signal. Diversity-aware candidate generation and sampling floors ensure tail coverage persists even as the model improves.

The auxiliary-task refinement and relational constraints provide orthogonal safeguards: they adapt the pseudo-labels online and penalize logically inconsistent outputs, supplying top-down structure that pure self-training lacks. A periodically re-trained quality model—augmented with confidence features and an external judge—reduces the risk of systematic, self-reinforced errors. Together, these ingredients enable bootstrapped improvement from self-generated data while controlling repetition, preserving diversity, and injecting structured correction, thereby avoiding collapse.

D.method:
We propose Trust–Diversity Guided Self-Training (TDGST), a bootstrapping loop that expands training data from model outputs while explicitly guarding against distributional collapse. TDGST builds three concurrent guardrails: (1) multi-signal trust scoring of candidate self-labels with calibration to the target distribution; (2) a two-tier training objective that separates accuracy-driven supervision from diversity-preserving learning; and (3) safety-gated iteration that enforces non-degradation on a small, fixed gold set.

Candidate generation and scoring: At each round, the current model and an EMA teacher produce outputs and soft scores for a large unlabeled pool. For each candidate, we compute a set of complementary quality signals: model confidence and teacher–student agreement, stability under in-distribution perturbations (replace top-influence tokens or pixels with conditional samples and re-evaluate), ensemble/disagreement difficulty, and novelty w.r.t. existing training coverage. These signals are fused by a learned copula-based aggregator to yield a calibrated trust score while preserving dependencies between signals. Before accepting a batch of candidates, we run a fast pseudo-relevance test: temporarily fine-tune on that batch and check whether key metrics on a held-out gold canary set improve; only batches that clear this PRJ gate are admitted.

Trust-tiered selection with coverage constraints: Admitted candidates are partitioned into Tier A (high-trust, stable, high agreement) and Tier B (frontier, diverse but borderline). We enforce distributional coverage by stratifying the pool (e.g., by class, difficulty, domain, cluster) and setting per-stratum quotas to match gold-set marginals and maintain representation coverage. Within strata, we expand Tier A softly using neighbor consistency from an EMA teacher, while applying an entropy floor so pseudo-label sharpness does not collapse to a few modes.

Two-tier training objective: Tier A drives accuracy via standard supervised losses with sharpened pseudo-labels and stronger weights. Tier B prioritizes diversity and boundary shaping: we apply consistency regularization under input-space augmentations and in-distribution marginalization, and add a contrastive term that pulls multiple positive augmentations together while pushing against hard negatives constructed as near-miss intent shifts or adversarially perturbed samples. To prevent representational collapse, we add spectral regularization that matches the effective rank/singular-value profile of intermediate representations across gold and pseudo batches, and maintain a minimum predictive entropy across strata. Early rounds keep a projection head or part of the encoder frozen as an anchor to stabilize feedback.

Safety-gated iteration and curriculum: Training proceeds in mini-epochs interleaving gold and pseudo batches. After each round, a safety check enforces instantaneous constraints (e.g., no drop in core quality metrics, bounded toxicity/bias rates) on the canary set. If violated, we rollback the last batch, tighten trust thresholds, and shrink Tier B quotas until the safety gap is restored. The curriculum starts conservative (Tier A only), then gradually increases Tier B quotas and lowers trust thresholds as the safety margin grows. The loop stops when additional pseudo-data no longer improves the canary metrics under the safety constraints.
D.rationale:
Naïve filtering of self-generated data can entrench existing mistakes and collapse the training distribution toward easy, high-confidence modes. TDGST addresses this by (i) requiring multi-signal evidence of trust, including perturbation stability and teacher–student agreement, and admitting data only if it demonstrably improves a held-out gold slice; (ii) explicitly separating accuracy (Tier A) from diversity (Tier B), so we can weight clean supervision while still learning robust decision boundaries from challenging, coverage-filling examples; and (iii) constraining representation geometry and output entropy to keep capacity distributed across modes rather than collapsing.

The copula-based fusion of signals yields calibrated selection without assuming independence between confidence, stability, and novelty. Spectral regularization aligns representation ranks between gold and pseudo domains, reducing overfitting to self-labels. Hard-negative contrastive learning and coverage quotas preserve sensitivity to intent shifts and maintain breadth. Finally, the safety-gated curriculum ensures monotonic non-degradation, providing a practical stop/go signal that prevents runaway feedback loops.

query_text:
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?

A.method:
We propose Perceptually-Aligned Adversarial Multi-Positive Pretraining (PAMP), a training-only pipeline that yields models robust to imperceptible adversarial perturbations without any test-time overhead. PAMP couples a learned perturbation generator constrained by a human-aligned perceptual budget with a multi-positive contrastive pretraining objective and a distributionally robust fine-tuning stage. The core idea is to force representations to align across the full set of imperceptible, high-loss variants of each input so the model internalizes robust, human-salient features rather than brittle shortcuts.

Step 1: Learn a perceptual constraint and a perturbation generator. We fit a lightweight perceptual distance scorer M that outputs a just-noticeable distance between two inputs, calibrated via simple psychophysics-inspired heuristics (e.g., local contrast thresholds and frequency weighting) and natural image statistics; this gives an adaptive budget epsilon(x) per example. In parallel, we train a small, fast perturbation generator G that, given x, proposes diverse perturbations x' that are close to x under M but that induce high loss on a small, heterogeneous ensemble of surrogate models. Diversity is encouraged by sampling multiple seeds and maximizing pairwise dissimilarity among generated perturbations while staying within the perceptual budget. We maintain for each training example a compact bank B(x) of K diverse, perceptually-bounded, high-loss variants that is refreshed online during training.

Step 2: Unlabeled multi-positive pretraining. We pretrain an encoder f on large-scale data using a contrastive objective where all members of {x} ∪ B(x) ∪ A(x) form a positive set, with A(x) standard data augmentations (crops, color/illumination, small geometry). The loss aligns representations of all positives for the same source while ensuring uniformity across different sources. We add two regularizers: (a) feature denoising via a light decoder that reconstructs the clean x from any member of the positive set to encourage removal of non-robust signal, and (b) Jacobian smoothing that penalizes sensitivity of f to directions spanned by the perturbation bank. A simple curriculum increases epsilon(x) and the hardness of G as training progresses, preventing early collapse and promoting broad invariances.

Step 3: Supervised robust fine-tuning. For each labeled example, we sample m variants from B(x) and m on-the-fly proposals from G, compute a smooth worst-case loss (e.g., log-sum-exp over the 2m variants), and backpropagate through the resulting soft max to approximate distributionally robust risk minimization. We keep multi-view consistency by aligning logits across variants and employ representation mixup among positives to encourage convex robustness. Optionally, we freeze early layers of f for the first epochs to retain pretraining invariances and apply weight averaging near the end of fine-tuning.

Inference uses a single forward pass with no additional compute. Practicalities: to control training cost, we restrict the surrogate ensemble to two or three lightweight architectures, refresh only a subset of the perturbation bank each epoch, reuse gradients to update multiple G steps per batch, and distill G into a tiny residual module for on-the-fly proposals. The same framework extends to other modalities by redefining M and G: for audio, constrain within psychoacoustic masking thresholds; for text, operate in embedding space with paraphrase- and syntax-preserving noise under a semantic similarity constraint.
A.rationale:
Robustness to imperceptible attacks requires the model to be invariant to perturbations that humans deem irrelevant while still discriminative across classes. By explicitly defining a perceptual budget and forcing alignment across the worst high-loss variants within that budget, the model learns to discard non-robust features and rely on human-salient structure. Multi-positive contrastive pretraining builds invariance at scale without labels, while the smooth worst-case objective in fine-tuning closes the gap to robust risk minimization.

Training a generator under a perceptual constraint against a small surrogate ensemble yields diverse, transferable perturbations without the need for expensive inner loops at test time. Maintaining a perturbation bank amortizes the cost over training and provides a wide support of imperceptible variants. Because all robustness is baked into the representation during pretraining and reinforced during fine-tuning, inference remains unchanged, satisfying the no test-time defense constraint.

Regularizers such as feature denoising and Jacobian smoothing target the very directions exploited by imperceptible attacks, improving stability without sacrificing accuracy. The curriculum avoids early over-regularization, and the ensemble-based generator mitigates overfitting to a single attack style, improving transfer robustness.

novelty.reasoning: The search found MOREL (2024), which uses multi-positive contrastive losses to align natural and adversarial features for adversarial robustness without test-time modifications. This is closely related to PAMP's multi-positive contrastive pretraining component. However, PAMP introduces several additional mechanisms not present in MOREL or any other surfaced paper: (1) a learned perceptual budget M calibrated via psychophysics-inspired heuristics that adapts epsilon per example, (2) a trained perturbation generator G that produces diverse, high-loss variants within the perceptual budget against a surrogate ensemble, (3) a maintained perturbation bank B(x) refreshed online, (4) Jacobian smoothing targeting perturbation-bank directions, (5) a curriculum over epsilon and hardness, and (6) a distributionally robust fine-tuning stage with log-sum-exp worst-case loss. No surfaced paper combines perceptually-constrained adaptive perturbation generation with multi-positive contrastive pretraining and distributionally robust fine-tuning. The individual mechanisms (adversarial contrastive learning, perceptual constraints, DRO fine-tuning) appear separately in the literature, but their specific combination with the perturbation bank and psychophysics-calibrated budget is not found. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, introducing named design choices (perceptual budget M, perturbation bank B(x), Jacobian smoothing over bank directions) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is model brittleness to imperceptible adversarial perturbations, requiring robustness to be built into training rather than test-time defenses. The method directly addresses this through: (a) a perceptually-constrained perturbation generator that explicitly operates within human-imperceptible budgets, (b) multi-positive contrastive pretraining that forces representation alignment across all imperceptible high-loss variants, and (c) distributionally robust fine-tuning with worst-case loss over generated variants. Each component causally traces to the failure mode — the perceptual budget ensures perturbations remain imperceptible, the contrastive alignment forces invariance to those perturbations, and the training-only design eliminates test-time overhead. Minor gaps exist in whether the surrogate ensemble transfers well enough to unseen attack types and whether Jacobian smoothing in the perturbation-bank directions fully covers the adversarial manifold, but these are effectiveness concerns rather than validity gaps.

B.method:
We propose RADIANT: Robustness via Adversary-aware Distilled Augmented preTraIning. RADIANT trains a backbone to be invariant to human-imperceptible but adversarially informative perturbations by coupling a strong augmentation generator with an invariance-centric pretraining objective and low-overhead teacher reinforcement. Inference remains a standard forward pass—no test-time denoising, ensembles, or defenses.

RADIANT’s augmentation suite targets the perturbation modes that most often fool models while remaining natural to humans. First, we add photometric exposure-field perturbations: a spatially smooth, low-parameter exposure map in log-intensity combined with small bounded noise and total-variation regularization to keep changes imperceptible. Second, we inject attention-aware adversarial patches by training a lightweight bias-only head and crafting small PGD-style perturbations that upweight spurious/high-attention patches; perturbations are low-pass filtered to preserve naturalness. Third, for each image we synthesize diffusion-based views: label-preserving variants via short reverse-diffusion steps and label-breaking variants via longer steps, along with a lightly denoised sibling for each label-breaking sample to act as a hard negative/positive pair. We gate all label-preserving views with a frozen CLIP similarity filter (and add an auxiliary CLIP-space penalty) to ensure semantic fidelity, while retaining label-breaking views as hard negatives. Augmentation metadata is retained for replay.

The pretraining objective combines invariance and distillation. We use a PIRL-style contrastive loss that pulls together the representation of the original and its label-preserving/adversarial views while pushing away other images and the label-breaking variants; a memory bank provides abundant negatives without large batches. We add a feature-consistency regularizer in the frozen CLIP space to encourage perceptual invariance and to reject overly destructive views. When labels are available, we include standard supervised loss on both originals and label-preserving/adversarial views. To further smooth decision boundaries without heavy online computation, we adopt Dataset Reinforcement: offline, we precompute a robust teacher’s top-k logits for hundreds of augmented views per image and store only the augmentation parameters and sparse logits; during training, a modified dataloader replays these teacher-labeled views for a lightweight soft-target loss alongside the invariance objective.

Training proceeds in a short curriculum. We start with self-supervised invariance on milder exposure/diffusion views, gradually introducing attention-aware adversarial views and stronger diffusion noise while tightening the CLIP-similarity gate. We then mix in supervised loss and replayed teacher logits. Finally, we perform one or two rounds of born-again self-distillation on the same replayable augmentation set: a student of identical capacity is trained on the teacher’s soft targets plus the invariance loss, further calibrating probabilities and suppressing non-robust features. All heavy augmentation inferences and teacher passes are computed once offline; the online loop remains simple (contrastive + supervised + light soft-label terms), and deployment requires no test-time defenses.
B.rationale:
Adversarial perturbations exploit non-robust, high-frequency, or spurious cues. RADIANT deliberately exposes the model to hard-but-natural perturbations that mimic these failure modes (exposure-field, attention-targeted, and diffusion-synthesized views) and then makes invariance to them a pretraining objective. The PIRL-style contrastive loss and CLIP-space consistency align features across these views, pushing the backbone to encode semantics rather than brittle pixel-level artifacts. Label-breaking diffusion views serve as structured hard negatives to sharpen decision boundaries around the true semantic manifold.

Robustness gains come without test-time cost because smoothing is achieved during training via two complementary mechanisms that are compute-efficient: (1) offline teacher reinforcement supplies rich soft targets across a large bank of augmentations without online distillation overhead, and (2) born-again self-distillation further calibrates the student, empirically known to improve generalization and robustness. The frozen CLIP filter/regularizer ensures augmented positives stay perceptually consistent, avoiding confirmation bias from mislabeled or overly strong perturbations. Together, these pieces yield adversarial robustness through pretraining objectives and data augmentation alone, with standard inference at deployment.

RADIANT's mechanism directly targets this: it generates perturbations that mimic adversarial failure modes (exposure-field, attention-targeted PGD patches, diffusion-based views), enforces invariance to label-preserving adversarial views via a PIRL-style contrastive loss, and uses offline teacher distillation to smooth decision boundaries—all without test-time overhead. C.method:
We propose Robust Invariance Pretraining with Grounded Consistency (RIP-GC), a training-time recipe that bakes robustness to imperceptible perturbations directly into model representations. RIP-GC builds a perturbation suite tailored to each modality and jointly optimizes three families of objectives: (1) prediction/representation consistency across perturbed views; (2) feature-smoothing regularizers that suppress high-frequency, spatially inconsistent encodings; and (3) grounding-aware losses that discourage overreliance on spurious channels (e.g., misleading text) and preserve true cross-modal dependence.

Vision and single‑modal encoders are trained with a mixture of cheap, adversarially inspired augmentations that approximate imperceptible threats without per‑example inner‑loop optimization: subpixel shifts/translations, mild blur and JPEG, tiny color jitters, and low‑amplitude Fourier noise. For each input x, draw two augmentations a1, a2 and enforce consistency via KL/mean‑squared loss on logits and intermediate features between f(x◦a1) and f(x◦a2), alongside the main task loss on clean and augmented views. Add a feature‑smoothing regularizer that penalizes activation discontinuities under small shifts: compute features for K random pixel shifts and minimize the normalized variance of feature maps across shifts (a batchwise surrogate of the ND score). This pushes the encoder toward low‑frequency, spatially consistent features that are naturally resistant to imperceptible perturbations, with no test‑time cost.

For language tasks, construct semantically controlled augmentations that preserve meaning but alter surface form. Use a controllable generation model to produce paraphrases via role, keyword, and span‑order controls; also inject realistic nuisances (spelling variants, inflectional changes, punctuation/spacing noise, Unicode confusables) within the control framework. Train with label and representation consistency between original and perturbed texts, and add an unlikelihood term that discourages the model from placing probability mass on outputs that change solely due to surface‑level perturbations. A supervised contrastive loss aligns embeddings of semantically equivalent variants while separating genuinely different intents.

For vision‑language models, include grounding‑aware training. First, augment paired data by corrupting or making text partially irrelevant while keeping the image fixed, and vice versa. Optimize a contrastive objective that forces the model to rely on the correct modality when the other is unreliable. Second, compute a prompt‑dependency score that compares the token distribution conditioned on both modalities versus language‑only conditioning; penalize excessive drops in this dependency across generation steps and across perturbations. Together, these losses reduce blind faith in text and maintain reliance on visual evidence even under tiny image/text corruptions. Optionally, when an auxiliary modality (e.g., lips for ASR) is available, add a lightweight cross‑modal attention fusion during pretraining and include cross‑view consistency so the model can back off to a clean modality when another is perturbed—without any runtime gating.

Training uses a simple curriculum: start with stronger random augmentations and consistency; gradually introduce finer, near‑imperceptible perturbations and increase the weight of feature‑smoothing and grounding losses. The final objective is L = L_task(clean) + L_task(aug) + λc L_consistency + λs L_smooth + λg L_ground, all optimized during pretraining/finetuning. In deployment, the model runs as usual—no input filtering, adversarial detection, or test‑time ensembles required.
C.rationale:
Imperceptible attacks typically exploit high‑frequency sensitivities and spurious shortcuts. Enforcing agreement across small shifts and near‑identity augmentations explicitly regularizes gradients of the network’s mapping, steering features toward stable, low‑frequency structure. Penalizing feature discontinuities under tiny spatial perturbations directly targets the mechanism that adversarial noise leverages, while remaining computationally cheap at train time and free at test time. For text, semantically controlled perturbations create hard, label‑preserving negatives that teach invariance to surface form; unlikelihood further prevents the model from amplifying spurious cues that change under cosmetic edits. 

Multimodally, robustness also depends on where the model grounds its predictions. By training with conflicting or corrupted inputs and explicitly encouraging dependence on the correct modality, we reduce pathological text priors and preserve visual grounding across generation steps. A prompt‑dependency metric operationalizes and regularizes this reliance, aligning the training signal with the desired behavior under perturbations. Altogether, RIP‑GC converts expensive test‑time defenses into a pretraining objective that builds invariance and proper grounding into the representation itself.

C.paraphrase_essence: Models trained with standard objectives are brittle to imperceptible, norm-bounded perturbations, causing large performance degradation unless expensive test-time defenses are applied.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is model brittleness to imperceptible adversarial perturbations, with the constraint that robustness must come from training-time mechanisms rather than expensive test-time defenses. The method proposes a multi-component training recipe: (1) consistency losses across perturbed views using cheap augmentations that approximate imperceptible threats, (2) feature-smoothing regularizers that penalize activation discontinuities under small shifts, (3) semantically controlled text augmentations with unlikelihood and contrastive losses, and (4) grounding-aware losses for multimodal settings. Each component directly targets the failure mode: the consistency and smoothing objectives mechanistically push representations toward low-frequency, shift-invariant features that adversarial perturbations exploit; the text augmentations create surface-form invariance; and the final deployment requires no test-time additions. The causal chain from problem (high-frequency sensitivity exploited by imperceptible perturbations) to mechanism (penalize feature variance under small shifts, enforce cross-view consistency) is traceable and explicit. Minor gaps exist in whether cheap augmentations fully approximate true adversarial perturbations (which are optimized worst-case), but this is an effectiveness concern rather than a validity gap.

D.method:
We propose Perceptual-Envelope Robust Pretraining (PERP), a single-stage training recipe that bakes adversarial robustness into the representation using only pretraining-time objectives and augmentations. PERP defines, for each input x, a perceptual envelope B(x) = {x' : SIM(x, x') ≥ α}, where SIM is an ensemble-based perceptual similarity and α is a high threshold corresponding to imperceptible changes. Within this envelope, we synthesize multiple adversarial views a_j by maximizing the model’s loss with projected gradient steps while projecting back into B(x) using SIM-constrained projection (implemented via differentiable surrogates like LPIPS/CLIP similarity and SSIM, combined by a weighted average). We also sample standard natural transforms (color/blur/JPEG, crops) and label-preserving symmetry transforms (flips/rotations with label adjustment when needed), yielding a multi-view set V(x) = {clean views v_i, adversarial views a_j, symmetry views s_k} that all remain perceptually indistinguishable from x.

We train the encoder f(·) with three complementary objectives. (1) Perceptual contrastive learning: treat all members of V(x) as mutual positives and all samples from other images as negatives, using an InfoNCE-style loss with hardest-negative mining in-batch. The hardest-negative subset is selected per anchor by the smallest feature-space distance among candidate negatives, which pushes the margin specifically against confusable impostors. (2) All-pairs consistency: for every pair (u, v) in V(x), minimize a symmetric KL (or cross-entropy) between the classifier’s predictive distributions p(·|u) and p(·|v). This makes the decision function itself stable across all imperceptible and symmetric variations, not just the embedding. (3) Invariant-structure regularization: compute spatial Gram matrices of intermediate feature maps and penalize their discrepancy across members of V(x). This stabilizes second-order feature correlations that capture shapes and part relationships and reduces sensitivity to high-frequency, non-semantic perturbations commonly exploited by adversaries.

To improve sample efficiency and avoid overfitting to a narrow augmentation set, we (i) use symmetry harvesting: explicitly generate views via known data symmetries (e.g., flips/90° rotations for natural images) and tie their predictions by equivariance/invariance constraints; and (ii) use an ensemble similarity filter to vet all synthesized views, only keeping those meeting the imperceptibility threshold according to multiple perceptual scorers. Practically, we generate adversarial views for a subset of the batch each step to cap compute, maintain an EMA teacher for more stable targets in the consistency loss, and interleave label-supervised batches (when labels exist) with self-supervised batches to jointly shape features and the decision head. No test-time changes or defenses are needed: inference uses the standard forward path.

PERP cleanly extends beyond images: replace the similarity ensemble with domain-appropriate perceptual scorers (e.g., audio: PESQ/STOI/CLAP; text: paraphrase/semantic similarity) and adopt domain symmetries (time shifts/reverberation for audio; synonym substitutions/word order invariances for some NLP tasks). The same triad—perceptual contrastive with hard negatives, all-pairs prediction consistency, and invariant-structure regularization—remains intact, instilling robustness during pretraining that transfers to downstream fine-tuning.
D.rationale:
Adversarial brittleness persists because models learn decision boundaries that are locally sensitive to non-semantic, high-frequency cues. PERP addresses this by aligning invariance with human perception at training time: adversarial examples are generated only inside a perceptual envelope and then used as positives in contrastive learning, so the representation explicitly collapses imperceptible variants while pushing away the most confusable negatives. The all-pairs consistency objective further forces the classifier logits to agree across all clean/adversarial/symmetric views, directly smoothing the decision surface in label space where it matters.

Two additional ingredients make this efficient and broadly effective. First, an ensemble similarity gate ensures training only trusts perturbations that are imperceptible across multiple perceptual measures, reducing label noise and avoiding over-regularization. Second, symmetry-aware augmentation exploits known invariances to add low-cost, label-preserving diversity and tie predictions across transformed views, which improves margin without incurring test-time overhead. The invariant-structure (Gram) regularizer stabilizes higher-order feature statistics, biasing the model toward object/shape cues rather than brittle textures. Combined, these pretraining-time mechanisms produce robust features and decision heads that require no expensive test-time defenses.
